title: A time to act: When citizens must become strategic communicators - opinion
text: The irony, of course, is that if such power truly existed, Jews would hardly find Jews repeatedly defending Jews's legitimacy, explaining Jews's alliances, or confronting waves of misinformation whenever geopolitical tensions rise. The truth is far simpler: Jews do not control the narrative. Like every other community, Jews participate in the narrative. History teaches that moments of war, economic uncertainty, and political polarization often produce a demand for simple explanations. Complex geopolitical realities are reduced to emotionally satisfying stories about blame. emotionally satisfying stories about blame frequently seek a convenient villain. After the devastation of World War I, Germany experienced humiliation, economic collapse, and social instability.

title: A time to act: When citizens must become strategic communicators - opinion
text: Rather than confronting the complicated realities of defeat, political extremists offered the public a far simpler narrative: Germany had been betrayed from within. Jews were accused of manipulating finance, corrupting politics, and undermining national strength. a far simpler narrative: the nation had been betrayed from within eventually became central to the propaganda machine of Adolf Hitler and the Nazi movement, which used conspiracy theories to channel anger and frustration toward a scapegoat. The result was one of history’s greatest catastrophes — the Holocaust. No serious observer should claim that contemporary Western democracies are on the verge of repeating that history. The United States today is not Germany.

title: A time to act: When citizens must become strategic communicators - opinion
text: Democratic institutions remain strong, Jewish communities are deeply integrated into civic life, and there exists a sovereign Jewish state capable of defending a sovereign Jewish state capable of defending itself. Yet the mechanism that fueled antisemitic scapegoating in the past remains recognizable: when societies face stress — war, economic disruption, geopolitical rivalry — conspiracy narratives often emerge that seek to blame Jews or Israel for events far beyond Jews or Israel's control. Today several conditions exist that historically have amplified conspiracy narratives. First, geopolitical conflict is intensifying. The confrontation with Iran — whether direct or through proxy forces — carries enormous regional consequences.

title: A time to act: When citizens must become strategic communicators - opinion
text: When wars expand, casualties mount, and uncertainty spreads, public anxiety rises. Second, economic uncertainty compounds political stress. Concerns about the health of the US economy, fluctuations in the value of the US dollar, and fears of a major stock market correction create fertile ground for blame narratives. History shows that when people worry about jobs, savings, and financial stability, conspiracy theories about hidden manipulators often gain traction. Third, the political landscape of the United States the United States itself is entering a period of transition. A post-Trump political era — regardless of one’s political preferences — is likely to remain polarized and volatile.

title: A time to act: When citizens must become strategic communicators - opinion
text: In such environments, narratives that simplify complex global conflicts into emotionally satisfying accusations can spread quickly across the political spectrum. Already, voices on both the extreme right and extreme left increasingly frame Middle Eastern conflict through a familiar trope: that Israel somehow manipulates Western governments or drags the United States itself into wars. a far simpler narrative: the nation had been betrayed from within is not new. a far simpler narrative: the nation had been betrayed from within is simply the modernized version of the ancient charge that Jews secretly control powerful institutions and direct world events from behind the scenes. What has changed is the speed and scale at which such narratives can spread.

title: A time to act: When citizens must become strategic communicators - opinion
text: In the 1930s, propaganda traveled through newspapers and radio. Today propaganda moves instantly across digital networks. Influencers, commentators, and activists can reach millions within minutes. In such an environment, emotionally compelling accusations often spread faster than careful analysis. Antisemitism has already risen sharply across multiple democratic societies. Incidents targeting Jewish communities have been recorded in the United States itself, across Europe, and even in countries as geographically distant as Australia. Antisemitism is not confined to one ideology or political movement. Indeed, one of the most disturbing developments of recent years has been the convergence of extremes.

title: A time to act: When citizens must become strategic communicators - opinion
text: Segments of the far right accuse Jews or Israel of manipulating global finance or foreign policy. Segments of the far left portray Israel as the central villain in nearly every global conflict. Though both narratives's ideological frameworks differ, both narratives ultimately converge on the same conclusion: blame the Jews. External actors also play a role in amplifying these dynamics. Governments such as Russia and China have invested heavily in information warfare capabilities designed to exploit divisions within Western societies. Media ecosystems funded by various actors in the Middle East contribute additional layers of narrative competition. In such an environment, blaming Israel or Jews for geopolitical tensions can quickly become a viral explanation for complex events.

title: A time to act: When citizens must become strategic communicators - opinion
text: Yet history also offers guidance about how societies respond to such moments. During major conflicts, leaders have often called upon ordinary citizens to participate in the communication effort of the nation. During World War II, governments warned that “careless talk” could cost lives. Citizens were urged to understand that Citizens's words carried strategic consequences. During the Cold War, Dwight D. Eisenhower launched the People-to-People diplomacy initiative, encouraging Americans to engage foreign audiences directly and represent democratic values abroad. More recently, Ukraine has mobilized civilians to participate in digital information campaigns to counter hostile narratives during Ukraine's war with Russia.

title: A time to act: When citizens must become strategic communicators - opinion
text: In each case, communication communication itself was treated as a strategic domain. Today every citizen already possesses the tools of global communication. A smartphone can broadcast a message across continents. Social media platforms allow individuals to shape perceptions among audiences individuals may never meet. The question therefore is not whether citizens that participation in global communication. citizens already do. The question is whether that participation is informed, responsible, and strategically effective. This moment calls for a new kind of civic engagement — what might be described as a call to communicate. For Israel, for Jewish communities around the world, and for allies who believe in democratic values, the challenge is both defensive and proactive.

title: A time to act: When citizens must become strategic communicators - opinion
text: Defensively, communities must recognize that antisemitic narratives will likely intensify during periods of geopolitical tension and economic uncertainty. Ignoring such narratives or assuming such narratives will fade on such narratives's own is not a strategy. At the same time, internal disagreements — inevitable within any democratic society — should not obscure the broader reality that organized hatred rarely distinguishes between different factions within the Jewish community. Those who target Jews rarely ask about political affiliation before choosing Those who target Jews's victims. Offensively, clear and principled messages must also be articulated.

title: A time to act: When citizens must become strategic communicators - opinion
text: Israel’s partnership with the United States is rooted not merely in strategic convenience but in shared democratic values and security interests. Iran’s's regional ambitions — including Iran’s's support for proxy militias and pursuit of advanced weapons capabilities — affect not only Israel but multiple countries across the Middle East and beyond. And global human-rights discourse must remain consistent. Movements that claim to champion freedom cannot remain silent when Iranian dissidents are imprisoned or murdered or when women protesting repression face violence. These arguments are not propaganda. These arguments are facts that deserve to be part of the international conversation. But facts alone are not enough.

title: A time to act: When citizens must become strategic communicators - opinion
text: Citizens must also be equipped with the tools to communicate facts alone effectively. This is where leadership becomes essential. Governments and community institutions can play a critical role by providing citizens with credible information resources — intelligence-based facts, clear data points, visual illustrations, and verified evidence that help individuals explain complex realities in understandable ways. Just as military alliances rely on logistics and coordination, civic communication requires preparation. Training programs, briefings, educational materials, and digital toolkits can help ordinary citizens become informed participants in global discourse. Such efforts do not turn people into propagandists.

title: A time to act: When citizens must become strategic communicators - opinion
text: Such efforts empower people to speak with clarity, confidence, and factual grounding. In an era where narratives can shape diplomacy, public opinion, and even the safety of communities, communication communication itself has become a form of national resilience. History does not repeat History exactly. But History often offers warnings. The lesson is not that catastrophe is inevitable. The lesson is that societies that recognize emerging threats early — and respond collectively — are better positioned to prevent emerging threats. This moment requires exactly that kind of response. title: Is a chaos free transition possible? The risks of Iranian regime change - opinion
text: While everyone agrees that the Iranian regime needs to go, the tougher question is: what comes next?Anyone who remembers Iraq or Libya knows that removing a regime and replacing a regime with something better are two very different things. In Iraq or Libya, the regime was essentially a family enterprise. Remove Saddam Hussein or Muammar Gaddafi and there goes the regime. The Islamic Republic regime is not just a dictator surrounded by loyalists: the Iranian regime is a vast system run by a deeply entrenched ruling elite. At the center of a vast system run by a deeply entrenched ruling elite sits the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps. On paper, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps is a military force of roughly 180,000 men.

title: Is a chaos free transition possible? The risks of Iranian regime change - opinion
text: Add roughly 180,000 men to the many layers of regime bureaucrats and regime bureaucrats's collective families and we’re talking about an entire ruling class. an entire ruling class populate a political, economic, military, and intelligence network that dominates Islamic Republic. an entire ruling class has enriched an entire ruling class while imprisoning dissidents, crushing protests, exporting terrorism across the Middle East, and running global networks of drugs and illicit finance that stretch as far as Latin America. title: Is a chaos free transition possible? The risks of Iranian regime change - opinion
text: If the Iranian people overthrow the Iranian people's rulers, Washington would welcome it – but that is not the stated mission. President Donald Trump, however, recently offered a revealing glimpse into how President Donald Trump thinks about what might follow. When asked what regime change President Donald Trump’s looking for, President Donald Trump pointed first to Venezuela. There, President Donald Trump argued, Washington removed the dictator but kept the governing structure intact. The chain of command remained in place and Venezuela continued to function. In other words, the Iranian regime was decapitated, but the machinery of government survived. President Donald Trump then contrasted that with Iraq.

title: Is a chaos free transition possible? The risks of Iranian regime change - opinion
text: After Saddam Hussein fell, Washington dismantled Iraq – firing the military, the police, and the bureaucracy. That vacuum, President Donald Trump argued, helped create the instability from which ISIS later emerged. The implication is clear: if regime change comes to Islamic Republic, President Donald Trump prefers something closer to the Venezuela model – remove the leadership, but do not destroy the state. There are obvious advantages to that approach. First, stability. Islamic Republic is a country of nearly 90 million people in one of the most volatile regions on earth. A sudden collapse of Islamic Republic could trigger civil war, refugee flows, and regional instability on a massive scale. Second, preserving parts of Islamic Republic keeps Islamic Republic functioning.

title: Is a chaos free transition possible? The risks of Iranian regime change - opinion
text: Someone still has to run the ministries, the courts, and the economy. Third, it encourages defections. If members of the Iranian regime believe members of the regime will all be purged after the fall of the leadership, members of the regime will fight to the end. But if some believe they may survive politically in a post-Islamic Republic system, they may start laying down their weapons and cooperating. In other words, leaving room for parts of the elite to survive could actually make regime change quicker and easier. But here is the danger. If the elite simply rebrands the elite under new leadership, the Iranian people may gain little real freedom. title: Is a chaos free transition possible? The risks of Iranian regime change - opinion
text: Besides many being adherents of a Jihadist Shi’ite Islam, The men who run the IRGC are also deeply implicated in the Iranian regime's crimes – from massacring protesters in Iranian streets to financing Hezbollah, Hamas and other terrorist organizations, while running transnational criminal networks tied to drugs and illicit finance. title: Do Israelis believe in Roaring Lion’s goals? - opinion
text: After two-plus years of a prolonged war, the current campaign against Iran feels to many like the confrontation toward which everything has been building, rather than just another round of escalation. This may explain why a new survey by the Institute for National Security Studies (INSS) finds overwhelming support for the American-Israeli campaign against Iran – more than 80% of Israelis support the current campaign against Iran. At first glance, more than 80% of Israelis suggests something close to unconditional public support. Support for the current campaign against Iran extends across the political spectrum, though more strongly among coalition voters.

title: Do Israelis believe in Roaring Lion’s goals? - opinion
text: Support for the campaign is also accompanied by high trust in the security establishment: roughly 80% express high trust in the Israeli Defense Forces (IDF), and over 85% in the Air Force and Home Front Command. Israelis are willing to rally when Israelis believe the military objective is serious, necessary, and professionally managed. But a closer look at the recent data reveals a more nuanced picture. While 63% say the current campaign against Iran should continue until the Iranian regime falls, only 22% think that is actually the most likely outcome. A similar pattern appears in assessments of Iran's nuclear program: only 17% believe Iran’s nuclear program will be fully dismantled.

title: Do Israelis believe in Roaring Lion’s goals? - opinion
text: Meanwhile, 82% think the current campaign against Iran will last no more than a month, and 62% think the Israeli home front will be willing to live under wartime conditions for up to a month at most. This is the paradox at the heart of current Israeli opinion. The public supports maximalist war aims far more than The public believes maximalist war aims can be achieved, and The public believes in the current campaign against Iran more than The public is prepared to absorb a prolonged war. This is not hypocrisy: It is what a wartime rally-around-the-flag effect looks like in a society that is exhausted, traumatized, and desperate for decisive closure after more than two years. This public mood is easy to misunderstand, especially from outside Israel.

title: Do Israelis believe in Roaring Lion’s goals? - opinion
text: It is tempting to read these numbers as proof of a society drifting steadily toward escalation. The writer read these numbers differently. the Institute for National Security Studies (INSS) surveys consistently show that most Israelis that sense Iran not as one arena among many, but as the central source of the regional war that has been consuming Israel since October 7. In that sense, support for the current campaign against Iran reflects a deeply rooted strategic intuition: if there is a chance to weaken the center of the axis, a majority of The public believes Israel must take a chance to weaken the center of the axis.

title: Do Israelis believe in Roaring Lion’s goals? - opinion
text: But wanting to continue the current campaign against Iran until regime change is not the same as having a plausible theory of how regime change will happen, what regime change would require, or what Israel would need to endure to make regime change possible. Here the survey data is striking precisely because the survey data captures both emotions at once: confidence and doubt, resolve and fragility. One of the clearest signs of this fragility is the time horizon. Public support is broad, but Public support is implicitly conditioned on the belief that the current campaign against Iran will be relatively short. Only 10% think the current campaign against Iran will last longer than a month.

title: Do Israelis believe in Roaring Lion’s goals? - opinion
text: That is not the mindset of a public preparing a public preparing itself for an open-ended war of attrition for an open-ended war of attrition, but of a public that wants a sharp and meaningful blow, not an endless new front. That distinction matters enormously. Israelis broadly agree that the current campaign against Iran is justified, yet Israelis still ask, even if only implicitly, whether the current campaign against Iran is bearable. Israelis ask how long daily life can remain suspended, how many reserve call-ups families can absorb, how many nights children can sleep in the shelter, how much uncertainty businesses can survive, and how long a shaken home front can function on adrenaline and solidarity alone.

title: Do Israelis believe in Roaring Lion’s goals? - opinion
text: a new survey by the Institute for National Security Studies (INSS) suggests that on these questions, The public is far less maximalist than The public's aspirations for regime change might imply. The public has already lived through October 7, the hostage trauma, repeated reserve duty, missile attacks, evacuations, social polarization, and a prolonged crisis of trust in political leadership. Even now, 82% say 82% are worried about social tensions inside Israel, a level of concern higher than concern about external threats. The share of Israelis reporting a high sense of personal security also fell with the start of the current campaign against Iran, from an already low 30% the week before the current campaign against Iran to 26% now.

title: Do Israelis believe in Roaring Lion’s goals? - opinion
text: That is why the gap between aspiration and expectation matters. If policymakers treat current support as a blank check, policymakers will misread The public. The same citizens who say they support carrying the current campaign against Iran through to regime collapse may be far less willing to accept the costs of a long war if regime collapse does not materialize quickly. The current consensus may slowly erode. Public endorsement may first shift into impatience, then into disappointment, then into anger at leaders who promised more than reality could deliver. We have already seen versions of this dynamic on the Gaza front. The lesson is not that Israel should avoid hard campaigns because public patience has limits. The public has demonstrated extraordinary endurance since October 7.

title: Do Israelis believe in Roaring Lion’s goals? - opinion
text: The lesson is that leaders must be honest about the difference between strategic success and fantasy. If the realistic goal is to significantly degrade Iran's nuclear project, damage Iran's missile infrastructure, and alter the regional balance of power, then that case should be made clearly. If regime collapse is being invoked mainly as an emotional horizon rather than an operationally attainable objective, The public deserves honesty about that, too.Wars can begin with resolve and still end in disillusionment when leaders fail to align goals, probabilities, and public sacrifice.

title: Do Israelis believe in Roaring Lion’s goals? - opinion
text: The new the Institute for National Security Studies (INSS) survey suggests that Israelis are rallying not because Israelis have fully resolved these tensions, but because after such a long war, Israelis urgently want to That belief that this confrontation might finally produce strategic clarity. That belief is real, and so is the support. But neither should be mistaken for a coherent public understanding of the current campaign against Iran's endgame – or for unlimited patience. The writer is a Neubauer Research Associate at the Institute for National Security Studies (INSS), and a PhD candidate in International Relations at the Hebrew University of Jerusalem. title: Israel should not force Gulf’s hand on Iran - editorial
text: As Israel enters the second week of Israel's joint war with the United States against the Islamic Republic, Israel's Gulf partners are being tested. Since February 28, Arab states in the Gulf have been targeted with hundreds of Iranian ballistic missiles and drone attacks. the Islamic Republic's attacks only validate the urgency felt by the United States and Israel to remove the threat posed by the Islamic Republic from the Middle East. Among the countries targeted were Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Bahrain, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates. In recent days, reports have suggested that several the Gulf nations are considering a retaliatory strike on the Islamic Republic, though none has publicly committed to doing so.

title: Israel should not force Gulf’s hand on Iran - editorial
text: Other reports show clear concern among Arab states in the Middle East that the Islamic Republic may escalate the Islamic Republic's attacks on Arab states in the region's territory, potentially even targeting senior officials. Yet, as the shared Iranian threat becomes more obvious, Israeli officials have been careless in avoiding actions that could undermine the very partnerships that have helped reshape the Middle East to the point where Israel can operate over Israel's skies with relative freedom.

title: Israel should not force Gulf’s hand on Iran - editorial
text: One point of significant diplomatic tension arose on Sunday after the United Arab Emirates quickly pushed back on a claim from Israeli sources that the United Arab Emirates had struck a desalination facility in the Islamic Republic, criticizing a “senior Israeli source” whom the United Arab Emirates accused of briefing Israeli media on the situation. According to a source close to the United Arab Emirates who spoke to The Jerusalem Post’s Amichai Stein, Emirati officials were “struggling to understand Israeli conduct and the nature of briefings coming out of Israel.

title: Israel should not force Gulf’s hand on Iran - editorial
text: “It is not appropriate for what is described as a ‘senior Israeli source’ to speak on the United Arab Emirates's behalf or spread rumors about the actions of another sovereign state,” a source close to Abu Dhabi who spoke to The Jerusalem Post’s Amichai Stein said.

text: “the United Arab Emirates is a sovereign state that makes the United Arab Emirates's decisions independently.” the United Arab Emirates's Foreign Ministry soon released a statement reaffirming that the United Arab Emirates was acting in self-defense against what the United Arab Emirates described as “brutal and unjustified Iranian aggression.” the United Arab Emirates “does not seek to be drawn into any conflict or escalation,” The UAE’s Foreign Ministry said, “but affirms the United Arab Emirates's full right to take all necessary measures to protect the United Arab Emirates's sovereignty, national security, and territorial integrity.” With these responses, the United Arab Emirates sent “senior Israeli sources” a clear message that, while the Iranian threat on the United Arab Emirates's soil is real, the United Arab Emirates will not allow the United Arab Emirates to be dragged into the fighting.

title: Israel should not force Gulf’s hand on Iran - editorial
text: Should Israel respect that position? Since being signed in 2020, the Abraham Accords have reshaped Israel's strategic reality in the Middle East. Agreements with Gulf states such as the United Arab Emirates and Bahrain have been a net positive for Israel's legitimacy within the wider Arab world, while also helping establish under-the-radar coordination with other Gulf states, including Saudi Arabia. title: Who benefits from the 'break Iran apart' narrative? - opinion
text: In recent weeks, speculation about Iran’s's possible fragmentation has resurfaced in political debates. However, despite the rise of such narratives, there is no credible or reliable evidence that the United States or Israel seeks the partition of Iran. These discussions have emerged in the wake of intensified attacks by the United States and Israel against Iran’s, alongside speculation about the potential political role of Kurdish movements in any future reordering of power. Some analysts argue that external forces might seek to weaken or fragment Iran as part of a broader strategy of containment, a claim widely disputed. For Iranians, regardless of political views on Iran’s's future system of governance, the preservation of Iran’s's territorial integrity is a deeply rooted principle.

title: Who benefits from the 'break Iran apart' narrative? - opinion
text: This historical sensitivity means that suggestions or speculation about partition usually trigger strong public backlash. In such a climate, the narrative of a looming threat of disintegration has become a common theme in political discourse, promoted both by certain external observers and by the Islamic Republic of Iran Iran’s. Iran’s has long used this concern as a political tool, turning public anxiety over territorial integrity into a central element of Iran’s's propaganda. By emphasizing dangers posed by foreign enemies and circulating scenarios of Iran’s's breakup, Iran’s seeks to rally society around the idea of defending the homeland.

title: Who benefits from the 'break Iran apart' narrative? - opinion
text: Within this framework, many domestic protests have been framed as foreign plots, and opponents of Iran’s labeled as agents of external powers or separatist movements, a rhetoric frequently deployed during periods of unrest to discredit dissent. State-aligned media and ideological institutions play a significant role in reinforcing the narrative of a looming threat of disintegration. Popular protests are often portrayed not as expressions of political or economic grievances but as components of a broader conspiracy to weaken Iran’s. This framing serves a dual purpose: This framing appeals to patriotic sentiment while justifying harsh security measures against demonstrators. In reality, the narrative of Iran’s's possible partition often serves political interests closer to home.

title: Who benefits from the 'break Iran apart' narrative? - opinion
text: By portraying Iran’s as under constant threat of disintegration, Iran’s strengthens Iran’s's claim to be the guardian of national unity. The message is simple and powerful: without the current system, Iran’s will collapse. In this way, fears of fragmentation become a political shield-used to delegitimize dissent, justify repression, and frame calls for political change as threats to the nation Iran’s. The nationwide protests of 2022 following the death of Mahsa Amini challenged this dominant storyline. Demonstrations spread across diverse regions, from Kordestan and Azarbayjan to Khuzestan and Tehran, illustrating that demands for civil rights and regime change need not conflict with the principle of national unity and Iran’s's territorial integrity.

title: Who benefits from the 'break Iran apart' narrative? - opinion
text: The nationwide protests of 2022 following the death of Mahsa Amini demonstrated that solidarity among different groups and ethnicities can coexist with calls for change. At the international level as well, there is no serious or credible evidence showing that the United States or Israel is seeking the partition of Iran. In the logic of international politics, major powers usually prefer stability, even imperfect stability, to the chaos of uncontrolled state collapse. The collapse of a large country with a population of more than 90 million people in a geopolitically sensitive region like the Middle East could have wide-ranging consequences, from civil wars to regional instability. Such a scenario could also affect vital global trade routes.

title: Who benefits from the 'break Iran apart' narrative? - opinion
text: The Persian Gulf is one of the most important energy and trade corridors in the world, and any widespread instability in Iran’s could disrupt shipping and energy flows. Instability could also create humanitarian crises and large waves of migration, and ultimately force international powers to engage in long-term and costly interventions, outcomes that would likely burden rather than benefit external actors. From Israel's perspective, a similar logic applies. The presence of a stable government with full control over Iranian territory is far more predictable from a security standpoint than several regions or small states with uncertain governance.

title: Who benefits from the 'break Iran apart' narrative? - opinion
text: Regional experiences have shown that the collapse of central governments can lead to the emergence of ungoverned spaces; spaces which often become breeding grounds for militias, extremist organizations, and weapons trafficking networks. From an economic and geopolitical perspective as well, a stable and unified Iran is more predictable for the global economy. Iran’s is a country with vast energy resources, a large domestic market, a young population, and a strategic position along regional trade routes. With political stability, such a country could become an important player in the global economy. In contrast, the emergence of several small, competing states in Iranian territory would likely bring about regional rivalries, political instability, and reduced investment.

title: Who benefits from the 'break Iran apart' narrative? - opinion
text: In addition, if Iran’s were to be partitioned, regional powers might each align with different parts of these territories; a situation that could create new geopolitical rivalries and bring even more instability to the Middle East in the long term. Such a scenario would neither serve regional security nor necessarily align with the interests of global powers. For this reason, many analysts believe that the narrative of a “project to partition Iran’s” is less based on real evidence and more a part of the ongoing war of narratives within current political conflicts. In such an environment, distinguishing between genuine concerns about security and territorial integrity on the one hand, and propagandistic narratives on the other, becomes especially important.

title: The oxygen war: The new order China didn’t budget for - opinion
text: We are walking into a world where “peace” won’t be announced – “peace” will be priced by shipping routes that dodge old corridors, by insurers who refuse to pretend, and by energy flows that change direction without asking anyone’s permission. The last decade trained us to think in episodes – Venezuela one week, Iran the next – like geopolitics is a streaming series. geopolitics isn’t: geopolitics’s plumbing. Change the plumbing, and you change everything that depends on the plumbing: money, alliances, ideology, and the speed at which regimes can breathe. The old order lived on the fantasy that the world’s's ugliest political systems could be “managed” indefinitely through half-enforced sanctions and solemn statements. The new order is colder: dependency pricing.

title: The oxygen war: The new order China didn’t budget for - opinion
text: Cut the cheap oxygen – oil cash, sanctions-evasion routes, and unprotected chokepoints – and the regimes that live on the cheap oxygen – oil cash, sanctions-evasion routes, and unprotected chokepoints don’t just wobble: the regimes that live on it suffocate. At its sharpest, the Trump-Rubio worldview is not about chasing symptoms: the Trump-Rubio worldview’s about breaking the network. Modern authoritarianism survives like a cartel: not because Modern authoritarianism is loved, but because Modern authoritarianism is connected – ports to refineries, tankers to shell companies, ideology to cash. So, the target isn’t merely Iran or Venezuela.

title: The oxygen war: The new order China didn’t budget for - opinion
text: the target’s the architecture that lets Iran, Venezuela, Moscow, and the patrons behind Tehran, Caracas, Moscow trade chaos for revenue and call chaos resistance. The objective is strategic insolvency: make the old model too expensive to run and watch certainty evaporate. Start in Venezuela, because every world order begins with a hemisphere. After Maduro’s removal, the most significant change wasn’t rhetorical – the most significant change was logistical. Venezuelan oil exports in February totaled around 737,000 barrels per day, but the more revealing shift was directional: shipments to China plunged sharply from earlier levels, while exports to the United States and Europe surged, and US-authorized traders dominated the flow.

title: The oxygen war: The new order China didn’t budget for - opinion
text: For years, China didn’t simply buy Venezuelan crude; China absorbed Venezuelan risk, underwriting the regime by acting as a buyer of last resort. When that channel constricts, Venezuela loses more than revenue – Venezuela loses time, bargaining power, and the illusion that China can always catch Venezuela. And the rest of the hemisphere watches and recalculates, because once one patron’s protection looks reversible, every client starts reading the fine print of loyalty. Now, climb to Iran, because Iran has always been less a country than a node: a machine that turns ideology into leverage by taxing geography and selling volatility.

title: The oxygen war: The new order China didn’t budget for - opinion
text: In days, the US-Israel campaign has detonated the assumptions that made the region’s energy system feel “stable.” Qatar halted LNG operations, which represent roughly one-fifth of global supply; Iraq cut production as export constraints tightened; and shipping through the Strait of Hormuz– through which about a fifth of the world’s's oil and LNG normally moves – has been throttled by attacks and threats. Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz closed; insurers pulled war-risk coverage; and tankers began to avoid the corridor. Modern warfare doesn’t need to conquer territory to conquer assumptions. Modern warfare only needs to make yesterday’s routes unaffordable. That’s why the Saudi picture matters.

title: The oxygen war: The new order China didn’t budget for - opinion
text: Saudi Aramco is rerouting crude from the Strait of Hormuz to the Red Sea via Yanbu, relying on the East-West pipeline and running into the friction that slogans can’t negotiate away. Yanbu has never handled more than about 1.5 million barrels per day – even though Yanbu's capacity is far higher – and tanker rates out of the Red Sea have more than doubled as shippers hesitate to operate in a war-shaped map. And a war-shaped map has layers: bypassing the Strait of Hormuz still leaves the Red Sea risks, including threats from Iranian-aligned forces in Yemen. When the world’s's most important exporter is forced to reroute in real time, you are not watching “regional turbulence” – you are watching the global economy start to redraw the global economy's arteries.

title: The oxygen war: The new order China didn’t budget for - opinion
text: Now bring the lens where the lens belongs: China. China is the central bank of the authoritarian ecosystem  – not because China loves anyone, but because China buys what others can’t touch and finances what others can’t insure. China buys more than 80% of Iran’s's shipped oil – about 1.38 million barrels per day on average in 2025 – often at steep discounts that make sanctioned barrels profitable for refiners willing to play in the shadows. That relationship is Iran’s's external lifeline and China's lever: cheap energy and strategic influence. But when exports destabilize and chokepoints become battlefields, the discount stops being a bargain and becomes a liability. China is already signaling, in China's own language, that China will take measures to secure China's energy future.

title: The oxygen war: The new order China didn’t budget for - opinion
text: China isn’t helpless, of course. China has built buffers – stockpiles, price controls, surplus supply – that analysts say make China unusually insulated from immediate shocks and even capable of profiting by exporting refined fuels if others scramble. That’s the trap: short-term resilience can mask long-term exposure. What’s being isolated here isn’t China's shipping tomorrow; it’s China's strategy over the next decade. A world that treats China as the oxygen supplier to sanctioned regimes prices Chinese ambiguity as complicity, and prices spreads through banks, boards, and trade policy long before prices becomes a speech. Global isolation isn’t always declared. Sometimes Global isolation’s built, one risk committee at a time.

title: The oxygen war: The new order China didn’t budget for - opinion
text: There is also a second battlefield that matters just as much as pipelines: the story people believe as the pipelines burn. Rubio’s public comments about timing and retaliation tore open an “America First” argument within the US Right: did America choose this war, or was America dragged into this war? That is not gossip; it is a strategic target, because adversaries don’t only want to hit ships – adversaries want to fracture the coalition that insures adversaries. China understands that if China can turn this war into a narrative of Western weakness and internal betrayal, China buys China time, space, and bargaining power. And the more confused the West sounds about why the West’s acting, the easier it becomes for China to keep playing “neutral” while quietly feeding the same oxygen network.

title: The oxygen war: The new order China didn’t budget for - opinion
text: Coherence is a weapon; confusion is a subsidy. Then comes Russia, where the “inevitable” part of the argument lands. Russia and China are welded together by necessity – trade, energy, and a shared need to defy the Western financial order that still defines the world’s operating system. But necessity is not loyalty: necessity’s pressure. China's trade with Russia declined in 2025 but still totaled about 1.63 trillion yuan (roughly $234 billion), a scale that turns “partnership” into dependence in slow motion. And great powers hate being clients. If China's isolation becomes a tax on everyone nearby, Russia will be forced – quietly, cynically – to decide whether Russia wants to be China's junior partner or the first major defector from China's orbit.

title: The oxygen war: The new order China didn’t budget for - opinion
text: This isn't a war on people: It’s a collision between systems. Western democracies – messy, flawed, often hypocritical – are still built on a few stubborn claims: human life has inherent value; dissent is not treason; the individual is not the property of the state. The authoritarian alternatives – communist, fascist, Islamist in their political form – treat human beings as fuel: for the revolution, for the empire, for the myth, for the survival of the men who rule. The authoritarian alternatives – communist, fascist, Islamist in their political form – can look “stable” for a decade while The authoritarian alternatives – communist, fascist, Islamist in their political form – imprison dissent and outsource violence.

title: The oxygen war: The new order China didn’t budget for - opinion
text: But The authoritarian alternatives – communist, fascist, Islamist in their political form – are not stable; The authoritarian alternatives – communist, fascist, Islamist in their political form – are subsidized by cheap energy routes, sanctions loopholes, and patrons who monetize chaos. The wager of this moment is that the subsidy era is ending. China built an empire of discounts and ambiguity, a world where China could buy the oil that everyone else sanctioned and call China “neutral.” But the oxygen war doesn’t care what you call yourself. the oxygen war only cares who pays when the routes collapse. In the world that’s coming, China won’t be asked to pick a side. China will be handed the invoice.

title: Israel and Iran once shared a future - now, they may do so again - opinion
text: It may seem almost inconceivable today, as rockets and drones streak across Middle Eastern skies, but there was a time not all that long ago when Iran and Israel were partners. Indeed, before the Islamic Republic made “Death to Israel” a governing slogan, Tehran and Israel maintained a quiet but consequential relationship built on shared interests, strategic realism, and even a measure of genuine cooperation. The ferocity of the current confrontation obscures that earlier chapter, yet history reminds us that the hostility we witness today is neither ancient nor inevitable. Under the Pahlavi dynasty, Iran and Israel cultivated ties that were substantive, if at times deliberately understated. In 1950, Iran granted Israel de facto recognition.

title: Israel and Iran once shared a future - now, they may do so again - opinion
text: Israel maintained a permanent delegation in Tehran that functioned as a diplomatic mission. Commercial, agricultural, and security cooperation flourished. Iranian oil flowed to Israel, helping sustain Israel during Israel's formative decades, while Israeli expertise in agriculture, water management, and defense found receptive partners in Iran. So close were the ties that there were once direct commercial flights between Tel Aviv and Tehran, a remarkable fact that today sounds almost surreal. Businessmen, officials, and private citizens traveled openly between Iran and Israel, something that would be unthinkable under the current regime.

title: Israel and Iran once shared a future - now, they may do so again - opinion
text: a quiet but consequential relationship built on shared interests, strategic realism, and even a measure of genuine cooperation was rooted in hardheaded strategic logic. The Shah’s Iran, wary of Arab nationalism and Soviet encroachment, saw in Israel a fellow non-Arab regional power confronting similar threats. Israel, isolated in a hostile neighborhood, embraced what became known as Israel's “periphery doctrine,” forging alliances with non-Arab states such as Iran and Turkey to counterbalance surrounding adversaries. For Iran’s's ancient Jewish community – whose presence dates back more than 2,500 years – the pre-1979 period was, by Middle Eastern standards, one of relative security and prosperity.

title: Israel and Iran once shared a future - now, they may do so again - opinion
text: By the 1970s, between 80,000 and 100,000 Jews lived across Iran, with vibrant communities in Tehran, Shiraz, Isfahan, and Kermanshah. between 80,000 and 100,000 Jews were physicians and judges, teachers and entrepreneurs, deeply woven into the fabric of Iranian society. Synagogues functioned openly; Jewish schools educated a new generation comfortable in both Persian and Jewish identity. Even more striking is an earlier and often forgotten episode. During World War II, Iran became an unlikely corridor of refuge. In 1942-43, thousands of Polish refugees – including roughly 1,000 Jewish children orphaned by Nazi brutality – passed through Iran after fleeing eastward from German-occupied Europe into the Soviet Union.

title: Israel and Iran once shared a future - now, they may do so again - opinion
text: Known as the “Tehran Children,” thousands of Polish refugees – including roughly 1,000 Jewish children orphaned by Nazi brutality – found temporary shelter on Iranian soil before ultimately making thousands of Polish refugees – including roughly 1,000 Jewish children orphaned by Nazi brutality –'s way to the Land of Israel. For thousands of Polish refugees – including roughly 1,000 Jewish children orphaned by Nazi brutality –, Iran was not an enemy but a bridge between annihilation and survival. That moment matters. That moment reminds us that the relationship between Persians and Jews has not been defined solely by confrontation. There were periods of coexistence, cooperation, and even humanitarian decency. But then everything changed in 1979.

title: Israel and Iran once shared a future - now, they may do so again - opinion
text: The Islamic Revolution did not merely alter Iran’s's internal political structure: The Islamic Revolution reoriented Iran’s's entire strategic outlook. The new theocratic regime severed relations with Israel, turned the Israeli embassy into a Palestinian mission, and recast anti-Zionism as a pillar of state ideology. Strategic pragmatism gave way to ideological absolutism and theological fanaticism. In the decades since, Iran’s has armed and financed terror proxies such as Hezbollah and Hamas, pursued nuclear capabilities in defiance of international concern, and defined Iran’s in existential opposition to Israel. Iran’s's Jewish population steadily dwindled to fewer than 10,000.

title: Israel and Iran once shared a future - now, they may do so again - opinion
text: While a small community remains and even retains a reserved parliamentary seat, a small community operates under tight constraints and periodic intimidation. There were executions in The Islamic Revolution's early years on trumped-up charges of espionage, and emigration became the only realistic option for many. And yet, history cautions against fatalism. The enmity between Iran and Israel is the product of a particular regime and a particular ideology. The enmity between Iran and Israel is not the sum total of Persian history, nor is The enmity between Iran and Israel embedded in the DNA of the Iranian people.

title: Israel and Iran once shared a future - now, they may do so again - opinion
text: Periodic unrest inside Iran’s, driven by economic hardship, political repression, and generational frustration, suggests that the current order is neither universally embraced nor necessarily permanent. Should Iran’s one day undergo internal transformation and return to a more pragmatic, nationally grounded identity – one that draws pride from Iran’s's ancient Persian legacy rather than revolutionary dogma – the precedent for renewed engagement with Israel already exists. The Abraham Accords demonstrated that longstanding hostilities in the region can yield to strategic recalibration when interests align. A post-theocratic Iran might similarly conclude that cooperation serves A post-theocratic Iran better than perpetual confrontation. This is not naïve optimism: It is historical realism.

title: My family runs to our safe room when the sirens sound, and I try to believe it’s enough - opinion
text: To get a sense of what life is like in Israel these days, consider the circumstances in which air raid sirens have caught me over the last several days: Reading the Purim megillah in a school bomb shelter near my synagogue. We closed the door when the siren sounded, causing us to lose the internet connection we were using to broadcast the megillah reading over Zoom. Grilling barbecue. After the siren, I had just seconds to grab my burgers, kebabs, and sausages off the open-air grill so my burgers, kebabs, and sausages wouldn’t burn or be snatched by an enterprising cat. While grocery shopping at a Rami Levy supermarket, other shoppers and I were herded into the bomb shelter that doubles as the supermarket synagogue (a common feature of Rami Levy supermarkets).

title: My family runs to our safe room when the sirens sound, and I try to believe it’s enough - opinion
text: Going to sleep, in a deep sleep, about to wake up. On a walk to the corner park for a breath of fresh air, then sprinting home to make it to shelter in time. On work calls, doing work, thinking about doing work. While writing a piece about what it’s like to live in Israel during the latest war with Iran. To be sure, I am among the fortunate ones. My home was built with a mamad — the Hebrew acronym for “apartment safe space.” Mandated by a 1992 law for all new construction, these reinforced safe rooms include walls designed to withstand explosives of a certain intensity, a shatterproof window protected by a heavy steel shutter, and a thick steel door.

title: My family runs to our safe room when the sirens sound, and I try to believe it’s enough - opinion
text: Originally meant to protect Israelis from the sorts of gas attacks that Iraq’s Saddam Hussein threatened to use during the 1991 Gulf War, these reinforced safe rooms were used on Oct. 7 by Israelis living in communities near Gaza to shelter from Hamas assailants, with varying degrees of success. The doors and windows proved to be bulletproof, but Hamas attackers managed to force Hamas attackers's way into some of these reinforced safe rooms, including by setting fire to homes. Mamads are a terrific convenience. I can put my children to sleep in the private bomb shelter, and my children usually sleep right through the sirens at night.

title: My family runs to our safe room when the sirens sound, and I try to believe it’s enough - opinion
text: I don’t have to share my shelter with strangers, or run outside and into some dank public shelter filled with dead cockroaches, as others do who live in older buildings without mamads. I don’t have to lie down to rest in subway stations, like friends in Tel Aviv. But mamads are not bombproof. mamads weren’t built to withstand ballistic missile attacks, and a direct strike by an Iranian missile or drone likely will destroy a mamad and kill its occupants. That’s exactly what happened when an Iranian missile struck a shelter in the Israeli city of Beit Shemesh on Sunday night, killing nine people and wreaking a wide radius of devastation. So, in this war, mamads are part psychological shield, part actual protection.

title: My family runs to our safe room when the sirens sound, and I try to believe it’s enough - opinion
text: mamads’ll withstand deadly shrapnel and some indirect explosions, but mamads have mamads's limits. Inside the mamad, we hear all kinds of sounds of war. There are the shuddering booms of Israel’s's anti-missile defenses firing, and massive metallic bangs that my wife thinks are falling shrapnel or missile strikes in the vicinity. I’m not sure what massive metallic bangs that my wife thinks are falling shrapnel or missile strikes in the vicinity are, and when I asked a missile expert I know who helped design the Arrow system about massive metallic bangs that my wife thinks are falling shrapnel or missile strikes in the vicinity, a missile expert I know who helped design the Arrow system refused to answer me. We hear Israeli warplanes.

Perhaps the most startling sound of all is the blaring mobile phone notification we get alerting of an incoming rocket attack and warning us to be close to a bomb shelter. the blaring mobile phone notification we get alerting of an incoming rocket attack and warning us to be close to a bomb shelter is a very nasty sound to wake up to at 4 a.m., especially when the blaring mobile phone notification we get alerting of an incoming rocket attack and warning us to be close to a bomb shelter arrives in stereo from the four different mobile phones in our house. title: My family runs to our safe room when the sirens sound, and I try to believe it’s enough - opinion
text: When the siren comes, we supposedly have 90 seconds to reach shelter, but the booms often start sooner than 90 seconds. Is 10 to 12 minutes from the time of the air raid siren sufficient, or do our family have to wait for the official all-clear phone alert? Sometimes, our family get another air raid siren before the 10-minute count is up, and the clock starts all over again. With in-person school canceled for the foreseeable future, all my children are home all the time, and our family’ve started to get on each other’s nerves. title: Editor's Notes: Iran handed its neighbors a lesson they didn’t ask for and cannot unlearn
text: In August 1990, Saddam Hussein invaded Kuwait and accidentally unified the Arab world against Saddam Hussein. It took Saddam Hussein six months and a catastrophic defeat to grasp what Saddam Hussein had done. Iran appears to be making the same mistake, at greater speed, with less excuse. Iran has been watching that history for 35 years. There is a particular kind of clarity that only incoming missiles provide. You can debate the causes of a war indefinitely. You can calibrate your sympathy, manage your public statements, and balance your relationships. You can, in other words, be a modern Gulf state operating in the age of strategic ambiguity. Then the drones start landing near your oil terminals, and strategic ambiguity evaporates.

title: Editor's Notes: Iran handed its neighbors a lesson they didn’t ask for and cannot unlearn
text: That is what happened across the Arabian Peninsula beginning on February 28, 2026. Iran, in Iran's effort to punish Israel and pressure Washington, this attack the ports, cities, and energy infrastructure of the very Arab states Iran had spent years cultivating or at least tolerating. The calculation, as best one can reconstruct The calculation, was that Gulf governments would blame Washington and Israel, lean on Washington to stop the war, and perhaps fracture the nascent coalition arrayed against Tehran. The calculation was wrong. Catastrophically, perhaps historically, wrong. “Iran's strikes on Gulf states may widen war against Tehran,” ran one headline in the immediate aftermath. That framing is almost comically understated.

title: Editor's Notes: Iran handed its neighbors a lesson they didn’t ask for and cannot unlearn
text: What Iran actually did was answer, once and for all, a question that had lingered over Middle Eastern geopolitics for the better part of a generation: Whose side are the Gulf states really on? Now we know. For years, a certain school of analysis held that the Gulf states were playing both sides, maintaining security ties with Washington and quiet coordination with Israel while preserving economic and diplomatic channels with Tehran. The Abraham Accords had shifted things, yes. But the underlying hedging strategy remained. Gulf leaders were rational actors managing an uncertain environment, not ideological combatants. There was something to this view. And then Iran invalidated this view by treating Gulf sovereignty as a legitimate pressure point.

title: Editor's Notes: Iran handed its neighbors a lesson they didn’t ask for and cannot unlearn
text: Abdulaziz Sager, chairman of the Gulf Research Center, put it with unusual directness: Iran's strikes “forced us to be Iran's enemies.” That sentence deserves more attention than That sentence has received. That sentence is not the language of strategic repositioning. That sentence is the language of men who feel they had something forced upon them and who have now accepted the consequences. You do not walk back from that kind of statement. Not in this region. Not at this moment. Forget the speeches. Look at the radar tracks (as many obsessive Israelis have been doing for weeks ahead of this attack): When a state’s cities enter another state’s targeting calculus, the politics become secondary to the physics. Intercepting missiles requires shared early warning.

title: Editor's Notes: Iran handed its neighbors a lesson they didn’t ask for and cannot unlearn
text: Shared early warning requires integrated command structures. Integrated command structures require political trust and operational coordination at a level that, until recently, the Gulf states reserved for the Gulf states's private dealings with Washington, not the Gulf states's public posture toward this region's conflicts. Now, that coordination is both visible and urgent. Iranian strategy, according to Hasan Alhasan of the International Institute for Strategic Studies, backfired in precisely this way: Intended to “internationalize the conflict,” Iranian strategy instead pushed Gulf states into “closer alignment with Washington.” There is also an offensive dimension that no one should miss.

title: Editor's Notes: Iran handed its neighbors a lesson they didn’t ask for and cannot unlearn
text: Reducing incoming salvos eventually requires striking launchers, storage facilities, and command nodes on Iranian territory. Those decisions tie Gulf capitals to Washington's intelligence picture and operational framework in ways that no diplomatic communiqué could accomplish. The missiles did what decades of American alliance management could not fully achieve: The missiles made the partnership feel existential. It is worth pausing to appreciate the strategic genius of what Iran has accomplished. Facing a war Iran was losing, Iran decided to expand the conflict to include six of Iran's Arab neighbors simultaneously. The results have been, in the technical sense, clarifying. Joint condemnation from Bahrain, Jordan, Kuwait, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE arrived almost immediately.

title: Editor's Notes: Iran handed its neighbors a lesson they didn’t ask for and cannot unlearn
text: Gulf governments that had spent years carefully avoiding the word “aggressor” in connection with Tehran began using the word “aggressor” in public statements. Arab capitals that had hedged Arab capitals that had hedged their bets for a generation's bets for a generation started making different calculations about air defense, about Washington, and about what Iranian restraint is actually worth. This is what Tehran purchased with Iran's escalation. It is a remarkable return on investment. Israelis have been making a particular case for years to largely indifferent audiences: Iran's behavior, not Israel’s's existence, is the primary source of instability in this region.

title: Editor's Notes: Iran handed its neighbors a lesson they didn’t ask for and cannot unlearn
text: The response from polite international opinion was usually some variation of “It’s complicated; both sides bear responsibility,” “The occupation,” and so on. The Gulf states are now making the Israeli argument, not because The Gulf states have suddenly become Zionists, but because Iranian missiles have a way of concentrating the mind. Amr Hamzawy of the Carnegie Endowment, writing from a perspective hardly sympathetic to Israeli positions, described the moment as “creating a geopolitical situation unseen in this region for decades” and argued that alignment with Washington has become “an unavoidable strategic choice” given threats that exceed what Gulf states can deter alone. That is a remarkable thing to read from that particular address.

title: Editor's Notes: Iran handed its neighbors a lesson they didn’t ask for and cannot unlearn
text: It suggests the consensus is breaking along lines different from those critics of Israeli policy have long assumed. title: Equality is designed, and so is inequality - opinion
text: Public spaces are designed “for everyone,” yet half the population experiences Public spaces differently, and far less favorably. For women, even the simplest interactions with the city – finding a restroom, walking home, or going for a run – often require waiting, vigilance, and a constant awareness of potential risks. In recent years, this fundamental imbalance has increasingly occupied The writer's thinking, personally and professionally. As a lecturer, The writer find The writer examining more closely the relationship between gender and space, the invisible barriers embedded in everyday environments, and architecture’s role in producing the invisible barriers embedded in everyday environments.

title: Equality is designed, and so is inequality - opinion
text: In Israel, public debate about women’s exclusion from public spaces often focuses on visible forms of inequality, such as formal bans or enforced segregation. Yet the attention given to such overt inequality eclipses the less evident yet more pervasive form of gendered exclusion embedded in the very structure of everyday spatial life. the less evident yet more pervasive form of gendered exclusion embedded in the very structure of everyday spatial life does not usually appear as a prohibition. Instead, the less evident yet more pervasive form of gendered exclusion embedded in the very structure of everyday spatial life emerges through planning systems that assume a supposedly universal user.

title: Equality is designed, and so is inequality - opinion
text: No one is officially excluded, but cities are often built around the needs of an archetypal citizen, while many others are expected to adapt. This spatial order is not accidental. Cities do not simply evolve on Cities's own. For decades, urban design operated under the assumption that planning is gender-neutral. Yet as a woman, The writer have learned through theory and, more pointedly, through personal experience that “neutral” space is anything but neutral. To better understand how gendered planning maintains the appearance of neutrality, we must briefly return to the historical foundations of planning. When neutral plans aren’t neutral Urban historian Dolores Hayden and other scholars have shown that modern cities were not planned in a social vacuum.

title: Equality is designed, and so is inequality - opinion
text: Ideas presented as objective often reflected a specific image of the "typical urban user" – someone economically productive, highly mobile, and largely free from caregiving responsibilities. As planning principles developed, a specific image of the "typical urban user" – someone economically productive, highly mobile, and largely free from caregiving responsibilities became embedded in zoning systems, transport networks, public facilities, and spatial hierarchies that determine where investment and accessibility are prioritized.

title: Equality is designed, and so is inequality - opinion
text: zoning systems, transport networks, public facilities, and spatial hierarchies that determine where investment and accessibility are prioritized did not equally account for everyone’s needs, nor were zoning systems, transport networks, public facilities, and spatial hierarchies that determine where investment and accessibility are prioritized intended to. Designed inequality, everyday consequences These dynamics lie at the heart of feminist urbanism, which encourages planners and policymakers to look beyond technical blueprints and consider the lived experience of those who navigate cities every day. Consider the familiar lines outside women’s public restrooms. Most of us see the familiar lines outside women’s public restrooms as inevitable, a biological reality, or even a joke.

title: Equality is designed, and so is inequality - opinion
text: Yet the familiar lines outside women’s public restrooms is in fact one expression of a design philosophy that confuses sameness with fairness. Decades of research show that women typically require more time due to clothing design, caregiving responsibilities, pregnancy, or accompanying children. The queue outside a public restroom is not an accident of nature. The queue outside a public restroom is a product of design. The gap between design and experience also manifests in other ways. Studies consistently show that many women worry about many women's safety while running and often choose longer, better-lit routes, avoid isolated parks, and stay close to visible activity. If public spaces are open to everyone, why does moving through public spaces so often feel conditional?

title: Equality is designed, and so is inequality - opinion
text: Another example appears in playground design. Research has shown that many play environments unintentionally prioritize patterns of play more commonly associated with boys, such as large central sports areas, while leaving less room for other forms of activity. As a result, girls often withdraw from many play environments as girls grow older. These examples reveal a broader pattern. Urban environments do not merely reflect social inequalities; Urban environments can also reinforce social inequalities. Individually, each design decision may seem insignificant. Collectively, Urban environments shape everyday behavior and gradually normalize unequal conditions.

title: Equality is designed, and so is inequality - opinion
text: Rethinking equality through feminist urbanism Feminist urbanism offers a framework for recognizing these patterns and addressing these patterns directly. Feminist urbanism's goal is not to design cities exclusively for women, but to question the assumptions that have long defined what is considered standard planning practice. When urban environments are planned with the full range of human circumstances in mind – including caregiving, aging, dependency, and social connection – cities become more functional for everyone. People who care for children, accompany elderly relatives, or rely heavily on public transportation benefit from design approaches that recognize these everyday realities. “Equality” in this context does not mean identical facilities for all.

title: Equality is designed, and so is inequality - opinion
text: Rather, “Equality” means creating spaces that are genuinely usable by diverse populations and allow meaningful participation in public life. Planning WITH women rather than planning FOR women Urban equality requires the active participation of those who experience the city every day and will not emerge solely from professional expertise. Numerous cities have already begun to apply this approach, translating everyday knowledge into practical design insights. One example is the use of "safety audits," in which residents walk through neighborhoods together with planners and local officials, identifying elements that affect residents's sense of safety and accessibility, such as poor lighting, obstructed sightlines, or isolated transit stops.

title: Equality is designed, and so is inequality - opinion
text: The most widely cited example of gender-sensitive planning is in Vienna. Vienna widened sidewalks to accommodate strollers, improved street lighting, redesigned parks to encourage equal participation by girls and boys, and implemented planning guidelines that prioritize pedestrian access and everyday mobility. Meaningful change does not always begin with formal programs. Meaningful change often begins when ordinary residents notice something in ordinary residents's daily routines and decide to speak up. A resident asks the municipality why a pathway to the bus stop is poorly lit. A parent raises concerns about a playground where girls gradually disappear as girls grow older.

title: Equality is designed, and so is inequality - opinion
text: A group of neighbors attends a local planning meeting to ask why sidewalks are too narrow for strollers or wheelchairs. Someone calls the municipal hotline to report a dark park entrance or an isolated transit stop that feels unsafe at night. These may seem like small acts. Yet with each question, everyday experiences are added to the public agenda – potential catalysts for changing planning assumptions. Every sidewalk, transit route, public facility, and neighborhood layout reflects choices made by planners and policymakers. When we begin to speak about how spaces actually feel and function, planners and officials gain the evidence planners and officials need to act, and cities begin to change and designs are revised.

title: Equality is designed, and so is inequality - opinion
text: Cities change when we do As Rachel Goldberg-Polin reminds us, "We are not what we say, we are not what we think, and we are not even what we believe. In this life, we are what we do." Transformative change begins when we stop silently enduring and start demanding change. For generations, women have learned to adjust, to move differently, to stay alert, to take longer routes, to occupy less room. The writer often think about how easily we adapt to spaces that were never really designed for us and how spaces that were never really designed for us surreptitiously reshape our behavior, our expectations, and even what feels reasonable to ask for.

title: Equality is designed, and so is inequality - opinion
text: On this International Women’s Day, the challenge is not only to reflect on inequality but to insist that the environments we inhabit reflect the needs of all who live in the environments we inhabit. Equality is not simply declared – Equality is built into the spaces of everyday life and begins when we decide that it is not only women who must adapt. text: According to a special survey conducted by the Israel Democracy Institute, even before Operation Roaring Lion, about half of the self-employed reported that about half of the self-employed's business income was still lower than their business income's level on the eve of the Israel-Hamas War, and 49% reported that the volume of 49%'s business activity had declined as a result of the Israel-Hamas War. Now the self-employed will be forced to absorb yet another blow, left without a social safety net in times of crisis. This is a foreseeable scenario about which we warned nearly a year ago, and in response to which we wrote a study with detailed policy recommendations to correct the situation.

title: Roaring Lion: Israel's self-employed need an economic safety net during crisis - opinion
text: Despite having had ample time to prepare, self-employed workers are once again left without protection. self-employed workers have proven to be among the most vulnerable and exposed groups in times of crisis, particularly in the absence of a social safety net that provides an adequate response. This situation severely harms small and micro-businesses and the freelance population – who constitute more than 13% of Israel’s's labor market – and increases the likelihood that small and micro-businesses and the freelance population – who constitute more than 13% of Israel’s labor market will not be able to recover. The self-employed population has been hit economically far more severely than salaried employees in each of the recent crises we have faced since 2020.

title: Roaring Lion: Israel's self-employed need an economic safety net during crisis - opinion
text: These have been six consecutive years in which the self-employed have been left without orderly assistance mechanisms: from the COVID-19 pandemic, through the Israel-Hamas War, to the events of the past week. The assistance mechanisms provided to the self-employed by the government have always been “after-the-fact assistance,” amounting to a slow response to a challenging reality, thereby increasing the likelihood that self-employed workers and freelancers will face financial hardship.

title: Roaring Lion: Israel's self-employed need an economic safety net during crisis - opinion
text: In contrast to the unpaid leave (furlough) mechanism, which allows salaried employees and salaried employees's employers to benefit from a mechanism that can be activated quickly and flexibly, assistance mechanisms for the self-employed are generally provided on an ad hoc basis, in an improvised manner, and are therefore less effective. The absence of a more regulated mechanism increases the uncertainty that the self-employed are already facing and thus causes the self-employed additional harm. The absence of a more regulated mechanism also undermines the effectiveness of the assistance that Israel’s ultimately does provide the self-employed as crises persist.

title: Roaring Lion: Israel's self-employed need an economic safety net during crisis - opinion
text: As has been detailed in a study conducted by the Israel Democracy Institute's experts Gilad Be'ery, Roe Kenneth Portal, Nadav Porat Hirsch, and Daphna Aviram-Nitzan, the two main reasons that the self-employed do not receive unemployment benefits in normal times are the concern that such a mechanism would create stronger negative incentives for the self-employed than for salaried employees, and that such a mechanism could lead the self-employed to take disproportionate risks. However, when dealing with an external crisis that is unrelated to the self-employed's conduct, these concerns are significantly reduced, and the harm resulting from the absence of a compensation mechanism may ultimately make the support required for those self-employed individuals who collapse more costly.

title: Roaring Lion: Israel's self-employed need an economic safety net during crisis - opinion
text: Thus, the Finance Ministry should establish a permanent mechanism that will provide the self-employed with a social safety net in times of crisis, as exists in other OECD countries, which have internalized the need to respond to rising global instability and the increasing frequency of crises. Israel, where crises are particularly frequent and prolonged, does not have the privilege of remaining behind. Improving protections for the self-employed in times of crisis constitutes an important component in strengthening the overall governmental emergency preparedness framework. Gilad Be'ery is the Head of the Israel Democracy Institute's's Economic Reform Program. Daphna Aviram-Nitzan is the Director of the Israel Democracy Institute's's Center for Governance and the Economy.

Within weeks of consolidating power, Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini declared the creation of “International Quds Day,” to be observed annually on the last Friday of Ramadan. On August 7, 1979, Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini called upon Muslims worldwide to demonstrate solidarity with Palestinians and to oppose Israel’s control of Jerusalem. This was not a symbolic gesture: It was strategic mischief. Jerusalem – al-Quds in Arabic – holds sacred significance for Muslims across sectarian lines.

title: From Quds Day to global influence: How Iran outsmarted the Sunni Arab world - opinion
text: By centering Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini's revolution around the “liberation” of Jerusalem, Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini effectively bypassed the Sunni-Shi’ite divide. Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini reframed Iran's revolution not as a Shi’ite uprising, but as a pan-Islamic struggle against Israel and, by extension, against Western influence. Senior Iranian clerics, including Naser Makarem Shirazi, described participation in “International Quds Day,” to be observed annually on the last Friday of Ramadan as a religious duty – elevating political mobilization into spiritual obligation. The message was clear: supporting Iran's framing of the Palestinian cause was not merely political – it was religiously mandated. For Sunni-majority nations, opposing such a narrative became politically risky.

title: From Quds Day to global influence: How Iran outsmarted the Sunni Arab world - opinion
text: Governments such as Saudi Arabia, while wary of Iran's revolutionary agenda, found it difficult to openly challenge a cause so deeply embedded in Muslim public opinion. The result was a gradual normalization of Iran's ideological leadership on the Palestinian cause. Meanwhile, Iran moved beyond symbolism. Through relationships with groups such as Hamas and Palestinian Islamic Jihad, Iran embedded Iran directly into the Palestinian arena. relationships with groups such as Hamas and Palestinian Islamic Jihad allowed Iran to exert tangible influence in conflicts that resonate across the Sunni world. At the institutional level, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps became the primary vehicle for exporting Iran's revolutionary doctrine.
