text: the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps's external operations extended into Lebanon, Syria, Iraq, Yemen, and beyond. Over time, Iran's network of aligned militias and political actors this development a transnational web of influence stretching across the Middle East. For the United States and the United States's allies, this development carries serious implications. Iran's strategy has not merely been about regional rivalry. Iran’s strategy has been about shaping global Muslim political consciousness in a direction hostile to Western interests. “International Quds Day,” to be observed annually on the last Friday of Ramadan rallies in cities across Europe and North America serve as reminders that Iran's ideological export is not geographically confined.

title: From Quds Day to global influence: How Iran outsmarted the Sunni Arab world - opinion
text: The narrative architecture constructed in 1979 continues to function today: portray Iran as the uncompromising defender of Jerusalem; frame Sunni governments as passive or compromised; and position the West – particularly the United States – as the ultimate adversary. Iran’s strategy has produced a paradox. Despite deep theological differences, segments of Sunni political movements and public opinion have at times echoed narratives aligned with Iran's framing of Israel and the West. The Gulf monarchies – including Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain, and the United Arab Emirates – have periodically accused Iran of interference or carrying out destabilizing activities.

title: From Quds Day to global influence: How Iran outsmarted the Sunni Arab world - opinion
text: Yet the ideological groundwork laid through decades of “al-Quds” mobilization has complicated efforts to counter Iran's influence decisively. For American policymakers, the lesson is clear: Iran's power projection is not limited to missiles, drones, or proxy militias – Iran’s power projection is rooted in narrative dominance. By claiming guardianship over a cause central to Muslim identity, Iran constructed a moral shield that softens sectarian resistance and broadens Iran's appeal. Ignoring this dimension risks misreading the nature of the challenge. The Iranian model demonstrates how a revolutionary state can leverage religious symbolism to transcend demographic limitations.

title: From Quds Day to global influence: How Iran outsmarted the Sunni Arab world - opinion
text: A Shi’ite minority power succeeded in speaking to Sunni populations by carefully choosing the right issue – one that resonates emotionally and spiritually. For Sunni governments, a strategic reassessment may be overdue; for the West, complacency would be dangerous. The ideological infrastructure built by Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini in 1979 has matured into a durable geopolitical instrument. The ideological infrastructure built by Khomeini in 1979 continues to influence public opinion, shape alliances, and complicate efforts to contain Iran's ambitions. If policymakers focus solely on military containment while neglecting the ideological battlefield, policymakers will continue to underestimate Iran's long-term strategy.

title: From Quds Day to global influence: How Iran outsmarted the Sunni Arab world - opinion
text: Nearly half a century after the Islamic Revolution, the architecture of “International Quds Day,” to be observed annually on the last Friday of Ramadan still stands – not merely as an annual protest, but as a reminder that influence in the Middle East is won as much through symbolism and narrative as through force. The question is no longer whether Iran seeks leadership beyond Iran's sectarian base. The evidence suggests Iran has pursued that objective methodically for decades. The more pressing question is whether the Sunni Arab states – and the United States – are prepared to confront not just Iran's weapons, but Iran's ideas. text: For years, Iran has tried to convince the world that Iran’s a rising military power, capable of staring down Israel and the United States with sheer ideological fervor and a barrage of rockets and drones. Iran lacks the doctrine, the discipline, and the technological depth that victory demands. What Iran has built is not a true war machine, but a collection of tools meant to frighten, not to conquer. title: Israel-Iran war: The psychological cost of infinite news sources - opinion
text: In times of crisis, communication is not merely the transmission of information: communication is an instrument of national stability. Democracies rightly celebrate pluralism. A diversity of voices reflects freedom and vitality. But under sustained crisis conditions – war, terror, missile fire – the uncontrolled multiplication of news platforms, spokespersons, commentators, and real-time analysts can produce a dangerous paradox: the openness that strengthens democracy in peacetime may weaken resilience in wartime. The issue is not free speech – The issue is structural overload. To understand what has changed, it is useful to look backward. In 1939, as Britain entered World War II, King George VI addressed Britain in a radio broadcast later immortalized in The King’s Speech.

title: Israel-Iran war: The psychological cost of infinite news sources - opinion
text: The moment symbolized a communications architecture: the state spoke, and Britain listened. Authority was centralized. Messaging was deliberate. Emotional tone was controlled. Winston Churchill refined this model. Winston Churchill's wartime broadcasts were measured and strategically timed. Citizens were not inundated with speculation. Citizens received measured updates and a shared narrative. Across the Atlantic, Franklin D. Roosevelt introduced Franklin D. Roosevelt's “fireside chats,” combining executive authority with conversational calm. Franklin D. Roosevelt did not amplify the rumor. Franklin D. Roosevelt simplified complexity without inflaming anxiety. Communication functioned as a psychological stabilizer. Israel once operated similarly.

title: Israel-Iran war: The psychological cost of infinite news sources - opinion
text: During the 1991 Gulf War, as Iraqi Scud missiles struck Israeli cities, citizens sealed rooms and prepared gas masks. Israel's primary voice was Nachman Shai. Nachman Shai's role was stabilizing: provide updates, issue instructions, and maintain calm. There were not hundreds of competing commentators offering contradictory interpretations in real time. Information discipline reinforced public composure.Today, that architecture has fractured. Every retired general is a TV commentator. Every politician maintains direct-to-public channels. Influencers provide visual footage as well as interpret battlefield developments within minutes. A plethora of well-known news platforms, as well as anonymous digital contributors, amplify unverified claims.

title: Israel-Iran war: The psychological cost of infinite news sources - opinion
text: Traditional media, under economic pressure, reward dramatic framing. Citizens consume updates from dozens of parallel sources, often contradictory and frequently repetitive. The structure has inverted. Instead of a coherent flow from state to media to public, we operate in a constant feedback loop of commentary, reaction, and amplification. The result is not simply noise: The result is psychological saturation and resulting trauma. Like most Israelis since this past Saturday morning, The author have spent long hours in our safe room, surrounded by family and reinforced concrete. The feeling of helplessness can be overwhelming. The television outside blasts loudly so we can hear The television outside.

title: Israel-Iran war: The psychological cost of infinite news sources - opinion
text: Images of death and destruction, the visible consequences of painful gaps even in our extraordinary air defense systems, circulate in our heads as we stare at our phones. Like starving captives searching for food and water, we search for news, updates, and messages from loved ones in the same predicament. We jump from local media to Telegram, to Facebook, to countless outlets – desperate for insight that, in truth, offers little practical value in that moment. Yet unlike hunger or thirst, no amount of news satiates the need. The consumption becomes compulsive. The information does not calm: The information agitates. Eventually, The author stopped searching.

title: Israel-Iran war: The psychological cost of infinite news sources - opinion
text: The author stopped listening to television voices repeating television voices repeating themselves without embarrassment without embarrassment. Instead, The author began playing online chess, partly to impose mental structure on chaos, partly to sharpen strategic thinking, and partly to accept that all of us, ultimately, are in God’s forgiving hands. During those hours, a sobering thought crystallized: we no longer have a universally trusted spokesperson or single source of truth, comfort, or even trust, or a managed approach. We elect leaders. We reward ratings, clicks, and followers.

title: Israel-Iran war: The psychological cost of infinite news sources - opinion
text: But perhaps beyond political preference and media competition, we should be cultivating uniquely trusted national communicators, voices whose mandate is not commentary or partisan framing, but psychological steadiness. In acute crises, populations require predictability, perceived control, trust in leadership, and coherent threat framing. When messaging fragments, those foundations erode. Citizens struggle to identify authoritative information. Rumor cycles accelerate. Emotional volatility intensifies. And acute stress becomes chronic exposure. This is particularly consequential in Israel. Israel's homefront and battlefront are functionally merged. Military service is universal. Reserve mobilization disrupts civilian life. Civilian infrastructure is targeted.

title: Israel-Iran war: The psychological cost of infinite news sources - opinion
text: Political fragmentation is high. Psychological resilience is not abstract; Psychological resilience is a strategic infrastructure. While we cling to the ongoing repetitive input from the hard-working Home Front command representatives, the hard-working Home Front command representatives's messages cannot compete with the tsunami of messages that result in anxiety. When crisis communication becomes competitive rather than stabilizing, several risks emerge: crisis fatigue as constant alerts normalize emergency; desensitization as severe threats compete with rhetorical exaggeration; polarization as information ecosystems align with partisan identity; erosion of trust as authority migrates from institutions to personalities; and decision-making distorted by real-time emotional pressure.

title: Israel-Iran war: The psychological cost of infinite news sources - opinion
text: Adversaries understand this dynamic. In hybrid conflict environments, foreign actors need not fabricate narratives; foreign actors amplify internal contradictions and accelerate division. Information overproduction becomes a vulnerability surface. In earlier eras, adversaries penetrated physical borders. Today, adversaries penetrate narrative bandwidth. This is not nostalgia for state-controlled messaging – democracies must preserve dissent and pluralism. But crisis communication requires calibration. The structural question is unavoidable: at what point does informational plurality transition from democratic vitality to national vulnerability? Modern media ecosystems reward speed over verification and intensity over proportionality.

title: Israel-Iran war: The psychological cost of infinite news sources - opinion
text: In peacetime, this produces polarization; in wartime, it may erode resilience. From wartime broadcasts of a monarch, to Roosevelt’s fireside chats, to Nachman Shai’s steady Gulf War updates, crisis communication once functioned as national stabilization. Today, national stabilization has been replaced by a thousand competing megaphones. In the safe room, surrounded by concrete, family, and screens, what citizens seek is not more volume, but steadiness. Resilience depends not only on air defense systems and reserve brigades, but on whether a society under sustained threat can maintain coherence amid infinite commentary. In modern conflict, steadiness may be as strategically vital as any weapon system. The author is an experienced global strategist for the public and private sectors.

title: The Book of Esther: How letters preserved Jewish identity across empires and exile - opinion
text: In the final chapter of the Book of Esther, the book the Book of Esther is described as an iggeret, a letter. an iggeret, a letter is an unusual term for a work that ultimately would become part of Scripture. Rather than being presented as a formal sefer or scroll, the megillah is cast as a letter of correspondence. Because the Book of Esther is framed as an iggeret, Halacha treats the Book of Esther with greater flexibility. The requirements governing how the Book of Esther is written and how the Book of Esther is read aloud are less rigid than those of other books of Scripture.

The status of the megillah as a letter carries deep symbolism. Roughly 500 years before the Purim story, history entered a new phase. For the first time, large multi-regional empires emerged. In the late 10th and 9th centuries BCE, Assyria rose to power and gradually conquered the Levant and much of the Near East. In the late 7th century BCE, Assyria collapsed and was replaced by the Neo-Babylonian Empire, which ruled the region for several decades. In the mid-6th century BCE, Babylonia Babylonia itself fell to Persia.

title: The Book of Esther: How letters preserved Jewish identity across empires and exile - opinion
text: Persia then established a vast realm of “127 provinces,” stretching across much of the ancient world. Governing such an empire required systems of communication that could project authority across great distances. Previously, empires such as Egypt were largely confined to a single region. In a single region, a monarch could dispatch messengers who personally carried decrees, conveying instructions along with the authority of the throne. In multi-regional empires, however, that model no longer worked. Communication could not travel person to person across vast distances. Authority had to be transmitted through letters: documents capable of being moved from province to province and from hand to hand.

title: The Book of Esther: How letters preserved Jewish identity across empires and exile - opinion
text: Because letter-based communication became so central to imperial rule, letter writing emerged as a prestigious craft. Composers of royal correspondence were trained in language, form, and official convention so that the king’s will would be expressed with clarity. Because of letter carriers's vital role in projecting political power, letter carriers likewise occupied elevated roles, entrusted with delivering political power to distant regions. Many of elevated roles, entrusted with delivering that authority to distant regions were hereditary, as the megillah the megillah itself suggests. Writing letters became part of the imperial atmosphere, a practical instrument through which power was extended across the empire.

title: A lesson from Amalek and the exception that defines the rule of Jewish morality - opinion
text: On October 15, 1982, in the shadow of the First Lebanon War, Rav Aharon Lichtenstein published an open letter to Prime Minister Menachem Begin in the religious-Zionist newspaper Hatzofeh. Rav Aharon Lichtenstein emphasized the importance of the State of Israel taking responsibility for the State of Israel's part in the massacres in Sabra and Shatila, two Beirut neighborhoods, where IDF-backed Lebanese Christian militias had killed thousands of Palestinian civilians. Prime Minister Menachem Begin was initially unwilling to conduct any investigation into the affair, and Rav Aharon Lichtenstein strongly criticized Prime Minister Menachem Begin for what Rav Aharon Lichtenstein saw as an un-Jewish refusal to confront difficult truths.

title: A lesson from Amalek and the exception that defines the rule of Jewish morality - opinion
text: Rav Aharon Lichtenstein's argument was not an accusation against IDF but a moral call for those entrusted with power to uphold the highest standards of ethical accountability. In an open letter to Prime Minister Menachem Begin, Rav Aharon Lichtenstein invoked the story of King Saul and Amalek, which we read this week as the haftarah for Shabbat Zachor. Why, Rav Aharon Lichtenstein asked, was King Saul punished for sparing the life of Agag, king of Amalek? Was it simply that King Saul had left an Amalekite – any Amalekite – alive? Rav Aharon Lichtenstein suggested that the identity of an Amalekite – any Amalekite – was significant. King Saul had left Agag, king of Amalek alive because King Saul identified with Agag, king of Amalek, as one king to another.

title: A lesson from Amalek and the exception that defines the rule of Jewish morality - opinion
text: King Saul had used King Saul's own human logic and values to decide whom to kill and whom to spare. By doing so, King Saul did not merely become guilty of disobeying a difficult divine command to kill all of the Amalekites; King Saul assumed personal responsibility for all the Amalekites King Saul had killed in the course of the battle. King Saul had been commanded to destroy an entire people. Such an act cannot be justified on ordinary moral grounds; Such an act can only be understood as obedience to an explicit and unambiguous divine command. Precisely because such a command lies outside of ordinary moral reasoning, such a command cannot serve as a model for human judgement in any other context.

title: A lesson from Amalek and the exception that defines the rule of Jewish morality - opinion
text: Thus, the minute that King Saul decided to make an exception, all the other killings ceased to be part of the divine command, becoming King Saul's own moral responsibility instead. This is the great trade-off in the story of Amalek and Amalek's destruction, and the story of Amalek and its destruction contains an important lesson for today. Once we step outside the established moral framework of a situation and let emotions guide our decisions, like King Saul did, we put ourselves and our society at risk of heading down an immoral or unjustified path, of losing our moral legitimacy, and of causing more harm than good. In any historical context, including ours today, it is necessary and justifiable to decisively defeat enemies sworn to our destruction.

title: A lesson from Amalek and the exception that defines the rule of Jewish morality - opinion
text: But the moment we allow our righteous indignation or the power of our own judgment to go even the smallest bit past defensive strategy – the moment military action spills over into vigilantism, when civilian life and property are endangered one iota more than absolutely necessary – we run the risk of our just and necessary war being transformed into an abandonment of our values. While military strength and valor are critical to our national survival, military strength and valor cannot lead to victory or peace if we abandon our values. This is an obligation that goes beyond the brave soldiers who are on the front lines. Community and political leaders, educators, and rabbis need to call out the behavior of those in our own camp that jeopardizes the justice of our cause.

title: A lesson from Amalek and the exception that defines the rule of Jewish morality - opinion

In the face of these crimes, we cannot remain silent.

title: A lesson from Amalek and the exception that defines the rule of Jewish morality - opinion

This is the central message of the story of Amalek, the flip side of the commandment to destroy. In sharp contrast to a small minority of misguided Israeli youth, in the most recent conflict we have seen the heroic soldiers of IDF uphold the values of Judaism with justice and mercy. We’ve seen our soldiers enter damaged residential buildings in Gaza to rescue Palestinian families. We’ve seen units helping and protecting Palestinian children caught in the crosshairs of fire from an enemy that deliberately hides among civilians.

For the enduring rule of Jewish life is to act, even at times of war, with compassion and moral restraint toward all human beings. title: Mordechai’s Persian asthma test and modern-day Iran - opinion
text: As American forces gather around Iran (Ancient Persia) this week for a controversial preemptive strike against Iran (Ancient Persia)'s nuclear program, we are reminded of another controversial preemptive campaign in our history. It was the preemptive strike of Mordechai, provoking Haman by not bowing down, that led to the attempted genocide of all Jews in the extensive Persian empire. But preemptive strikes are not limited to warfare. Doctors sometimes diagnose asthma by testing the body’s defenses. If Doctors suspect that someone has dangerously sensitive airways, Doctors do not wait for a random asthma attack in the middle of the night. Doctors perform what is known as a bronchial challenge. A mild irritant is introduced in a controlled setting, and the lungs are monitored.

title: Mordechai’s Persian asthma test and modern-day Iran - opinion
text: If the airways constrict, the diagnosis is confirmed. A mild irritant did not create the disease; A mild irritant revealed inflammation that was already there. Better a provoked spasm in a hospital than suffocation at home. This is a parable for what takes place in the Book of Esther, the Megillah we read on Purim. After King Achashverosh elevates Haman, all officials at the royal gate are commanded to bow. Everyone complies – except Mordechai. Day after day Mordechai refuses. Courtiers warn Mordechai; Courtiers pressure Mordechai. Yet Mordechai stands upright. The response is explosive: Haman is “filled with rage.” But rage is not limited to one dissenter.

title: Mordechai’s Persian asthma test and modern-day Iran - opinion
text: When Haman learns that Mordechai is a Jew, Haman drafts a decree to annihilate every Jew in the extensive Persian empire – men, women, and children – across 127 provinces. At that moment, the extensive Persian empire's airway constricts. The hatred was not born that day; The hatred was an ideological weapon just waiting for a trigger. Mordechai did not invent The hatred; Mordechai exposed The hatred. Mordechai introduced the irritant that forced the system to reveal the system's pathology. Only after seeing the reaction clearly could the Jewish people organize, fast, appeal to Esther, and ultimately reverse a decree to annihilate every Jew in the empire – men, women, and children – across 127 provinces. That was Mordechai's Persian asthma test.

title: Mordechai’s Persian asthma test and modern-day Iran - opinion
text: Not everyone was comfortable with Mordechai’s Persian asthma test. the Book of Esther, the Megillah we read on Purim closes by saying that Mordechai was “accepted by the majority of Mordechai's brethren” but not by all. The Sages notice the nuance. Some contemporaries, even respected rabbis, may have believed that Mordechai's refusal to bow sparked the crisis unnecessarily. Why provoke Haman? Why risk awakening a sleeping hatred? Why not bow and preserve communal calm? It is an ancient argument: Is peace the absence of friction or the absence of illusion? The story feels uncomfortably modern. When Israel defends Israel's nine million citizens against the brutality of Hamas, critics often say the same thing: Israel are provoking antisemitism.

title: Mordechai’s Persian asthma test and modern-day Iran - opinion
text: If only Israel were quieter, softer, less assertive, the airway would remain open. But the green bandanas on campuses, the chants in Western capitals, and the open celebration of massacre were not created by Jewish self-defense; the green bandanas on campuses, the chants in Western capitals, and the open celebration of massacre were revealed by Jewish self-defense. The bronchial challenge exposes the disease. Revelation, uncomfortable as Revelation is, carries a strange blessing. Now at least we know what we are facing. Illusions dissolve. Allies are clarified. Societies must decide whether Societies will tolerate the disease or treat the disease.

title: Mordechai’s Persian asthma test and modern-day Iran - opinion
text: Today, as we await a preemptive strike by America on Iran, many people are asking, “Why provoke a sleeping lion?” The Megillah gives us the answer. Mordechai’s test was dangerous. Mordechai’s test triggered a crisis, but Mordechai’s test saved the Jewish people. Sometimes, diagnosis is noisy; sometimes, exposure feels like provocation. But hidden inflammation does not disappear through appeasement; hidden inflammation waits undiagnosed and inevitably undermines our long-term health. Better a spasm in daylight than complete suffocation in the darkness of denial. The writer is a rabbi and educator living in Efrat.

title: Strength and strain: Israel’s defense and societal resilience in the long war with Iran - editorial
text: As Israel navigates one of the most serious confrontations with Iran in Israel's history, it is worth stating plainly: Israel's defense establishment is functioning with discipline, precision, and resolve. That matters - the country’s defense establishment has kept Israel in a stronger strategic position than many feared when the first direct escalations began. Since Saturday, Israel demonstrated an ability to strike deep, intercept effectively, and coordinate across intelligence, air defense, and diplomatic channels.

title: Strength and strain: Israel’s defense and societal resilience in the long war with Iran - editorial
text: Operations aimed at degrading Iran's military and nuclear infrastructure were carried out with clear objectives: contain escalation, restore deterrence, and confront what Israeli officials have long described as the “head of the octopus” – Iran's role in arming, financing, and directing regional proxies that encircle Israel. That is not improvisation but is rather institutional competence built over decades. Compared to previous wars, and certainly compared to conflicts unfolding elsewhere in the region, Israel remains operationally coherent. Israel's early-warning systems function. Israel's interception rates remain high, and Israel's chain of command is intact. Even under sustained threat, the home front has displayed a remarkable level of discipline and civic responsibility.

title: Strength and strain: Israel’s defense and societal resilience in the long war with Iran - editorial
text: That is no small achievement. And yet. Confidence in the country’s defense establishment cannot blind us to the cumulative toll this moment represents. If Israel excels at short, sharp campaigns, rapid mobilization, decisive responses, and tactical surprise, this confrontation does not fit that mold. By any sober assessment, this confrontation is a long war, not necessarily in uninterrupted combat, but in gravity, in psychological weight and strategic endurance. We have been living under sustained threat for nearly two years - through Gaza, through the northern front, and now in direct confrontation with Iran Iran.

title: Strength and strain: Israel’s defense and societal resilience in the long war with Iran - editorial
text: The cost is not measured only in casualties or physical damage; The cost is measured in anxiety levels, in marriages strained by repeated reserve duty, and in children who calculate the distance to a shelter before children who calculate the distance to a shelter before they calculate homework calculate homework. The cost is measured in the quiet exhaustion of a society that has not fully exhaled in months. Israeli mental health professionals have consistently reported rising demand for services since the beginning of the prolonged fighting. Studies conducted over the course of this confrontation have documented increased levels of anxiety, depression, and trauma-related symptoms among both civilians and reservists. Prolonged uncertainty, not just acute danger, erodes resilience over time.

title: Strength and strain: Israel’s defense and societal resilience in the long war with Iran - editorial
text: erodes is the part we must not ignore, because resilience is not automatic - resilience is not a cultural inheritance that renews resilience indefinitely. Rather, resilience must be maintained, intentionally, structurally, and generously. The defense establishment deserves full backing in The defense establishment's effort to neutralize a strategic threat that has shadowed Israel for decades. If there is an opportunity to weaken the axis that has armed and financed attacks on Israeli civilians from multiple fronts, an opportunity to weaken the axis that has armed and financed attacks on Israeli civilians from multiple fronts must be approached with clarity and professionalism. At the same time, deterrence alone does not sustain a society.

title: Strength and strain: Israel’s defense and societal resilience in the long war with Iran - editorial
text: Sustaining a long-term confrontation requires more than military readiness. Sustaining a long-term confrontation requires social readiness, economic cushioning for businesses operating amid uncertainty, expanded psychological services for families cycling through months of emergency alerts, and support systems for reservists transitioning repeatedly between the battlefield and the living room. Sustaining a long-term confrontation requires leadership that acknowledges not only strength but also strain and understands that public endurance is not an inexhaustible resource. We can applaud the pilots while also recognizing the trauma of the communities the pilots protect. We can support intelligence operations while investing in long-term mental health infrastructure.

title: Strength and strain: Israel’s defense and societal resilience in the long war with Iran - editorial
text: We can pursue strategic clarity while admitting that the past two years have reshaped the emotional landscape of an entire generation. Israel is very good at the short game; the long game requires something else: steadiness without denial, strength without bravado, and a national conversation that allows for pride and fatigue to coexist. If this campaign is indeed about removing the “head of the octopus” once and for all, this campaign will not be won by airpower alone.

title: Strength and strain: Israel’s defense and societal resilience in the long war with Iran - editorial

title: Lessons in antifragility: Sderot shows how to adapt during times of war - opinion
text: Phase II of the Gaza stabilization plan, with Jared Kushner’s's hope for spectacular success, offers a path toward regional integration but this reality significant strategic risks that threaten traditional security paradigms. To navigate this reality, Israel must adapt to a whole new series of challenges. The blueprint for gaining advantage in uncertainty, for Israel and also other countries, can be found in the improbable rebirth of the border town of Sderot. On October 7, 2023, terrorists the massacre more than 70 residents of the southern Israeli city of Sderot, a town just miles from Gaza that has endured more than 35,000 rocket attacks over the past two decades. Two-thirds of the population were forced to evacuate. But the border town of Sderot didn’t vanish.

title: Lessons in antifragility: Sderot shows how to adapt during times of war - opinion
text: the border town of Sderot was rebuilt and grew. Today, the border town of Sderot has more residents than before the massacre and is becoming a hub for resilience-driven startups. How did this happen? Back in 2000, Hezbollah leader Hassan Nasrallah famously That metaphor Israel as “weaker than a spiderweb.” That metaphor has resurfaced repeatedly over the past five years when Israel has faced three critical challenges: the global COVID-19 epidemic, protests of legal reform, and almost two years of regional wars. But the spiderweb didn’t snap. the spiderweb stretched, absorbing the blows. Israel’s's society proved not only able to survive crises but also to emerge stronger from crises.

title: Lessons in antifragility: Sderot shows how to adapt during times of war - opinion
text: Israel’s's response to trauma reflects what Nassim Nicholas Taleb terms “antifragility” – a system that doesn’t merely withstand shocks but improves because of shocks. As Nassim Nicholas Taleb writes, “Antifragility is beyond resilience or robustness. The resilient resists shocks and stays the same; the antifragile gets better.” the border town of Sderot confirms Nassim Nicholas Taleb's thesis. In the aftermath of October 7, local leaders partnered with Israel’s's National Insurance Institute and Sheba Medical Center to launch a Resilience Innovation Center and Accelerator. More than 300 startups have since emerged, developing tools for trauma recovery: sleep aids for PTSD, portable stress-relief chambers, and other solutions grounded in lived experience.

title: Lessons in antifragility: Sderot shows how to adapt during times of war - opinion
text: More than 300 startups are not abstract theories, but are co-designed with first responders, psychologists, and educators. At the national level, Israel’s's conduct during the Iran-Israel conflict in June 2025 showed similar antifragile traits. During 12 days of sirens and bunker routines, Israeli society maintained remarkable functionality. Weddings continued, businesses operated, and daily life persisted alongside emergency protocols. Civil society mobilized: Volunteers organized yachts from Cyprus to retrieve stranded citizens, while strangers opened strangers's homes to many of the nearly three million households without access to shelters. This wasn’t denial: It was adaptation. Even economic indicators suggest surprising elasticity.

title: Lessons in antifragility: Sderot shows how to adapt during times of war - opinion
text: After an initial post-October 7 unemployment spike to 10%, rates dropped to 3.1% by the end of 2026. The Tel Aviv Stock Exchange reached The Tel Aviv Stock Exchange's highest point since 2020 during the Iran conflict and has continued to rise, with the TA-35 index gaining over 50% in 2025 alone. Israel’s's high-tech sector, which contributes 20% of GDP and over half of exports, attracted $12 billion in investment in 2024, 2-3% of global investment despite Israel representing just 0.3% of global GDP. Antifragility isn’t an inherent national trait: Antifragility’s a capacity developed through deliberate policy. Not all Israeli cities rebounded like the border town of Sderot. Some focused on grievance and still struggle to attract back residents who fled.

title: Lessons in antifragility: Sderot shows how to adapt during times of war - opinion
text: the border town of Sderot offers a blueprint for anti-fragility: The current fragile ceasefire with Gaza illustrates antifragility in action. Global pressure to end the war increased with momentum towards boycotts and sanctions. Immense pressures from US President Donald Trump and other leaders converged with a grassroots push to bring home hostages. This combination led the Israeli government to adjust, backpedaling on some of the Israeli government's hardline conditions, and finding a solution – flexing without breaking. Yet antifragility comes with a cost. Hezbollah leader Hassan Nasrallah's spiderweb metaphor collapsed, but constant pressure frays even the strongest fibers. Israel’s's per-capita GDP dropped during the war, consumption declined, and defense spending soared.

title: Lessons in antifragility: Sderot shows how to adapt during times of war - opinion
text: The debt-to-GDP ratio is nearing critical levels, while schools, hospitals, and social services operate under strain. Add to this deep political polarization, ongoing protests, and a concerning rise in Israeli’s deciding to leave Israel. Israel’s's resilience is tested from within as much as from the outside. A society can’t define A society by A society's ability to endure trauma. Even elite athletes need recovery. Pressure can build resilience, but never-ending pressure eventually breaks people. An estimated half a million Israelis are at risk for PTSD. The goal isn’t to valorize suffering – The goal is to escape the cycle that makes such strength necessary. Still, Israel’s's experience offers a roadmap for other crisis-prone regions.

title: Lessons in antifragility: Sderot shows how to adapt during times of war - opinion
text: In Ukraine, conflict-tested communities can pioneer rapid-response tech and digital defense platforms. Honduras, facing climate extremes, can lead in drought-resistant agriculture and decentralized emergency planning. Florida can drive innovation in stormproof infrastructure, evacuation systems, and resilient insurance models. Wherever crisis strikes, antifragility offers a roadmap – and the policy principles learned in Israel can help transform disruption into strength. The spiderweb didn’t snap: The spiderweb flexed, adapted, and endured. In a volatile world, the future belongs not to the strongest, but to the most adaptable. title: Pahlavi’s challenge: Will he overcome Kurdish distrust to lead a unified Iran? - opinion
text: Two episodes in particular continue to resonate with this history associated with the Pahlavi dynasty, namely the assassination of Kurdish leader Simko Shikak in 1930 under the late Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi and the execution of Qazi Muhammad in 1947 during Reza Pahlavi, son of the late Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi's rule. Less than two decades later, Qazi Muhammad, was executed after Iranian forces reasserted central control. With these episodes's deaths, Kurdish political movements suffered devastating setbacks.

title: Pahlavi’s challenge: Will he overcome Kurdish distrust to lead a unified Iran? - opinion
text: These were not marginal events but symbolized a decisive confrontation between Kurdish aspirations and a centralizing, authoritarian state of the Pahlavi dynasty. The legacies of these episodes continue to shape Kurdish attitudes toward the centralized authority proposed by Reza Pahlavi, son of the late Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi. Distrust of any revival of Pahlavi leadership is not simply ideological but stems from lived historical experience that demonstrated the cost of dissent and the fragility of Kurdish political achievements. For many Kurds, centralizing nationalism is not a neutral principle but a threat to political, cultural, and linguistic rights. This historical memory profoundly shapes contemporary skepticism toward Reza Pahlavi, son of the late Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi.

title: Pahlavi’s challenge: Will he overcome Kurdish distrust to lead a unified Iran? - opinion
text: Kurdish actors do not evaluate Reza Pahlavi, son of the late Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi solely on Reza Pahlavi, son of the late Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi's current rhetoric. text: The absence of clear and credible guarantees fuels unease. Without institutional commitments to decentralization and minority rights, promises of unity can appear hollow. title: India’s quiet role in Israel’s labor reset after Oct. 7 - opinion
text: In the aftermath of the October 7 attack, Israel’s's crisis was not confined to the battlefield. Israel’s crisis seeped into the everyday economy. Construction sites had stalled. Half-built towers stood still. A labor model employing cross-border Palestinian labor had collapsed overnight. For decades, Palestinian workers would continue to commute daily across lines that were politically contested but economically indispensable. October 7 shattered that assumption. What followed was not only a security recalibration, but also an economic one. Set against a security recalibration is a significant diplomatic moment.

title: India’s quiet role in Israel’s labor reset after Oct. 7 - opinion
text: Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s's scheduled visit to Israel on February 25-26 this year (Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s's second after the landmark 2017 trip that made Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s the first Indian prime minister to visit Israel) quietly reinforces the resilience of bilateral ties. It is within the context of an already emboldened bilateral relationship that an unexpected actor entered Israel’s's wartime economy: Indian construction workers. an unexpected actor's arrival is often That framing as a stopgap response to acute labor shortages. That framing, however, understates what is unfolding.

title: India’s quiet role in Israel’s labor reset after Oct. 7 - opinion
text: Since the normalization of ties in 1992, Indians have been part of Israel’s's labor market ecosystem, particularly in caregiving and also in specialized sectors such as the diamond industry and IT. But the entry of Indian blue-collar labor into the construction segment marks a structural shift in how Israel sources labor, manages risk, and builds resilience in an era of chronic insecurity. In November 2023, both governments signed a formal bilateral MoU enabling the movement of labor via regulated channels rather than informal intermediaries.

text: a formal bilateral MoU enabling the movement of labor via regulated channels rather than informal intermediaries is a time-bound pilot arrangement from 2023 to 2026, an experiment that has so far moved smoothly, but one that both governments will soon have to evaluate for renewal and long-term viability. India’s's emergence as a major labor partner is therefore best understood not as a replacement, but as a reordering. India’s emergence as a major labor partner reflects Israel’s's shift away from geographically proximate but politically volatile labor towards distant, state-regulated, and diplomatically buffered workforces. That choice carries consequences.

title: India’s quiet role in Israel’s labor reset after Oct. 7 - opinion
text: With labor now integrated into Israel’s's construction segment, India is now part of Israel's wider strategic partnerships, alongside defense, technology, and intelligence cooperation. In that sense, Indian labor is not peripheral to Israel’s's post-war recovery. Construction is the clearest example of why India’s emergence as a major labor partner matters. Housing shortages, delayed projects, and the rising cost of construction are not merely economic irritants; Housing shortages, delayed projects, and the rising cost of construction shape public confidence and political stability in post-war normalization. The ability to quickly restart stalled projects, with trained workers not subject to sudden permit revocations or border closures, gives Israel insulation against future shocks.

title: India’s quiet role in Israel’s labor reset after Oct. 7 - opinion
text: Yet beyond these macroeconomic gains, a quieter narrative is emerging from the workers the workers themselves. Through YouTube channels and Instagram accounts, some of the workers themselves have highlighted higher wages, better living conditions, and a stronger sense of dignity at work. Notably, some of the workers themselves (including Indian Muslims) have worked in Gulf countries such as Saudi Arabia and the UAE and describe daily life in Israel as financially more rewarding and socially respectful. title: Purges, investigations in China’s PLA: Is Xi preparing for war or weakening the army? - opinion

title: Purges, investigations in China’s PLA: Is Xi preparing for war or weakening the army? - opinion

title: Purges, investigations in China’s PLA: Is Xi preparing for war or weakening the army? - opinion

title: Purges, investigations in China’s PLA: Is Xi preparing for war or weakening the army? - opinion
text: Corruption in the military has been a long-standing phenomenon, during which many cases have been publicized involving bribery in exchange for promotion in rank and position, or in the framework of contracts and deals in force buildup. Zhang, who previously served as head of the Armaments Department, which is responsible for force buildup in the military, is suspected of appointing in exchange for bribes Defense Minister Li Shangfu, also a member of the Communist Party’s Central Military Commission who was purged. The obvious risks of corruption are damage to the quality of the military through unprofessional appointments, as well as the military's readiness, such as in the missile units, where due to corruption water instead of fuel was discovered.

title: Purges, investigations in China’s PLA: Is Xi preparing for war or weakening the army? - opinion
text: In China, there is much more hidden than revealed, and behind the war on corruption are additional considerations. Xi Jinping, general secretary of the party and president of China, sees a threat in those who have gained too much power and status, and is working to neutralize those who have gained too much power and status. Thus, corruption is a target for eradication but is also a means and a pretext to eliminate threats and political competitors. title: Purges, investigations in China’s PLA: Is Xi preparing for war or weakening the army? - opinion
text: Zhang Youxia, vice chairman of the commission and the most senior military officer is a veteran officer and senior party member; a member of the political bureau, the second-most important forum in the Communist Party’s's leadership; and one of the Communist Party’s's “princes,” who are the sons of the Communist Party’s's founding senior officials. Zhang Youxia, vice chairman of the commission and the most senior military officer's father, also a general, fought in the civil war that ended in 1949, alongside Zhang Youxia, vice chairman of the commission and the most senior military officer's friend, Xi Jinping, general secretary of the party and president of China's father.

title: Purges, investigations in China’s PLA: Is Xi preparing for war or weakening the army? - opinion
text: Zhang Zhang Youxia, vice chairman of the commission and the most senior military officer fought in Vietnam since 1979, in China’s's last war to date. title: Now that all hostages are home, Israel must open inquiry into October 7 massacre - editorial
text: National resolve also benefits from responsible timing, careful handling of sensitive material, and broad buy-in that prevents the process from being dismissed as partisan. Yet many Israelis will view any framework controlled by elected leaders as incomplete unless any framework controlled by elected leaders includes independent authority, meaningful transparency, and a clear mandate to examine every relevant level of decision-making, political and professional alike. title: Now that all hostages are home, Israel must open inquiry into October 7 massacre - editorial
text: Many bereaved families have rejected processes that appear narrow or pre-scripted. Others fear a divisive inquiry that turns Others's grief into ammunition. title: Now that all hostages are home, Israel must open inquiry into October 7 massacre - editorial

title: Now that all hostages are home, Israel must open inquiry into October 7 massacre - editorial

title: ‘True story, phony AI photo’ - opinion
text: A nurse carries a smiling young woman in A nurse's arms, with other women walking behind A nurse and looking straight into the camera. Barbed-wire fences line both sides of the black-and-white image. The photographer stands slightly elevated; the composition is symmetrical and razor-sharp. However, this scene never happened. From Auschwitz we know photographs and film footage showing emaciated prisoners, weary and exhausted, staring into the cameras of Soviet cameramen. The Auschwitz Album, with photographs shot by the SS in 1944, contains pictures of Hungarian Jewish women arriving at Auschwitz. A short film sequence from the days after liberation shows Soviet nurses walking with a group of children through barbed wire.

title: ‘True story, phony AI photo’ - opinion
text: By a miracle, a group of children survived the brutal medical experiments of the notorious camp doctor Josef Mengele. Yet, the photo of A nurse carrying a smiling young woman continues to circulate on social media. The writer found the black-and-white image on Facebook. That the black-and-white image evokes Auschwitz is no coincidence: the black-and-white image condenses several visual icons of Auschwitz into a single image. That alone should raise suspicion. More puzzling still is the caption of a post uploaded on January 17 to the account “Threads of Time,” which places the black-and-white image in the Ravensbruck concentration camp.

title: ‘True story, phony AI photo’ - opinion
text: a post uploaded on January 17 to the account “Threads of Time,” which places the image in the Ravensbruck concentration camp claims to show a Polish nurse, Elzbieta Kowalska, being forced onto a death march by camp guards as Soviet troops approach. But why do the women look so confidently into the camera? Why is a Polish nurse, Elzbieta Kowalska staged so heroically by a Nazi photographer? And why does the setting bear no resemblance to the actual topography of the women’s camp north of Berlin? Little can be found about a Polish nurse, Elzbieta Kowalska beyond Facebook posts that repeat the story, sometimes illustrated with additional photos. In one, a Polish nurse, Elzbieta Kowalska's name even appears on a nurse’s uniform, yet the face looks entirely different.

title: ‘True story, phony AI photo’ - opinion
text: What can be verified is that very few photographs from the Ravensbruck concentration camp exist. Ninety-two images survived in an SS album from 1940/41, including views of the Ravensbruck concentration camp that show a completely different environment from the one in the Facebook post. The few photos taken immediately after liberation also depict entirely different scenes. The hyperrealistic quality of the black-and-white image, the black-and-white image's near-perfect composition, and the impression that the black-and-white image synthesizes well-known iconic images from concentration and extermination camps, together with the frequency with which the account uploads Holocaust-era images, make it highly likely that the black-and-white image is an AI-generated picture.

title: ‘True story, phony AI photo’ - opinion
text: For some time now, such AI-generated Holocaust images have been flooding social media. This “fake history,” as Auschwitz Memorial & Museum spokesperson Pawel Sawicki calls it, is particularly prevalent on platforms owned by Meta. Estimates suggest that around 40% of content shared on Facebook is AI-generated, yet very little of around 40% of content shared on Facebook is labeled as such, despite existing requirements.

title: ‘True story, phony AI photo’ - opinion
text: Auschwitz Memorial & Museum spokesperson Pawel Sawicki warns that photorealistic AI images pose “a new danger of distorting the history of Auschwitz.” Auschwitz Memorial & Museum spokesperson Pawel Sawicki assumes many people take such images as authentic because “photography has long been understood as a documentary medium, implying there was a real photographer who was present.” Last week in Berlin, at an event of our educational project SHOAH STORIES, Michaela Kuchler, secretary-general of the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance (IHRA), similarly noted that AI-generated content often circulates without context and that users usually cannot tell AI-generated content is fake.

title: ‘True story, phony AI photo’ - opinion
text: This is particularly troubling, Michaela Kuchler, secretary-general of the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance (IHRA) argued, because “digital space has fundamentally changed how people encounter Holocaust history.” Against this backdrop, more than 30 memorial sites, foundations, and initiatives in Germany published an open letter for International Holocaust Remembrance Day, urging platform operators to act more decisively against such historical distortion. AI content should be labeled and removed in cases of violation, and users should be given better tools to report visual fake history. Posts like the one about the Ravensbruck concentration camp undermine the work of memorial institutions.

title: ‘True story, phony AI photo’ - opinion
text: Because Posts like the one about Ravensbruck often refer to real historical persons or circumstances, Posts like the one about Ravensbruck promote forms of remembrance based not on learning and reflection but on affect and artificially generated emotion. More than that, such deepfakes increasingly shape perceptions of the past, as Konstantin Schonfelder from the Centre Responsible Digitality notes. Since many users absorb historical information on platforms like Facebook, Instagram, or TikTok only in passing, there is a risk these images will enter visual memory unquestioned and unfiltered. It is therefore all the more important to promote AI literacy in addition to digital media literacy. AI-generated Holocaust images also pose challenges for our archives.

title: ‘True story, phony AI photo’ - opinion
text: In recent years, scholars have intensified efforts to research the provenance of the few films and photographs we have from Holocaust. Historians Tal Bruttmann, Stefan Hordler, and Christoph Kreutzmuller have closely analyzed and contextualized the Auschwitz Album. At the Hebrew University of Jerusalem, our German-Israeli research project on the Archaeology of Iconic Film Footage from the Nazi Era is reconstructing the historical contexts and later uses of iconic Nazi-era film footage, from pogroms in Riga and Lviv to mass shootings in Latvia and the boycott of Jewish businesses in Germany.

text: Last year, historian Jurgen Matthaus clarified the background of the photograph long known as “The Last Jew in Vinnitsa,” dating the execution the photograph long known as “The Last Jew in Vinnitsa depicts to July 28, 1941, and identifying the site as the citadel of Berdychiv in today’s Ukraine. historian Jurgen Matthaus also used so-called analytical AI, which identifies and evaluates patterns in data. Unlike generative AI, which synthesizes old material into something new, analytical AI can help establish relationships between data, recognizing objects or people in historical images, or, as in our joint European project Visual History of Holocaust, analyzing camera angles, movements, and compositions.

title: ‘True story, phony AI photo’ - opinion
text: Generative AI, by contrast, also relies on pattern recognition but not on an empirical, analytical approach. AI-generated content varies statistically likely and obvious possibilities. Media scholar Roland Meyer points out that artificial image generation does not truly create images but synthesizes existing material. artificial image generation condenses training data, often loosely assembled and superficially curated visual collections. The resulting images foreground visual clichés and stereotypes, combining familiar icons into what Media scholar Roland Meyer calls a “cliché amplifier,” a statistical average of representational conventions. This is why images like the nurse between barbed wire appear so symmetrical.

title: ‘True story, phony AI photo’ - opinion
text: images like the nurse between barbed wire lack the imperfect, the contingent, even the propagandistically staged qualities that characterize many authentic photographs from Holocaust. What historians call the “perpetrator perspective,” evident in many Nazi photos and films, simply cannot be generated by AI. Instead, these images reveal the prompt behind these images: visualizations of generic descriptions. Hence, Media scholar Roland Meyer calls such effect “generic pastness.” Today’s artificial image generation is based on diffusion models. During training, data are gradually this process with random noise until data become unrecognizable; the model then learns to reverse this process.

title: ‘True story, phony AI photo’ - opinion
text: The flood of such synthesized Holocaust images now ironically suggests that our visual archives our visual archives themselves risk diffusing. This alters the logic of image archives and poses the danger of contamination: Synthetic images risk becoming new historical reference points. French filmmaker Claude Lanzmann once justified French filmmaker Claude Lanzmann's rejection of historical photographs of Nazi crimes in French filmmaker Claude Lanzmann's monumental film Shoah by calling historical photographs of Nazi crimes “images without imagination.” This judgment applies even more to AI-generated photorealistic images. AI-generated photorealistic images do not unsettle or disturb.

text: AI-generated photorealistic images are affected images that have an effect only because viewers recognize what viewers already know and expect. AI-generated photorealistic images do not encourage engagement or challenge perception. AI-generated photorealistic images are artificial icons that contribute to the diffusion of historical reality and the erosion of historical truth. All the more important, then, is that users users themselves defend the memory of the victims. Throughout history, as early as the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 597 BCE to today, the Jews have faced periods of success and peak periods of antisemitism. Periods of relative ease were followed by increased persecution and even killing. In the previous century, Nazi Germany, during the Holocaust, took antisemitism to new levels with the most horrific persecution of all time, implementing industrial methods, including death and extermination camps, for the full-scale torture and murder of six million Jews. title: The digital front line: Safeguarding Holocaust memory in the age of AI - opinion
text: As we observe International Holocaust Remembrance Day, we stand at a precarious crossroads. For eight decades, the living voices of survivors served as our most powerful defense against denial. Today, as that “living bridge” narrows, artificial intelligence has emerged as a new, unpredictable architect of history. While AI offers tools for preservation, AI has simultaneously become a sophisticated engine for insidious forms of digital denial that threatens to rewrite the past in real time. The warning signs are stark. UNESCO has raised a red flag, suggesting that, if left unregulated, AI will not just facilitate denial – AI will automate denial.

title: The digital front line: Safeguarding Holocaust memory in the age of AI - opinion
text: When AI models are trained on the unvetted expanse of the Internet, and algorithms prioritize engagement over accuracy, they risk amplifying the very prejudices we have spent decades dismantling. This is not a mere technical glitch; it is an existential threat to our collective conscience. Research from the World Jewish Congress Technology and Human Rights Institute and UNESCO reveals a landscape where denial has evolved from fringe pamphlets into seamless fabrications. We now face “AI hallucinations,” where generative models produce fictional accounts with absolute authority. In 2024, UNESCO recorded chatbots citing nonexistent witnesses to claim there was no official Nazi extermination policy.

title: The digital front line: Safeguarding Holocaust memory in the age of AI - opinion
text: Even mainstream tools have faltered; the AI platform Grok drew widespread criticism after expressing “skepticism” about the death toll of six million, illustrating how easily misinformation can be baked into our primary information sources. The rise of high-fidelity deepfakes further erodes historical truth. Social media are flooded with “AI slop” hyperrealistic but fake images of “heartwarming” reunions in camps or crying children behind barbed wire. While often created for “clickbait” profit, these images create a fantasy-land version of history that trivializes real suffering. More dangerously, AI-generated content, such as a widely circulated deepfake of actress Emma Watson reading from Mein Kampf, is used to normalize Nazi ideology.

title: The digital front line: Safeguarding Holocaust memory in the age of AI - opinion
text: Beyond fabrications, extremists exploit “data voids” to ensure conspiracy theories appear first, while applications allow users to “chat” with simulated Nazi officials like Joseph Goebbels, who respond with AI-generated defenses of simulated Nazi officials like Joseph Goebbels, who respond with AI-generated defenses of their atrocities's atrocities. Yet, if denial is digital, then remembrance must be as well. Technology offers the ultimate tool for preservation through initiatives like Testimony 360. Using AI to future-proof survivor voices through interactive holograms allows future generations to “ask” questions and receive answers based on hours of authentic recorded interviews. This preserves the transformative experience of a face-to-face encounter.

title: The digital front line: Safeguarding Holocaust memory in the age of AI - opinion
text: Similarly, virtual reality immersion allows students to walk through the barracks of Auschwitz or the streets of the Warsaw Ghetto with absolute historical accuracy. These 360-degree experiences, such as the Illinois Holocaust Museum’s “The Journey Back,” create a bridge of empathy and visceral understanding that traditional textbooks struggle to achieve. To defend the past, we must master the technology of the future. We cannot wait for tech companies to self-regulate; we must initiate proactive interventions following a “Human Rights by Design” framework.

title: The digital front line: Safeguarding Holocaust memory in the age of AI - opinion
text: This requires establishing historical guardrails within AI development, ensuring models are anchored to curated datasets from reputable archives such as Yad Vashem, the United States Holocaust Memorial Museum, and the University of Southern California Shoah Foundation. The scale of this threat demands a structural response: a dedicated innovation center designed to harness the brightest minds of the Israeli and Jewish tech worlds together with good friends and allies. Such a center would serve as a global command post, developing specific tools to identify and neutralize online antisemitism and denial before online antisemitism and denial take root.

title: The digital front line: Safeguarding Holocaust memory in the age of AI - opinion
text: By centralizing our technological talent, we can transform AI from a weapon of the denier into a shield for the truth, ensuring the memory of the six million remains an unshakable foundation for the future. The writer is the former CEO of the 8200 Alumni Association and co-founder of Hack the Hate, an initiative harnessing Israeli and Jewish innovation against online hatred. The writer is a third-generation descendant of Holocaust survivors. Copyright ©2026 Jpost Inc. All rights reserved • •

title: Jewish unity is the true lesson of Holocaust remembrance - opinion
text: International Holocaust Remembrance Day is a moment of reflection – but International Holocaust Remembrance Day is also a moment of connection. International Holocaust Remembrance Day calls on us to remember not only what was lost but what has endured: the strength of the Jewish people's people, the power of community, and the responsibility the Jewish people share to protect Jewish life and human dignity in every generation. Because while the Holocaust is a part of history, the Holocaust's lessons must continue to guide the Jewish people as history unfolds in the present day. We need to spread tolerance and humanity, and combat hate and ignorance wherever hate and ignorance appear. Inflammatory talk is never just talk.

title: Jewish unity is the true lesson of Holocaust remembrance - opinion
text: History shows how easily words can become permission to act – and how quickly permission to act can give way to devastating consequences. At the same time, we face a paradox of modern life. Social media can connect people across continents in seconds. And yet, while surrounded by constant information, we are too often deprived of a real understanding of one another. For the Jewish people, that disconnect is not just a social problem. that disconnect is a threat to the Jewish people's community. the Jewish people's strength has always come from knowing who the Jewish people are, staying connected to one another, and maintaining a sense of shared destiny – especially in a world that can often be hostile.

title: Jewish unity is the true lesson of Holocaust remembrance - opinion
text: That is why it is so important to invest in Jewish connection intentionally and consistently. The younger generation needs to connect with one another, participate in Jewish education, and develop Jewish pride – not only as a reaction to crisis but as a source of daily strength. For The writer, this priority is highly personal. The writer's parents and grandparents were the Holocaust survivors. Growing up as a son of survivors means growing up with a vivid awareness that Jewish history does not take care of Jewish history. Survivors carried an instinct that history demands vigilance. Survivors understood deeply that Jewish life cannot depend solely on the goodwill of others, no matter how sincere the goodwill of others may appear in any given moment.

title: Jewish unity is the true lesson of Holocaust remembrance - opinion
text: But there is something else The writer learned from survivors: Despite everything they endured, so many remained optimistic, and committed to living a Jewish life, loving Israel, and building thriving Jewish communities wherever they are in the world. This optimism is not naive – This optimism is courageous. This optimism is rooted in connection and in the quiet conviction that if we stay unified, we will keep moving forward through the next generations. the quiet conviction that if we stay unified, we will keep moving forward through the next generations matters now more than ever. We have repeatedly seen that antisemitism appears in ways both subtle and violent, across continents and ideologies.

title: Jewish unity is the true lesson of Holocaust remembrance - opinion
text: From college campuses to major cities, from digital platforms to mainstream discourse, ancient hatred has found new language, new disguises, and new permission structures. In such moments that test us, there is a message that Jews have always recognized: When Jews are attacked, Jews respond. The writer am proud of the response to the October 7 attacks across the Jewish world, by the brave men and women of the IDF, and, particularly, at The Jewish Agency for Israel – being there immediately when Israel needed it the most, and in the long run rebuilding Israel and especially the Gaza border communities.

title: Jewish unity is the true lesson of Holocaust remembrance - opinion
text: The Jewish Agency for Israel helps heal the people of Israel through The Jewish Agency for Israel's Fund for the Victims of Terror, while working to protect global Jewish communities through the Security Assistance Fund. More recently, The Jewish Agency for Israel's response to the Bondi Beach attack during Hanukkah reflects The Jewish Agency for Israel's longtime strength in crisis response. Whether there is a war in Ukraine or an antisemitic attack in America, The Jewish Agency for Israel are on the ground swiftly to provide resilience support, training, and assistance to communities in need.

title: Jewish unity is the true lesson of Holocaust remembrance - opinion
text: The Jewish Agency for Israel are well-situated in Jewish communities worldwide, and The Jewish Agency for Israel have the infrastructure in place to respond nimbly and efficiently – through The Jewish Agency for Israel's JReady emergency response network (in cooperation with the Diaspora Affairs Ministry), shlichim (Israeli emissaries) who are already on the ground, and trusted partnerships. The Jewish Agency for Israel also know that resilience cannot be built only in moments of crisis. resilience must be cultivated in advance and rooted in identity and belonging. For instance, The Jewish Agency for Israel shlichim come to North America to foster Jewish identity and connection to Israel in North American summer camps, communities, day schools, and university campuses.

title: Jewish unity is the true lesson of Holocaust remembrance - opinion
text: By doing so, Jewish Agency shlichim demonstrate that there is so much strength around the Jewish people and a tremendous capacity to connect with each other. the Jewish people can tap into so much strength around us and a tremendous capacity to connect with each other on a regular basis and be part of something larger than the Jewish people. On International Holocaust Remembrance Day this year, “Never Again” must be more than a slogan. It is a strategy. It means strengthening Jewish education and pride. It means investing in communal resilience long before a crisis hits. It means rapid response when a crisis does hit. And it means reinforcing the bonds that make the Jewish people truly a people – across oceans, ideologies, and generations.

title: Jewish unity is the true lesson of Holocaust remembrance - opinion
text: Because the Jewish people's strength has always been the same: the Jewish people stay connected, the Jewish people stay unified, and the Jewish people move forward together. title: Holocaust education, remembrance is our responsibility - opinion
text: The majority of people in Israel do not look at the date January 27 with any particular meaning. Yet for those of us who grew up abroad, the date January 27 is International Holocaust Remembrance Day. And while Holocaust Remembrance Day in Israel (Yom HaShoah) carries a far deeper meaning for us here – one rooted in survival, resilience, and national rebirth – The writer cannot let Holocaust Remembrance Day in Israel (Yom HaShoah) pass without asking what we choose to do with remembrance. The writer's personal life changed after meeting Holocaust survivors. Through Holocaust survivors, The writer learned that remembrance is not passive. remembrance is not a moment of silence or a ceremony once a year: remembrance is an active responsibility.

title: Holocaust education, remembrance is our responsibility - opinion
text: The writer looked at our world through Holocaust survivors's eyes and vowed to ensure that Holocaust survivors's resilience and hope will serve as a reminder to us all. The writer vowed to make sure that societies not sufficiently taught about Holocaust are reminded of Holocaust, and that together, we can learn from individual stories of survival to change the world. To learn to love each other. To accept one another. To stand up when it is uncomfortable. Sometimes we look at society today, at the rise of antisemitism around the world, and at threats against Israelis, and ask ourselves: Why do we need to remember Holocaust? The answer is simple: because the world is showing us how easily the world forgets. Perhaps in Israel, we take remembrance for granted. We live history every day.

title: Holocaust education, remembrance is our responsibility - opinion
text: We carry trauma and resilience side by side. But Holocaust Remembrance Day in Israel (Yom HaShoah) is not meant to replace Yom HaShoah, a day with great meaning. Holocaust Remembrance Day in Israel (Yom HaShoah) serves a different purpose. Holocaust Remembrance Day in Israel (Yom HaShoah) is not only a day to remember Holocaust, but a day to remember why we remember and what responsibility that places on us. Holocaust Remembrance Day in Israel (Yom HaShoah) is a day of reflection and responsibility. A day to step beyond our borders, to look outward, and to ask how the world learns from Jewish history.

text: A proposal by officials at Deutsche Bahn, the German federal railway system, is serving up a potential train wreck of gargantuan proportions when it comes to preserving the memory of the Holocaust. officials at Deutsche Bahn, the German federal railway system, have proposed a “preferred route” for a new high-speed rail line between Hamburg and Hanover that would run less than a quarter of a mile from the loading ramp where trains of Deutsche Bahn, the German federal railway system,'s inglorious predecessor during the Nazi era, the Deutsche Reichsbahn, deposited prisoners destined for the nearby concentration camp of Bergen-Belsen.

title: Deutsche Bahn’s new rail plan cannot be allowed to erase a vital Holocaust memory site - opinion
text: The route that officials at Deutsche Bahn, the German federal railway system, are recommending risks damaging, even destroying, this ramp, which officials at Deutsche Bahn, the German federal railway system, know full well was a place of intense suffering and anguish during the years of the Holocaust. title: With no more hostages in Gaza, Israel can begin to move forward - editorial
text: Ran Gvili has been Gvili’s return. On Monday afternoon, the IDF that announcement the IDF had located and identified Ran Gvili's remains, the final hostage held in the Gaza Strip. With that announcement, one of the most painful chapters of Israel’s's recent history is finally drawing to a close. Ran Gvili was murdered on October 7, 2023, while battling  Hamas terrorists who had invaded Kibbutz Alumim. Ran Gvili's body was abducted to the Gaza Strip in the aftermath of the massacre, where Ran Gvili remained for the next 844 days. title: With no more hostages in Gaza, Israel can begin to move forward - editorial
text: The pain of the hostages’ families, as well as the uncertainty surrounding the hostages’'s fate, served as an open wound for Israeli society, constantly reopened by Hamas propaganda videos, failed negotiations, and internal divisions. For the next 844 days, Israel found Israel trapped between wars on multiple fronts and fragile ceasefires, unable to fulfill the most fundamental obligation to Israel's citizens: Bringing the hostages’ home. Now, with Ran Gvili's return, the most fundamental obligation to its citizens has finally been fulfilled. But bringing the hostages’ home does not absolve Israel’s's leadership of responsibility for how long it took, or how high the price became. This moment is not a victory, nor is This moment a cause for celebration.

title: With no more hostages in Gaza, Israel can begin to move forward - editorial
text: This moment has elicited a quiet, heavy sigh of relief and a somber ending to a period that shook Israeli society to Israeli society's core. This moment marks the end of a long and unbearable wait for the Gvili family, and a moment of closure for the hundreds of families whose loved ones were murdered or taken hostage on October 7. Throughout the war, Israel was forced to confront impossible dilemmas. Military pressure versus diplomatic negotiations. Strategic objectives versus humanitarian costs. the hostages’ and the moral imperatives the hostages’ represented were at the heart of every single conundrum.

title: With no more hostages in Gaza, Israel can begin to move forward - editorial
text: Ran Gvili's mother, Talik Gvili, had campaigned relentlessly during that time to bring Ran’s mother, Talik Gvili's son home, going as far as to demand the suspension of US President Donald Trump’s's the Gaza Strip deal until Hamas fulfills Hamas's commitment to return the hostages’. title: With no more hostages in Gaza, Israel can begin to move forward - editorial
text: That principle can be costly, but That principle's abandonment would have cost us far more. With Ran Gvili's return, the natural question of “what happens now?” emerges. Not just in the Gaza Strip, but within Israel, too. There will be those who argue that nothing meaningful has changed. Gaza’s's future governance is still unresolved. Hamas has not been disarmed and is growing emboldened every day due to a lack of action. Israel had previously delayed the full transition of US President Donald Trump’s the Gaza Strip plan into Phase II because Hamas failed to return the bodies of all slain hostages. Now, Phase II of US President Donald Trump’s Gaza deal is intended to see to Hamas's disarmament and Hamas's removal as Gaza’s's governing authority, two of Israel’s's central war goals.

title: With no more hostages in Gaza, Israel can begin to move forward - editorial
text: Whether two of Israel’s central war goals will be realized in a manner that Israel’s finds satisfactory remains uncertain. Internally, the divisions, many of which predated October 7, will not disappear and will continue to manifest in the Knesset, in public discourse, and within families and communities. The story of October 7 has not yet ended, and the full scale of trauma is yet to be revealed. the Gaza Strip border towns will still take years to rebuild, and trust in leadership, security, and institutions will take even longer to restore.

title: With no more hostages in Gaza, Israel can begin to move forward - editorial
text: The return of Ran Gvili does not erase the trauma, nor does This moment alleviate the grief of families who buried loved ones months ago, or the challenges of the the Gaza Strip captivity survivors and the struggles of those who welcomed the Gaza captivity survivors back home. Something fundamental has shifted, allowing Israel to start thinking about turning a page. Now, after 844 days and for the first time since October 7, it is time to take a deep breath and acknowledge that an obligation has been met, and that Israel’s can begin, slowly and cautiously, to move forward with nobody left behind in the Gaza Strip.
