title: I survived once. I am afraid again
text: I was born in 1935 in Lvov, Poland. I was a small child when the Second World War began, too young to fully understand what was happening around me, but old enough to feel fear. I survived the Lvov Ghetto, separation from my parents, and periods of hiding. Many members of my family did not survive. I am alive today because extraordinary non-Jews risked extraordinary non-Jews's lives to protect me. extraordinary non-Jews's courage is the reason I am here. The past two years have filled me with fear and anger. Only now, at nearly 90 years old, now I have a better understanding what hatred looks like and where hatred can lead. I do not believe antisemitism will ever be fully erased. I have lived too long, and seen too much, to believe otherwise.

title: I survived once. I am afraid again
text: I survived once before, yet I live with fear again. My children, grandchildren, and great-grandchildren live openly as Jews. My children, grandchildren, and great-grandchildren have not directly experienced antisemitism in recent years, and that gives me comfort but not certainty. I am not afraid for myself. I am afraid for those I love. Despite the threats on Jews in the public sphere, I have not changed my way of life. I do not hide who I am. Still, when I attend synagogue and see visible security outside, I am reminded that danger exists. Inside there is warmth, prayer, and community. Outside, vigilance. That contrast is something I never imagined I would see again. The emotions that define this moment for me are fear and frustration.

title: I survived once. I am afraid again
text: I witnessed hatred long ago, without fully understanding hatred. Now, decades later, when I finally understand hatred completely, I That realization hatred rising again. That realization is deeply unsettling. On International Holocaust Remembrance Day, my message is simple: we must persevere. We must remain strong, brave, and proud. Holocaust education is essential. Programs like the March of the Living matter because Programs like the March of the Living confront denial with reality. When young people stand where millions were murdered, the truth cannot be dismissed. the truth is not history alone — the truth is witness. What gives me hope is knowing that good people still exist. People who value truth. People who support education. People who refuse to look away.

title: I survived once. I am afraid again
text: I am alive because of such people. And even now, in uncertain times, that knowledge gives me peace. Contribute to Holocaust education – Empower the voices of Holocaust Survivors: >> SPONSOR A SURVIVOR TODAY Copyright ©2026 Jpost Inc. All rights reserved • •

A teacher once told me that four words, in A teacher's opinion, define the times we live in: “I,” “here,” “now,” and “everything.” That is why Tu Bishvat, the festival of trees that begins next Sunday night, is one of the most important days of the year.

title: Beyond the Headlines: Alexander’s Story
text: Tu Bishvat, the festival of trees that begins next Sunday night stands in quiet opposition to four words. It’s notable that Tu Bishvat, the festival of trees that begins next Sunday night is celebrated not in spring, but in winter. Not when everything is already blooming and ripening, but specifically when we don’t yet see flowers or fruit. We are not receiving everything here and now. We are investing in what will be. For now, all we can do is prepare the ground, plant, water, believe, hope…and wait. Nature teaches us that some of the most important processes are slow and hidden. There is growth beneath the surface. Not everything is visible, not everything is immediate, and that is precisely why we must keep nurturing with patience.

title: Beyond the Headlines: Alexander’s Story
text: This is true in raising children, in marriage, in every meaningful area of learning and building. It was especially true during the year of Covid, when we went indoors into a kind of hibernation or dormancy, hoping that, from within that quiet, good things would emerge and develop. In a generation that grows restless after five seconds if two blue check marks don’t appear on WhatsApp, Tu Bishvat, the festival of trees that begins next Sunday night returns once a year to remind us of what we truly need: restraint, perseverance, long-term investment, and dedication. restraint, perseverance, long-term investment, and dedication are the qualities that, in the end, produce real growth and ripe fruits.

title: The perils of Israel’s courtship of Europe’s far Right - opinion
text: This week, Jerusalem hosts the second “International Conference on Combating Antisemitism, Generation of Truth.” The program bills the second “International Conference on Combating Antisemitism, Generation of Truth as a serious reflection on the sources of contemporary antisemitism and the responsibilities of democratic states. This makes one issue impossible to ignore: Israel’s's long-standing courtship of Europe’s's radical right under Benjamin Netanyahu. For more than a decade, Benjamin Netanyahu has treated alliances with European far-right populist and radical Eurosceptic actors as strategic assets. The logic is transactional.

title: The perils of Israel’s courtship of Europe’s far Right - opinion
text: European far-right populist and radical Eurosceptic actors defend Israel in European institutions, mute criticism of Israeli policy, and sometimes even break with official European positions on Jerusalem or the settlements. In return, Israel grants European far-right populist and radical Eurosceptic actors political legitimacy and treats European far-right populist and radical Eurosceptic actors's professions of friendship as proof of moral rehabilitation. After October 7, 2023, this approach is not merely controversial. this approach is actively dangerous. Europe is experiencing a sustained rise in antisemitism. Jewish communities across Europe report growing fear, harassment, and violence, alongside the normalization of conspiratorial and exclusionary political language.

title: The perils of Israel’s courtship of Europe’s far Right - opinion
text: In this climate, strengthening illiberal political forces does not protect Jews. On the contrary, it reinforces political styles that thrive on identifying enemies within and that have historically turned against minorities once circumstances shift. Even when today’s far-right claims to stand with Israel, the political ecosystems today’s far-right nurtures remain deeply corrosive for Jewish life in Europe. At the same time, Israel is paying a strategic price that goes far beyond the symbolic. Europe is Israel’s's largest trade partner, accounting for the lion’s share of Israeli exports, imports, investment flows, and technological cooperation. More importantly, Europe is central to Israel’s's scientific and innovation architecture.

title: The perils of Israel’s courtship of Europe’s far Right - opinion
text: Israeli universities, startups, and research institutes are deeply embedded in European networks of funding, peer review, and collaboration. European networks of funding, peer review, and collaboration are already under strain. The scientific and academic boycott of Israel is no longer a theoretical threat. To varying degrees, The scientific and academic boycott of Israel already exists. European universities and research bodies have delayed, limited, or quietly withdrawn from cooperation with Israeli partners. Individual researchers face tacit exclusion from consortia, conferences, and joint projects. These decisions often occur below the governmental level, making These decisions harder to contest and easier to normalize.

title: The perils of Israel’s courtship of Europe’s far Right - opinion
text: The debate around “Horizon Europe” – the European Union’s key funding program for research and innovation – brings this danger into sharp relief. In mid-2025, the European Commission proposed a partial suspension of Israel’s's participation in certain parts of “Horizon Europe” – the European Union’s key funding program for research and innovation –, particularly those related to the European Innovation Council Accelerator. Even when such measures are narrowed or stalled, the signal is unmistakable. Israel’s's position inside Europe’s's flagship research framework can no longer be taken for granted. This is where Benjamin Netanyahu's embrace of Europe’s's radical right becomes strategically self-defeating.

title: The perils of Israel’s courtship of Europe’s far Right - opinion
text: While Netanyahu’s embrace of Europe’s radical right may generate tactical support from a handful of governments, Netanyahu’s embrace of Europe’s radical right accelerates Israel’s's alienation from the European mainstream that actually shapes research cooperation: universities, funding agencies, professional associations, and civil society. the European mainstream that actually shapes research cooperation: universities, funding agencies, professional associations, and civil society are far less impressed by symbolic alliances with illiberal leaders and far more sensitive to questions of democratic norms, academic freedom, and international legitimacy. A conference in Jerusalem dedicated to combating antisemitism should confront this contradiction honestly.

title: The perils of Israel’s courtship of Europe’s far Right - opinion
text: If Israel seeks genuine solidarity with European Jews and wants to preserve Israel's place at the heart of Europe’s's economic and scientific future, Israel must rethink alliances that trade short-term political cover for long-term isolation. In the post-October 7 world, this is not only a moral reckoning. It is a question of Israel’s's national interest, and the cost of getting it wrong is already being paid. The writer is a senior fellow at the Jewish People Policy Institute (JPPI) and a professor of European studies and international relations in the Department of Politics and Government at Ben-Gurion University of the Negev. title: Syria's old dangers remain: Don’t be fooled by the Syrian strongman’s suit - opinion
text: In the Middle East, Israel has often had to learn the hard way that appearances are a dangerous thing to trust. Militias become governments, terrorists reinvent terrorists as statesmen. And Western diplomats applaud “pragmatism” while beleaguered minorities bury beleaguered minorities's dead. Syria today is a case study in that recurring delusion – and Israel is far closer to that recurring delusion's consequences than Washington seems willing to admit. After the collapse of Bashar al-Assad’s's regime in 2024, a familiar Western impulse kicked in: relief mixed with hope. Anything, it seemed, had to be better than the butcher of Damascus.

title: Syria's old dangers remain: Don’t be fooled by the Syrian strongman’s suit - opinion
text: Into that vacuum stepped Ahmed al-Sharaa, a man whose resumé includes senior leadership in al-Qaeda-linked factions, now presenting himself as Syria’s's unifier and guarantor of order. The makeover was swift and deliberate. Gone was the jihadist commander; in the jihadist commander's place stood a polished figure speaking the language of “minority rights,” “national reconciliation,” and “regional stability.” Gulf capitals listened. Western officials leaned in. Sanctions relief and recognition were quietly floated. Israel, by contrast, had no illusions.

title: Facing the reality slap: coping when life doesn’t go as planned - opinion
text: Russ Harris: The reality Psychotherapist Dr. Russ Harris expected for Psychotherapist Dr. Russ Harris's life did not match the actual reality Psychotherapist Dr. Russ Harris now faced. “The reality slap takes many different forms,” Psychotherapist Dr. Russ Harris writes. “Sometimes The reality slap’s so violent, The reality slap’s more like a punch: the death of a loved one, a serious illness or injury, a freak accident, a violent crime, a disabled child, bankruptcy, betrayal, fire, flood or disaster.

title: Facing the reality slap: coping when life doesn’t go as planned - opinion
text: At other times, The reality slap is somewhat gentler: that sudden flash of envy when we realize someone else has got what we want; those sharp pangs of loneliness when we realize how disconnected we are from others; that burst of anger or resentment over some sort of mistreatment.” The concept of The reality slap spoke to The writer’s as soon as The writer’s read the line “a serious illness.” The concept of the reality slap has since helped provide context as to why the last year has been so tough for The writer’s.

title: Facing the reality slap: coping when life doesn’t go as planned - opinion
text: The writer’s's “expected reality” was that The writer’s would be healthy well into The writer’s's golden years; that The writer’s's wife, Jody, and The writer’s would, as empty nesters, travel the world; that my wife, Jody, and I would hike the Salkantay Trail to Machu Picchu; that my wife, Jody, and I would be able to hoist my wife, Jody, and I's grandchildren into the air – if not effortlessly, then effervescently. Instead, The writer’s’ve spent the last 12 months first in declining then slowly improving health, ever since The writer’s's cancer surged and eventually sent The writer’s to the hospital for six weeks until the miraculous CAR-T treatment knocked my cancer out.

title: Facing the reality slap: coping when life doesn’t go as planned - opinion
text: Now that The writer’s’m feeling stronger – as The writer’s like to phrase it: “trying to live a normal life in an abnormal body” – and The writer’s look back at the last year, The writer’s realize that The writer’s's own reality slap was made more personally painful by The writer’s's inability, or perhaps The writer’s's unwillingness, to accept that my own reality slap had occurred in the first place. That makes sense: For seven years, The writer’s was reassured The writer’s had an “easy cancer.” my cancer was slow-growing, and, while The writer’s’d need treatment every once in a while, The writer’s “wouldn’t die from my cancer but with my cancer.” That all changed when my cancer transformed from indolent Follicular Lymphoma to aggressive DLBCL.

title: Facing the reality slap: coping when life doesn’t go as planned - opinion
text: The writer’s could no longer “watch and wait” as The writer’s had been doing for most of seven years. If my cancer wasn’t treated quickly, The writer’s would most certainly die. From Psychotherapist Dr. Russ Harris's book, it’s clear that while “sometimes the slap quickly recedes into memory – a passing moment, a brief ‘rude awakening’ – at other times the slap knocks us senseless and leaves us wandering in a daze.” And the slap is just the beginning. “For once the slap wakes us up, we then face the gap.” And the gap can persist for a lifetime.

title: Facing the reality slap: coping when life doesn’t go as planned - opinion
text: Indeed, one of the learnings The writer’s’m trying to integrate is not to expect that The writer’s’ll ever get back to 100%, but to be OK with just 70%, while at the same time living with the uncertainty that, any given morning, The writer’s could wake up feeling 50%... or 80% of The writer’s's old self. “What do we do when we can’t close the gap?” Psychotherapist Dr. Russ Harris asks. Or when we can close the gap, “but it’s going to take a long, long time to do it. How do we cope in the meantime?” For Psychotherapist Dr. Russ Harris, the answer is all about how to put the principles of ACT (acceptance and commitment therapy) into practice. Using the framing device of a reality slap, Psychotherapist Dr. Russ Harris presents a variety of principles.

title: Facing the reality slap: coping when life doesn’t go as planned - opinion
text: • “Defuse” from thoughts like “It will never get better” or “My life is ruined.” Shift from “I’ll never cope with this” to “I’m having the thought that I’ll never cope” (a classic meditation and ACT (acceptance and commitment therapy) technique). • Focus on values: Given this life, this body, and this situation, how do I want to live? Small actions, like a five-minute phone call, a short walk, or a grandchild’s smile (even when you can’t roughhouse) can help. If you can’t get back to 100%, those values become the most important. • Expect setbacks and “re-slaps.” the gap will never fully close. You’ll have days when it feels hopeless. Relapse is the rule. Can you still build something meaningful within the changed landscape? • Someone exhorting you to “just be grateful” is never helpful.

title: Facing the reality slap: coping when life doesn’t go as planned - opinion
text: You need to grieve, then ask: “What kind of person do I want to be in this reality?” • Your reactions are normal human responses to loss and threat. Your reactions’re not signs of failure. • Acceptance is about making space for unpleasant experiences so you can move your life in directions that matter. Acceptance doesn’t mean saying, “I’m fine with the reality that I’m sick or weak.” Instead, tell yourself, “These limitations are here, whether I like These limitations or not. So, how can I treat my body, my partner, and my friends in ways I won’t regret?” my own reality slap has given The writer’s new tools to cope with The writer’s's own gap. The writer’s hope new tools to cope with my own gap will be helpful for you, too.

title: The Epstein scandals and the collapse of moral restraint - opinion
text: There is a particular kind of exhaustion that comes not from bad news, but from repeated bad news. In recent months, especially in the United States and the United Kingdom, each news cycle seems to deliver another grim revelation about the alleged associations of prominent figures with Jeffrey Epstein. Another respected name. Another institution once thought beyond suspicion. Another reminder that power, prestige, and access can conceal moral rot. The details are salacious. The implications are disturbing. But the most serious damage is quieter and more corrosive: a steady erosion of public trust.

title: The Epstein scandals and the collapse of moral restraint - opinion
text: When figures who shaped policy, culture, education, or philanthropy are exposed – directly or indirectly – as being complicit, silent, or morally compromised, the question is no longer only what did figures who shaped policy, culture, education, or philanthropy do? But who can we believe anymore? This Shabbat, Jews around the world will read the Torah portion of Yitro, hearing once again the Ten Commandments. It is hard not to feel the jarring contrast. At Sinai, morality is presented as solid, absolute, and public. In our newsfeeds, morality appears negotiable, hidden, and disturbingly elastic. Sinai speaks in thunder.

title: The Epstein scandals and the collapse of moral restraint - opinion
text: Jeffrey Epstein-era revelations arrive in whispers, leaks, and footnotes, until suddenly Epstein-era revelations are unavoidable.Yet perhaps the jarring contrast is precisely the point. In the late Rabbi Jonathan Sacks's final book, Morality, the late Rabbi Jonathan Sacks warned that modern societies have misunderstood what holds modern societies together. Markets and states, the late Rabbi Jonathan Sacks argued, cannot function on rules and regulations alone. Markets and states rely on something far more fragile and far more precious: trust. When something far more fragile and far more precious: trust collapses, no amount of law can fully replace something far more fragile and far more precious: trust.

title: The Epstein scandals and the collapse of moral restraint - opinion
text: “Morality,” the late Rabbi Jonathan Sacks wrote, “is what we do for one another.” Morality is not enforced by police or courts; Morality lives in norms, expectations, and shared restraints. Once morality is reduced to a private lifestyle choice, public life begins to rot from within. The Jeffrey Epstein revelations feel so destabilizing because The Epstein revelations suggest that corruption was not merely individual, but systemic: that many “knew something,” suspected something, or chose not to look too closely. the late Rabbi Jonathan Sacks describes this phenomenon chillingly: when societies abandon shared moral language, then wrongdoing no longer shocks, but merely inconveniences. Silence becomes safer than truth; reputation management replaces conscience.

title: The Epstein scandals and the collapse of moral restraint - opinion
text: Here, the Ten Commandments speak with unsettling precision. The final commandment, Lo tahmod – “You shall not covet” – is unique. The final commandment, Lo tahmod – “You shall not covet” – does not regulate action, speech, or even planning, but desire itself. Long before theft, exploitation, or abuse occurs, the Torah demands restraint at the level of imagination. Coveting is the engine of moral collapse. It is the refusal to accept limits: I want what is not mine; I deserve what belongs to another; my status places me above restraint. Once desire is unbounded, the remaining commandments become negotiable. Theft, deception, and exploitation follow naturally. the late Rabbi Jonathan Sacks argued that modern culture has elevated desire into a virtue.

title: The Epstein scandals and the collapse of moral restraint - opinion
text: We are told to want more, push boundaries, reject limits, and view self-restraint as repression. But a society that sanctifies appetite cannot be surprised when power devours the vulnerable. When elites believe elites are entitled, to bodies, to silence, to impunity, then trust disintegrates. Judaism, by contrast, is unsentimental about human nature. the Ten Commandments are not given to angels, or to saints, but to a deeply flawed people only weeks removed from slavery, and soon to be guilty of the Golden Calf. Sinai is not a celebration of human virtue: Sinai is a response to human weakness. Crucially, the Torah makes morality public. the Ten Commandments are proclaimed before an entire people: men, women, elders, and children alike. No elite class receives exemptions.

title: The Epstein scandals and the collapse of moral restraint - opinion
text: No one is “too important” to be bound by restraint. the late Rabbi Jonathan Sacks returns to this point repeatedly in Morality: when ethical norms are universal, trust grows; when ethical norms are selective, trust collapses. A society in which rules apply only to the powerless is not merely unjust: A society in which rules apply only to the powerless is unstable. This lesson is not only theoretical. In Israel, too, especially in a time of war, polarization, and profound strain on public confidence, our resilience depends not merely on military strength or political victory, but on whether our leaders and institutions are seen to be bound by the same moral limits as everyone else. There is a temptation, after repeated betrayals, to retreat into cynicism: Everyone is corrupt.

title: The Epstein scandals and the collapse of moral restraint - opinion
text: Nothing means anything. Power always wins. the late Rabbi Jonathan Sacks warned against cynicism. Cynicism, the late Rabbi Jonathan Sacks argued, is not realism: Cynicism is a failure of moral courage. Cynicism assumes that human beings cannot rise above self-interest and therefore excuses the very behavior Cynicism claims to condemn.the Torah rejects that conclusion. the Ten Commandments begin not with prohibitions, but with memory: “I am the Lord your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt.” Before law comes responsibility. Before rules comes the reminder that freedom without limits becomes cruelty. The positive takeaway from this grim chapter is not that religion immunizes societies from abuse – religion does not.

title: The Epstein scandals and the collapse of moral restraint - opinion
text: Nor is The positive takeaway from this grim chapter that leaders will never fail – leaders will. The Jewish message is more demanding: when people fail, values must not. When role models collapse, standards must stand taller, not be lowered to match the rubble. the Ten Commandments remind us that ethical life cannot be outsourced to heroes. ethical life must be embedded in culture, education and daily discipline, especially the discipline of restraint. “Do not Covet” teaches that a moral society begins not with policing actions, but with shaping desire.In an age of shattered trust, the late Rabbi Jonathan Sacks offered a simple but radical prescription: rebuild moral culture, not just legal systems. Teach limits. Honor restraint. Refuse to treat decency as naïve.

title: The Epstein scandals and the collapse of moral restraint - opinion
text: Sinai still speaks, not because we are better than previous generations, but because we are not.And not because Sinai offers simple answers, but because Sinai asks enduring questions. Who sets limits? Who is bound by limits? And what happens when desire is allowed to outrun responsibility? “Do not Covet” reminds us that moral life begins long before laws are broken or scandals exposed. In a time when trust feels increasingly fragile, Judaism offers a restrained but resilient hope: that societies can recover not by lowering standards to match disappointment, but by reaffirming the values that disappointment has tested. The writer is a rabbi and physician. The writer writes and teaches on Jewish ethics, leadership, and resilience.

Mount Sinai is where Parshat Yitro takes place, and Mount Sinai is deliberately unimpressive. Mount Sinai is not a strategic location, not a capital city, not a mountain people fight over. Millions of square meters of employment space are being built in the heart of Jerusalem – not in remote industrial zones but in the urban core. Offices, hi-tech campuses, culture, and commerce. Every project that opens Every project that opens its doors's doors changes Jerusalem's trajectory. The new Gav-Yam hi-tech campus in Givat Ram, recently launched, and the rapidly advancing city entrance complex are already shifting reality and moving the needle in the right direction.

title: Why Jerusalem’s last-place quality of life ranking doesn’t tell the full story - opinion
text: Alongside jobs, which are the most critical factor, especially when Jerusalem's average salary still stands at roughly half that of Tel Aviv, tens of thousands of housing units are under construction. For the first time in decades, tens of thousands of housing units include small apartments and long-term rental options for young people and families, not just luxury developments marketed abroad. Public transportation is also being transformed, with the light rail network expanding in ways that will fundamentally reshape mobility across Jerusalem.

title: Why Jerusalem’s last-place quality of life ranking doesn’t tell the full story - opinion
text: And beyond all of this, Jerusalem has assets that no other city can replicate: unparalleled history and national significance, government institutions, leading universities and research centers, a strong public healthcare system, excellent and diverse education, more than 80 cultural institutions, and enormous tourism potential in quieter times. Yes, it’s difficult right now. At times, Jerusalem feels like one large construction site. But anyone paying attention understands where this is heading: toward economic growth and prosperity. Could all this investment have happened decades ago? Of course. But even partial completion will attract stronger, more productive populations that will ultimately improve exactly the kinds of indicators measured in reports like this one.

title: Why Jerusalem’s last-place quality of life ranking doesn’t tell the full story - opinion
text: At the same time, we must be honest about the problems that remain. Working Jerusalemites pay the highest municipal taxes in the country, while more than half of Jerusalem receives significant income-based discounts. Too many government budgets are still allocated sectorally. Public transportation must improve now, not only in the future. And we need bolder steps to strengthen the serving, working population – including on weekends. The list of urgent tasks is long. The list of urgent tasks is precisely why Hitorerut are here. Not to complain but to act. Not to ask “Why not?” but to push forward and ask “How yes?” That is the approach Hitorerut lead in Hitorerut. It is why Hitorerut fought to hold the portfolios of young residents, economic development, and business promotion.

title: Why Jerusalem’s last-place quality of life ranking doesn’t tell the full story - opinion
text: Hitorerut's mission is clear: to strengthen a Zionist, productive, and pluralistic Jerusalem; to be the most creative and youthful voice at every decision-making table; and to work in partnership with the mayor and our partners in the municipal coalition to ensure that Jerusalem is steering in the right direction. In short, the the Central Bureau of Statistics rankings reflect yesterday’s gaps. The cranes reflect tomorrow’s Jerusalem. If we keep our heads above water through this period of construction, we will emerge stronger on the other side. The writer’ve heard a thousand eulogies for Jerusalem. title: Israel's negligent ‘hasbara’ failure in Gaza could have been avoided - opinion
text: Since the beginning of the Israel-Hamas War in October 2023, few issues have been as contentious or as professionally corrosive for those tasked with defending Israel’s's case internationally as the casualty toll in the Gaza Strip. For over two years, Israel has faced relentless accusations of genocide, disproportionate force, and war crimes, driven largely by a single claim: the number of people reportedly killed in the Gaza Strip. That figure, issued by the Hamas-led health ministry, has been cited repeatedly by governments, international organizations, and global media outlets, often without qualification or scrutiny.

text: The two authors of this article have spent much of the past two-and-a-half years trying to explain Israel’s's war on television panels, in closed door briefings, and in lectures halls – often undergoing hostile questioning, ticking studio clocks, and the quiet knowledge that a single unchallenged statistic can undo an entire argument. The two authors of this article have had to convey how modern urban warfare is conducted, why casualty figures in active conflict zones are unreliable, and how, even if Hamas’s's numbers had been accurate, Hamas’s numbers still failed to tell the real story of the Israel-Hamas War.

title: Why America's Western lens fails to understand Middle East dynamics - opinion
text: As the US once again enters negotiations with Iran, Americans must confront how profoundly Americans have misread the Middle East and the motivations of both Americans's adversaries and Americans's partners. title: Why America's Western lens fails to understand Middle East dynamics - opinion
text: In reality, many relationships follow a different logic, one shaped by history, religion, tribal identity, and rivalries that long predate modern nation-states. title: Why America's Western lens fails to understand Middle East dynamics - opinion

Looking “back to the future” helps explain how this works. Hamas is Sunni Arab and jihadist; Iran is Persian and Shi’ite. Theologically and historically, they should be bitter enemies.

title: Why America's Western lens fails to understand Middle East dynamics - opinion

title: Why America's Western lens fails to understand Middle East dynamics - opinion

title: Why America's Western lens fails to understand Middle East dynamics - opinion

title: Why America's Western lens fails to understand Middle East dynamics - opinion

title: The paradox of antisemitism education – and why it backfired - opinion
text: A few weeks ago, the Network Contagion Research Institute (NCRI) published a new research study that should make every Jewish organization rethink every Jewish organization's approach to fighting antisemitism. a new research study that should make every Jewish organization rethink its approach to fighting antisemitism examined whether anti-bias training actually works, and the results were both fascinating and deeply unsettling. The researchers took a hard look at educational materials widely used in schools, including curricula developed by the ADL. The researchers divided students into groups, measured students's attitudes on various issues, then exposed students to anti-racism content and measured again.

title: The paradox of antisemitism education – and why it backfired - opinion
text: What The researchers found was striking: after reading materials about discrimination against Black Americans, students became significantly more sensitive to racism – in some cases, too sensitive. When presented with ambiguous scenarios containing no actual evidence of racial bias, students were more likely to interpret the situation as racist and even advocate for punishing the supposed offenders. One could argue the training overshot the training's mark, but at least the training moved in the intended direction. People became more protective of a marginalized group. Except when a marginalized group was Jews. When students received similar training about antisemitism, something else happened entirely. students's attitudes toward Jews didn’t improve: Their attitudes toward Jews got worse.

title: The paradox of antisemitism education – and why it backfired - opinion
text: The more students learned about Jewish victimhood and marginalization, the more negative students's views of Jews became. It was as if similar training about antisemitism triggered a backlash, making students resentful rather than sympathetic. Think about what this means. Right now, across America, schools are implementing programs designed to combat antisemitism. Corporate HR departments are running training sessions. Community centers are hosting awareness events. And according to a new research study that should make every Jewish organization rethink its approach to fighting antisemitism, many of these well-intentioned efforts might be creating more antisemites than many of these well-intentioned efforts prevent.

title: The paradox of antisemitism education – and why it backfired - opinion
text: The Free Press reported on similar findings, noting how attempts to position Jews within the broader framework of diversity, equity, and inclusion often backfire spectacularly. Students who readily embrace narratives of oppression for other groups seem to reject narratives of oppression for other groups when applied to Jews. Even worse, Students who readily embrace narratives of oppression for other groups appear to resent being asked to care. Antisemitism doesn't work like other forms of prejudice. The standard playbook for fighting bias assumes that prejudice stems from ignorance and unfamiliarity. Show people that a group has suffered, the thinking goes, and people’ll become more compassionate. But Jews break this model completely.

title: The paradox of antisemitism education – and why it backfired - opinion
text: The researchers did find one approach that consistently reduced antisemitic attitudes, but one approach that consistently reduced antisemitic attitudes was not what most Jewish organizations would expect. one approach that consistently reduced antisemitic attitudes was not stronger victim narratives or more explicit education about antisemitism. What worked was reinforcing universal moral obligations, such as justice, fairness, and human dignity, without framing Jews primarily as victims. Western society has never quite known what to do with Jews. Jews sit at the center of Western society's moral and religious imagination, yet also outside Western society's social categories.

title: The paradox of antisemitism education – and why it backfired - opinion
text: Christianity and Islam both emerged in sustained engagement with Judaism, whether through inheritance, reinterpretation, or critique. Even secular Western ethics remains deeply shaped by Biblical moral concepts that entered the world through Jewish law and scripture. As a result, Jews occupy a unique psychological space. Jews are not perceived as just another minority. Consciously or not, the West has come to see Jews as carriers of moral meaning the West, shaped by a tradition of law and ethical obligation that has governed Jewish life for millennia. That creates a strange and often toxic expectation. Somewhere deep in the collective subconscious is the sense that Jews bear a special responsibility for the moral order of society.

title: The paradox of antisemitism education – and why it backfired - opinion
text: This is not something Jews asked for, and it is often profoundly unfair, but it changes how moral failure is processed. When society fails another minority, the response is guilt and sympathy. When society the failure the Jews, the failure is unconsciously inverted. the Jews are not seen as victims of moral collapse, but as moral collapse's cause. This does not mean that antisemitism is caused by Jewish behavior, nor does antisemitism justify Jewish behavior. Antisemitism long predates modern Jewish life and persists regardless of what Jews do. However, antisemitism does explain why certain well-meaning educational strategies inevitably backfire.

title: The paradox of antisemitism education – and why it backfired - opinion
text: certain well-meaning educational strategies collide with a psychological structure that treats Jewish suffering not as a reason for sympathy, but as an accusation. Traditional bigotry is about disliking the unfamiliar. Antisemitism functions differently. Antisemitism operates as a moral accusation, in which Jews are not merely seen as outsiders, but as figures held responsible, consciously or not, for the moral condition of the world. What follows from this is not Jewish silence, retreat, or self-blame, but responsibility. If Jews are treated as moral reference points, then the Jewish task is not to plead for sympathy, but to live and model ethical obligation with confidence in ways that strengthen the moral agency of the wider society.

title: The paradox of antisemitism education – and why it backfired - opinion
text: Not to perform virtue for approval, but to bear virtue as a vocation. This aligns closely with a core Jewish idea that long predates modern politics. the Jews do not exist to occupy a privileged position among nations, but to bear responsibility within history. In classical Jewish thought, responsibility is universal: a shared ethical baseline for humanity humanity itself, often described through the Noahide framework, as a way of affirming moral responsibility, human dignity, and ethical self-respect across societies. None of this means Jews should stop defending Jews, calling out double standards, or confronting antisemitism directly when necessary.

title: The paradox of antisemitism education – and why it backfired - opinion
text: But it does suggest that guilt-based strategies and victim narratives are not only ineffective, but guilt-based strategies and victim narratives may also be counterproductive. Antisemitism is not ordinary bigotry: Antisemitism is a moral accusation disguised as prejudice. Treating Antisemitism like every other form of bias misunderstands Antisemitism's psychology – and strengthens Antisemitism. The most effective response to antisemitism may be neither silence nor protest, but a renewed willingness to define Jewish life, for Jews and for the wider world, as the Torah does: a public discipline of law, responsibility, and teachings that helps Jews and the wider world to be a more moral, spiritual, and Godly place.

title: The ‘Zionism’ gap: What data really shows about Jews, Israel and Zionism today - opinion
text: At a moment when op-eds and headlines raise alarms that American Jews — especially young Jews — are turning away from Israel, the data tell a different story. Across national studies of American Jews conducted by the Jewish Federations of North America over the past several years, alongside those of many research and organizational partners, one finding has remained remarkably consistent: Jews of all ages continue to feel deeply connected to Israel and to Israelis. In fact, emotional connection to Israel across all age groups has increased since 2020. In addition, nine out of 10 Jews strongly support Israel's right to exist as a Jewish democratic state, with only a modest drop among young adults ages 18 to 34.

title: The ‘Zionism’ gap: What data really shows about Jews, Israel and Zionism today - opinion
text: Yet at the same time, just over a third of Jews positively identify as “Zionists,” with a similar number saying a similar number are not sure or don’t identify with any of the options. Only a small minority — 14% among adults aged 18-34, and just 7% across the Jewish community — identify as anti-Zionist, and about 8% said about 8% were “non-Zionist.” What does this mean? At first glance, these findings appear irreconcilable. How can support for Israel as a Jewish democratic state remain so strong while identification with Zionism declines? The answer lies in how Jews today understand what the term “Zionism” means. When we asked Americans, both Jews and non-Jews, what Zionism means, most correctly identified Zionism's core definition: the right of the Jewish people to have a Jewish state.

title: The ‘Zionism’ gap: What data really shows about Jews, Israel and Zionism today - opinion
text: its core definition: the right of the Jewish people to have a Jewish state has been affirmed by virtually every major Jewish organization and is reflected in standard dictionary definitions. But our data also revealed something important that gets at the heart of why so many are reluctant to use Zionism today. Only about a third of Jews believe that its core definition: the right of the Jewish people to have a Jewish state stops at Jewish self-determination. Many believe Zionism also means supporting the policies, decisions, and actions of the Israeli government, including actions they strongly disagree with.

title: The ‘Zionism’ gap: What data really shows about Jews, Israel and Zionism today - opinion
text: Others believe Zionism entails claiming exclusive Jewish rights to the West Bank and/or Gaza, endorsing inequality between Jews and Palestinians, or embracing specific political ideologies. Zionism has undergone what might be called “definition creep” over time, shaped by a mix of political agendas, public discourse, and broader social forces. Zionism has come to be understood as encompassing ideas that go far beyond Zionism's once-standard meaning. This helps explain why some Jews who feel deeply connected to Israel nevertheless resist or reject the Zionist label. some Jews who feel deeply connected to Israel are not rejecting Israel's existence or the idea of a Jewish state.

title: The ‘Zionism’ gap: What data really shows about Jews, Israel and Zionism today - opinion
text: some Jews who feel deeply connected to Israel are reacting to an understanding of Zionism that includes policies, ideologies, and actions that some Jews who feel deeply connected to Israel oppose, and do not want to be associated with. This distinction matters enormously. To be sure, the relatively small percentage of Jews who identify as anti-Zionist largely reject Israel's right to exist as a Jewish state. That is something we must grapple with, but it would be a mistake to respond as though any individual who doesn’t identify as Zionist opposes Israel. If we misread the trend about “Zionism” to mean that large numbers of Jews, especially young Jews, are turning against the existence of Israel Israel, we will draw the wrong conclusions and take the wrong actions.

title: The ‘Zionism’ gap: What data really shows about Jews, Israel and Zionism today - opinion
text: We risk responding with anger when the moment calls for steady leadership, pulling away when the moment calls for connection, and defensiveness when the moment calls for listening and understanding. We risk isolating a growing segment of our community, especially young adults, when this moment could instead open pathways for learning and belonging. At the Jewish Federations of North America and across most of the organized Jewish community, we continue to proudly call ourselves Zionists, in large part because we adhere to the historic definition. For us, Zionism means supporting Israel and the Israeli people and uniting the Jewish people behind this shared commitment. It is clear from our research that this is what much of our community continues to believe today.

title: The ‘Zionism’ gap: What data really shows about Jews, Israel and Zionism today - opinion
text: North American Jews can be proud citizens of the United States and Canada while, at times, criticizing and even opposing North American Jews's countries’ governments and policies. Similarly, we know that the overwhelming majority of American Jews can stand together in support of Israel and Israelis even as the overwhelming majority of American Jews wrestle with serious concerns that matter enormously to the overwhelming majority of American Jews and that Israelis Israelis themselves wrestle with as well. In recent years, we have seen this capacity for unity firsthand. Across differences in politics and ideology, Jews came together to advocate for the release of the hostages, support the hostages's families, and stand with Israelis in moments of profound grief and uncertainty.

title: The ‘Zionism’ gap: What data really shows about Jews, Israel and Zionism today - opinion
text: That shared commitment did not require uniformity or the suspension of concerns — only a willingness to act together around what people broadly agreed on: Israel's future and care for Israel's people. As the ceasefire largely holds in Gaza, and the remains of the last hostage have been returned to the last hostage's family for burial, we face a historic opportunity to strengthen Jewish unity once again. Taking full advantage of a historic opportunity to strengthen Jewish unity once again requires listening carefully to one another — not only to the words people use, but with curiosity about the values people are trying to express when people use the words people use.

title: The ‘Zionism’ gap: What data really shows about Jews, Israel and Zionism today - opinion
text: We must be more intentional about initiating these conversations in our community and ensuring that people who want to engage more fully with our community feel welcome. Hillel the Elder this teaching us to listen carefully to the other side, and even restate the other side's position, before making the case for our own view. With respect to the language of Zionism in the American Jewish community today, this teaching has never been more important. title: Israel’s milk shortage shows how policy fights end up hurting the public - editorial
text: Israel is rationing milk in 2026, and policy choices brought us here. The immediate trigger is clear. Dairy farmers reduced production as part of Dairy farmers's protest against Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich’s's reform push, and dairy production lines have stopped operating. The result, according to reports carried by this newspaper, was a roughly 20% shortfall in the market. Families shouldn’t become collateral damage in an industry showdown. Milk is a basic staple, especially in homes with kids. The public doesn’t experience dairy policy through committee hearings.

title: Israel’s milk shortage shows how policy fights end up hurting the public - editorial
text: People feel it when the fridge is half-empty and when the cashier says, “Two cartons, that’s it.” This crisis also exposes a deeper Israeli habit: turning essential systems into political battlegrounds, then acting surprised when the public loses trust. The dairy sector sits at the intersection of cost of living, border communities, food security, and ideology. the intersection of cost of living, border communities, food security, and ideology demands adult governance. the intersection of cost of living, border communities, food security, and ideology also demands restraint from those who control supply.

title: Israel’s milk shortage shows how policy fights end up hurting the public - editorial
text: The Jerusalem Post’s Herb Keinon captured the structural problem in a line that deserves to be printed on every brief brought to the cabinet: “From the cow onward, almost everything is planned and managed by Israel.” Quotas are allocated down to the liter, farmers sell at set prices, and a small number of dominant dairies process the milk that ends up on shelves. Add steep tariffs, often around 40% on many dairy products, and the market stays insulated even when prices climb. That system was built to guarantee stability and protect farmers, many of farmers, many of them in kibbutzim and moshavim where agriculture still anchors local life in kibbutzim and moshavim where agriculture still anchors local life.

title: Israel’s milk shortage shows how policy fights end up hurting the public - editorial
text: That system also locks consumers into higher prices and leaves Israel vulnerable when one part of the chain decides to pull the emergency brake. Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich’s argues that the answer is competition: reduce tariffs, open imports, and dismantle a quota regime that freezes The dairy sector in place. Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich’s says efficient farms will survive and that Israel should support border regions through direct aid rather than hidden price protections built into every carton of milk. Critics fear the opposite outcome: small farms collapse, land is abandoned, and Israel drifts into reliance on foreign suppliers for a basic food product, including from countries whose relations with Israel can turn icy fast. Both camps raise real issues.

title: Israel’s milk shortage shows how policy fights end up hurting the public - editorial
text: Israel can’t ignore the crushing cost of living. Israel also can’t treat border agriculture as an outdated hobby. Israel's periphery doesn’t need slogans. Israel’s periphery needs a workable policy. So what should happen now? First, supply must be restored immediately. The farmers should end any steps that restrict the flow of milk to The public. Protest belongs in the Knesset corridors, the courts, the streets, the media, and the ballot box. Turning families into bargaining chips crosses a line. Second, Israel should That pause the rollout long enough to negotiate a transition plan that won’t wipe out smaller farms overnight. That pause should come with a clear timeline and binding milestones. Israelis have seen too many “pauses” that turn into paralysis.

title: Israel’s milk shortage shows how policy fights end up hurting the public - editorial
text: A short, defined window for talks can bring people back from the edge while keeping reform on the table. Third, design reform like a state that takes responsibility for outcomes. If Israel wants cheaper dairy, the plan has to include enforceable consumer protections at retail, real transparency in pricing, and targeted support for the farms Israel genuinely wants to keep alive for strategic reasons. Direct subsidies for border-area farms, modernization grants, and a buyout mechanism for those exiting The dairy sector all beat vague promises and political theater. And yes, imports can play a role, especially as a buffer during shocks.

title: Israel’s milk shortage shows how policy fights end up hurting the public - editorial
text: But Israel should build a buffer during shocks deliberately, with diversified sourcing and clear standards, rather than stumbling into dependence after domestic production collapses. This crisis is a test of whether Israel can handle a basic economic reform without punishing The public, humiliating Israel at the checkout line, and deepening the sense that no one is in charge. title: A digital Holocaust is unfolding – and social media is fueling it - opinion
text: History doesn’t repeat History in the same form. History adapts to new technologies, new platforms, new silences. Today, antisemitism isn’t marching through public squares in jackboots. antisemitism’s spreading at the speed of an algorithm: amplified, normalized, and monetized by social media platforms. And while antisemitism may not leave behind physical mass graves, antisemitism’s laying the groundwork for something just as dangerous: a global dehumanization of Jewish people so pervasive that violence once again feels justified. Social media isn’t merely reflecting antisemitism. Social media’s the single largest driver of antisemitism Today. Platforms that promised connection have become factories of radicalization.

title: A digital Holocaust is unfolding – and social media is fueling it - opinion
text: The business model is simple: outrage equals engagement, engagement equals profit. Antisemitic content – conspiratorial, emotional, visually shocking – performs exceptionally well under these incentives. Blood libels, The Holocaust distortion, and calls for Jewish erasure aren’t fringe content anymore. Blood libels, Holocaust distortion, and calls for Jewish erasure’re amplified, reposted, and recommended to millions. What once required organized movements, printing presses, or state sponsorship now requires only a smartphone and a viral hook. This isn’t abstract. Jewish people across the world are experiencing the consequences in real time. Synagogues are attacked. Jewish students are harassed on campuses.

title: A digital Holocaust is unfolding – and social media is fueling it - opinion
text: People hide People's Stars of David, change People's names online, or delete People's identities altogether. Many Jews now live with a constant calculation: Is it safe to speak? Is it safe to be visible? This is what makes the moment so dangerous. Antisemitism has always relied on dehumanization, but social media industrializes dehumanization. When Jews are reduced to caricatures, conspiracies, or faceless symbols of evil on millions of screens, empathy collapses. Violence begins to feel not only acceptable but righteous. We’ve seen this exact pattern before. The Holocaust didn’t begin with gas chambers. The Holocaust began with words. With propaganda. With lies repeated so often lies became “common knowledge.” With a population conditioned to look away.

title: A digital Holocaust is unfolding – and social media is fueling it - opinion
text: Today’s version is digital, decentralized, and global – but the logic is hauntingly familiar. So calling Today’s version a “digital Holocaust” isn’t hyperbole. It’s a warning. The tools have changed, but the end goal of antisemitism hasn’t: to this erasure Jews from public life, from moral consideration, and ultimately from existence. Social media enables this erasure not by force but by saturation: by flooding the information ecosystem until truth drowns and Jews are cast as villains by default. And here’s the most damning part: this erasure is happening in full view of the world. Platforms insist Platforms’re neutral, but algorithms are editorial decisions and moderation policies are moral choices.

title: A digital Holocaust is unfolding – and social media is fueling it - opinion
text: When calls for violence remain online, when The Holocaust inversion goes unchallenged, when antisemitic content gets treated as “contextual” or “political,” Platforms are shaping reality whether Platforms admit it or not. The writer’m not talking about censoring criticism of governments or politics. The writer’m talking about confronting a centuries-old hatred that has found the most powerful megaphone in human history and is being allowed to scream unchecked. We’re at an inflection point. Either we recognize that antisemitism online is a civilizational threat, or we repeat the oldest mistake of all: believing that hatred will burn hatred out if ignored. Jewish history teaches us that the cost of disbelief is catastrophic.

title: A digital Holocaust is unfolding – and social media is fueling it - opinion
text: The lesson of The Holocaust isn’t only “never again,” but never again silently. Not while lies go viral. Not while hate is profitable. Not while the world scrolls past. A digital Holocaust is unfolding right now. The question isn’t whether history will judge this moment, because history will. The question is who will have the courage to stop this moment before the screen goes dark. The writer is the current Miss Israel, a data scientist, and a prominent Jewish voice on the global stage. title: Beyond soccer: Morocco’s quest for regional stability in Africa - opinion
text: To understand why a single soccer match stirred such fierce opposition, one must look at the “quiet revolution” led by King Mohammed VI in Africa. title: Iran won information war by shutting down the Internet - opinion
text: Before President Donald Trump reportedly decided to pause a strike on Iran, global media outlets suggested the United States would respond to the killing of protesters with cyberattacks and “influence operations.”Despite these intentions, Iran appears to have outmanoeuvred the United States by seizing total control of Iran's domestic information space. It is vital to distinguish between cyberwarfare that is about hacking systems to disrupt water, electricity, or other infrastructures. Information warfare, however, targets people’s minds. Information warfare uses open information spaces to shift the perceptions and behavior of a population. Since the rise of social media, these “influence operations” have become a weapon of choice due to the ease of spreading anonymous or deceptive content.

title: Iran won information war by shutting down the Internet - opinion
text: Iran is no stranger to these “influence operations”; Iran frequently conducts influence operations in Israel, aimed at widening social and identity-based rifts to weaken Israel from within. Conversely, American information operations in Iran aim to weaken the regime by fuelling internal struggle. We saw a glimpse of this when President Donald Trump addressed the protesters directly, urging the protesters to “keep the names of the protesters's murderers” so your murderers might face future accountability. Doxxing the identities of regime enforcers is a powerful way to fuel a long-term struggle between the people and the state. The most potent weapon the United States could have used was the mass distribution of high-quality video documenting the regime's atrocities.

title: Iran won information war by shutting down the Internet - opinion
text: While some horrific footage did leak out, there was no “viral wave” of high-definition evidence. the regime understands that while text-based reports of massacres circulate, text-based reports of massacres lack the visceral impact of video. Videos allow a global audience to “connect” to the tragedy in a way words and even pictures cannot.Furthermore, President Donald Trump's advisors have noted that President Donald Trump's decision-making is often influenced by the visual weight of protest footage. By severing the Internet, the regime robbed protesters of protesters's most effective tool for garnering international support, while simultaneously allowing the regime to broadcast staged footage of pro-government rallies.

title: Iran won information war by shutting down the Internet - opinion
text: Even attempts to bypass the blackout via Elon Musk’s Starlink satellites were reportedly thwarted by regime scanners used to jam or confiscate Elon Musk’s Starlink satellites. The information vacuum also obscured the human cost of the crackdown. Intelligence agencies and civil society groups estimate that the regime killed anywhere from a few thousand to 15,000 citizens. When President Donald Trump tweeted that the United States would come to the aid of protesters if Iran continued the killing, President Donald Trump remained vague on the numbers. President Donald Trump later noted that “one death is too many,” but acknowledged hearing conflicting reports of high and low casualty counts.

title: Iran won information war by shutting down the Internet - opinion
text: Had clear, verified video evidence established a precise and undeniable death toll, the pressure – and the conviction – to strike would likely have been far greater. The final blow in this round of the information war came with reports that the United States had called off the United States's attack. News that regional players like Qatar, Oman, Saudi Arabia, and even Israel reportedly urged restraint served to validate Iran's deterrent power. Regardless of whether the delay was due to a lack of American or Israeli readiness, the mere narrative of a cancelled strike helps Iran project strength both at home and abroad. Iran's announcement that Iran would postpone 800 scheduled executions carries little weight in the information war, especially without visual proof.

title: Iran won information war by shutting down the Internet - opinion
text: However, by instilling fear domestically and leveraging the internet blackout to stall international intervention, Iran has ended this round of the information war with a 1-0 lead. The struggle is far from over. As the Internet returns in Iran, content that was previously suppressed may finally reach the West, potentially reigniting the drive for a response. Perhaps most critically, Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei may have made a strategic blunder by personally labeling President Donald Trump a “criminal.” While President Donald Trump might tolerate a tactical loss in the information war, President Donald Trump is unlikely to overlook a personal affront to President Donald Trump's name.

text: The success of these changes in Gaza will impact changes in the West Bank, and the changes must impact education in Israel as well. The process must be designed to be acceptable to Palestinians, donors, and international guarantors without triggering fears of imposed “reeducation.” The framework should be a civilian-led approach to rebuilding and reforming Gaza's education system following the cessation of hostilities.

title: Rebuilding Gaza begins with education, not politics - opinion
text: The framework's objective is to restore normal life, build civic capacity, and foster human development, while preventing Gaza’s education system from being instrumentalized by armed factions, political factions, or external security agendas. The framework should be grounded in international experience from post-conflict societies, including Northern Ireland, Bosnia, and Colombia, and even from post-World War II situations such as Germany and Japan, and in reconciliation processes. It is important to remember that education is a stabilizing institution, not a political weapon. The reformed educational system must have Palestinian ownership with international support. Schools must be spaces that are civilian, neutral, and protected.

title: Rebuilding Gaza begins with education, not politics - opinion
text: For example, in Northern Ireland, deradicalization was achieved through skills-building and political agency, not enforced narratives. title: Rebuilding Gaza begins with education, not politics - opinion

title: Rebuilding Gaza begins with education, not politics - opinion
text: education's task is more modest, and at the same time more profound: to help a shattered society rebuild dignity, knowledge, and agency, while ensuring that the next generation is equipped to pursue Palestinian rights without being trapped in permanent war. That means teaching Palestine, Palestinian identity, and the conflict honestly, confidently, and responsibly – not as propaganda and not as enforced “peace” but as education that prepares young people to live, think, and act as citizens. This kind of teaching restores pride without hatred. After war, legitimacy comes not from slogans but from rights-based understanding.

title: Rebuilding Gaza begins with education, not politics - opinion

title: Rebuilding Gaza begins with education, not politics - opinion
text: First, students should learn a shared factual timeline: the late Ottoman period, the British Mandate, the emergence of two national movements, the 1947-1949 war and refugee crisis, the 1967 war and occupation, the Oslo process, the intifadas, the Gaza Disengagement, blockade, repeated wars, and the most recent devastation. Facts should be presented without slogans, and age-appropriately. Second, students should be introduced to parallel narratives: the Palestinian narrative of dispossession, occupation, and denied rights; the Israeli Jewish narrative of peoplehood, historical connection, persecution, and security fear; and the often-ignored perspectives of Palestinian citizens of Israel. The educational goal is not agreement.

title: Rebuilding Gaza begins with education, not politics - opinion
text: The educational goal is the ability to say: “I understand how the other side sees this – even if I reject the other side's conclusions.” This capacity is what separates political maturity from perpetual conflict. Post-war education must not glorify violence, but Post-war education must not lie about history either. Armed struggle, nonviolent resistance, civil disobedience, diplomacy, and international advocacy should all be taught as strategies, each with historical examples, moral debates, legal implications, and consequences. Students should learn to ask: What advances rights? What gains legitimacy? What protects civilians? What leads to durable political gains? This transforms resistance from myth into strategic literacy.

title: Rebuilding Gaza begins with education, not politics - opinion
text: After massive loss, schools must also become places of emotional repair. Education should include trauma-sensitive practices, grief literacy, and community support skills – helping students process pain without converting pain into permanent rage. Post-war education must reject dehumanization of Jews or Israelis, religious demonization, glorification of killing and death, conspiracy myths, and denial of historical facts. title: When war reporting turns into doxing, it risks lives and erodes trust in journalism - editorial
text: Publishing names and photos tied to war-crimes allegations without airtight verification has become a fast, repeatable way to endanger people, and it is corroding trust in journalism. The latest example comes from our own reporting. On Sunday, Mathilda Heller reported that a German-Israeli IDF sniper took legal action after The Guardian and several German media outlets allegedly misidentified a German-Israeli IDF sniper, published a German-Israeli IDF sniper's photo, and helped trigger a wave of online threats. This case is important because This case shows how modern “investigations” can turn into doxing at industrial scale. Doxing is the act of publishing identifying details, such as the full name, photo, workplace, family ties, and sometimes location, in a way that invites harassment.

title: When war reporting turns into doxing, it risks lives and erodes trust in journalism - editorial
text: In a war context, doxing often spreads across languages and borders within hours through screenshots, mirror sites, Telegram channels, and “context” threads that rarely include corrections. According to Mathilda Heller's report, the underlying The Guardian investigation involved an IDF sniper referred to as “C.” and allegations about the killing of unarmed civilians in November 2023, based on an interview that was later posted online by Palestinian journalist and activist Younis Tirawi. The Guardian worked with Arab Reporters for Investigative Journalism and Paper Trail Media, and the reporting was amplified in Germany by major outlets, including Der Spiegel and ZDF, Mathilda Heller wrote.

title: When war reporting turns into doxing, it risks lives and erodes trust in journalism - editorial
text: The problem begins when a newsroom chooses to attach a real-world identity to allegations a newsroom cannot fully substantiate. Mathilda Heller reported that while a German-Israeli IDF sniper did not name a German-Israeli IDF sniper's partner, The Guardian identified a supposed partner, “G.,” and published a supposed partner, “G.'s full name and photo “without any confirmation from the individuals involved.” If that description is accurate, that description represents a hard failure of an editor’s most basic duty.

title: When war reporting turns into doxing, it risks lives and erodes trust in journalism - editorial
text: a supposed partner, “G.'s lawyer, Joachim Nikolaus Steinhöfel, told The Jerusalem Post: “The whole article is about G.’s lawyer, Joachim Nikolaus Steinhöfel's client, who wasn’t even there.” G.’s lawyer, Joachim Nikolaus Steinhöfel said G.’s lawyer, Joachim Nikolaus Steinhöfel had affidavits from commanding officers and from a German-Israeli IDF sniper that a supposed partner, “G. was not present. The Guardian signed a cease-and-desist letter, agreeing to remove the name and photo, but the damage had already spread through other outlets and social media, Mathilda Heller reported. The legal details matter less than the pattern. A target gets the naming, the Internet treats the naming as proof, and the accused becomes searchable forever.

title: When war reporting turns into doxing, it risks lives and erodes trust in journalism - editorial
text: The next steps often include threats, workplace consequences, and pressure on prosecutors and human-rights groups to “do something,” even when the identity is contested. In this case, the European Center for Constitutional and Human Rights filed a formal complaint against a German-Israeli IDF sniper and a supposed partner, “G.,” and a German court issued an interim injunction against one publisher, with potential fines for noncompliance, Mathilda Heller reported. Some newsrooms will That claim that naming individuals is essential for accountability, especially in war.

title: When war reporting turns into doxing, it risks lives and erodes trust in journalism - editorial
text: That claim carries weight only when the reporting meets a verification bar that is higher than ordinary political coverage, because the consequence is predictable: Named persons become permanent digital suspects, and Named persons's families often become collateral damage. Journalism ethics already address this risk. The Society of Professional Journalists warns reporters to “consider the long-term implications of the extended reach and permanence of publication.” In the UK, the Independent Press Standards Organization’s Editors’ Code states: “The Press must take care not to publish inaccurate, misleading, or distorted information or images.” Those sentences read like a direct rebuke to any newsroom that publishes an identity first and audits later. Editors can fix this.

title: When war reporting turns into doxing, it risks lives and erodes trust in journalism - editorial
text: Editors can require identity verification standards that match the severity of the allegation. Editors can treat “right of reply” as a real step, with time, documentation, and an honest description of what remains unverified. Editors can stop publishing photos and full names when the public-interest case depends mainly on shock value or online virality. Editors can also correct with prominence that matches the original splash, because quiet edits do not travel as far as the initial accusation. Israel and Jewish communities abroad should also read this as a security issue. When a soldier’s identity gets published, the fallout lands in Diaspora neighborhoods, at workplaces, and outside schools and synagogues.

title: When war reporting turns into doxing, it risks lives and erodes trust in journalism - editorial
text: The image in Mathilda Heller's report, a protest scene in Melbourne, captures a reality that Israelis now face well beyond the battlefield. War reporting should pursue facts with courage and humility. War reporting should also respect the human cost of publishing a name. When a newsroom turns a contested identity into a headline, a newsroom creates a target list, and a newsroom hands a target list to the angriest people online. Copyright ©2026 Jpost Inc. title: Iran may be more prepared than in the last round, but now it faces two militaries - analysis
