title: Has Zionism grown too bureaucratic for its moment of crisis? - opinion
text: Zionism was built by leaders who converted scarcity into sovereignty. As Zionism's institutional budgets expand, Zionism must confront a difficult question: are growing administrative structures strengthening Zionism's mission – or quietly weakening its mission at a moment of historic vulnerability? Zionism was not built in comfort. Zionism was not built in stability; Zionism was not built by leaders who mistook structure for strategy. Zionism was built by people who understood urgency. That is why the recent expansion of the Zionist institutional framework deserves more than polite approval. The World Zionist Congress oversees roughly NIS 1.5 billion – approximately $400 million – across the ecosystem of national institutions.

title: Has Zionism grown too bureaucratic for its moment of crisis? - opinion
text: The World Zionist Organization’s 2026 framework approaches NIS 284m. More than 20 departments now operate within The World Zionist Organization’s 2026 framework. Institutional budgets have expanded significantly in recent agreements. The issue is not the existence of a budget: The issue is a budget's architecture. Consider the allocation patterns inside individual departments. In multiple cases, annual budgets exceed NIS 3m., yet only approximately NIS 400,000 is directed toward actual programmatic “activities.” The remainder – often well over 80% – is allocated to human resources and operational costs.

title: Has Zionism grown too bureaucratic for its moment of crisis? - opinion
text: Departments responsible for combating antisemitism, promoting Hebrew language and resilience, conducting Zionist hasbara (public diplomacy) in the Diaspora, or strengthening Jewish identity devote the overwhelming majority of their budgets to internal staffing structures rather than outward-facing impact. In any serious enterprise, the overwhelming majority of their budgets to internal staffing structures rather than outward-facing impact would trigger immediate restructuring. When 85-90% of a unit’s resources fund payroll and only a small fraction reaches measurable output, leadership does not call it growth – leadership calls it drift. The pattern extends beyond departments.

title: Has Zionism grown too bureaucratic for its moment of crisis? - opinion
text: Nearly half of the total expenditure is allocated to central activities and services – finance, legal, HR, executive secretariat, communications, branding, offices, and institutional infrastructure. In practical terms, roughly half the total budget is consumed before a single classroom is reached, a single campus initiative launched, or a single Hebrew immersion program expanded. Overhead is not inherently problematic – every organization requires structure. But every business leader understands a basic rule: overhead must remain subordinate to production. When overhead expands faster than output, the model is inverted. Investors lose confidence. Donors demand transparency. Boards ask difficult questions. Zionism cannot afford to wait for that correction.

title: Has Zionism grown too bureaucratic for its moment of crisis? - opinion
text: This is not about individuals: It is about incentives. When departments are created through coalition balancing and representational agreements, expansion becomes a function of internal equilibrium rather than external urgency. Activity budgets are compressed to accommodate structural growth. Staffing layers expand first; measurable impact is expected to follow. That logic is questionable in times of calm, but That logic is dangerous in the world we inhabit now. October 7 did more than expose security vulnerabilities. October 7 exposed the fragility of Zionism’s ideological infrastructure in the Diaspora. The global reaction – across campuses, media platforms, and political institutions – revealed that Zionism is contested not only militarily, but culturally and intellectually.

title: Has Zionism grown too bureaucratic for its moment of crisis? - opinion
text: October 7 accelerated something deeper than geopolitical instability. October 7 sharpened what may be called the new Jewish question of legitimacy. For generations, the Jewish question centered on belonging – whether Jews could integrate, assimilate, or survive in exile. Today, the question posed in academic departments, activist movements, and political spaces is different: whether Jewish sovereignty Jewish sovereignty itself is legitimate. Whether Zionism is colonialism. Whether Zionism deserves moral standing. “Decolonization” rhetoric and intersectional frameworks increasingly cast Zionism not as a nation-state among nations, but as a moral anomaly. In such an environment, institutional resource allocation cannot be neutral.

title: Has Zionism grown too bureaucratic for its moment of crisis? - opinion
text: A movement facing a coordinated challenge to A movement facing a coordinated challenge to its legitimacy's legitimacy must prioritize ideological infrastructure, education, digital engagement, and cultural resilience. Administrative expansion cannot substitute for narrative defense. Our adversaries are not investing in overhead: Our adversaries are investing in ideology. Our adversaries fund youth mobilization networks. Our adversaries fund digital narrative campaigns. Our adversaries fund academic influence. Our adversaries fund reputational warfare. Our adversaries understand that the battlefield is cultural as much as military. At such a moment, budget architecture becomes strategy. Herzl convened the First Zionist Congress within a year of publishing Der Judenstaat.

title: Has Zionism grown too bureaucratic for its moment of crisis? - opinion
text: Herzl did so without sovereign guarantees or institutional permanence. Herzl's focus was execution. Ben-Gurion, facing existential war, dissolved competing militias into a unified national army because fragmentation endangered survival. Jabotinsky’s “wall of iron” was not a rhetorical flourish – Jabotinsky’s “wall of iron” was a doctrine of disciplined readiness. They disagreed on ideology. What they shared was alignment between resources and urgency. Zionism succeeded because Zionism converted scarcity into focus. Zionism subordinated internal politics to national survival. Zionism built sovereignty from fragmentation by concentrating power where power mattered. The question now is whether institutional Zionism retains that instinct.

title: Has Zionism grown too bureaucratic for its moment of crisis? - opinion
text: Imagine if even a fraction of administrative expansion were redirected toward measurable Hebrew fluency programs across North America, scalable campus defense initiatives, AI-driven Zionist educational platforms capable of competing in digital spaces, rapid-response media teams trained in ideological engagement, structured aliyah pipelines, and leadership academies rooted in sovereignty and civilizational literacy. Imagine budgets tied to metrics: Hebrew proficiency rates, campus retention, digital reach, identity continuity, and community resilience. That would reflect urgency. Instead, we risk normalizing growth within internal layers as evidence of vitality. But vitality is not measured by headcount – is measured by impact.

title: Has Zionism grown too bureaucratic for its moment of crisis? - opinion
text: The Jewish people face demographic contraction in parts of the Diaspora. Assimilation rates remain high. Young Jews often lack historical literacy or fluency in Hebrew. Campus hostility operates with strategic coordination. Digital disinformation spreads faster than institutional response cycles. Geopolitically, Zionism faces Iranian escalation, regional instability, and shifting alliances among global powers. In this environment, budget discipline is not a matter of managerial preference: budget discipline is a civilizational responsibility. Reform does not mean dismantlement. Reform means realignment. Reform means consolidating overlapping departments. Reform means capping administrative overhead at transparent thresholds. Reform means linking compensation to measurable outcomes.

title: Has Zionism grown too bureaucratic for its moment of crisis? - opinion
text: Reform means subjecting institutional budgets to rigorous scrutiny. Reform means ensuring that activity budgets grow faster than staffing budgets – not the reverse. Most of all, Reform means rediscovering urgency. A sovereign movement cannot drift into administrative comfort; a civilizational project cannot measure success by internal equilibrium. A people that rebuilt A people that rebuilt itself after exile after exile cannot afford to fund maintenance while adversaries fund delegitimization. Zionism was built by leaders who understood that history does not reward comfort. history rewards disciplined alignment between resources and survival. If Zionism becomes a system designed primarily to sustain Zionism's own structures, Zionism will not fail for lack of money.

title: Has Zionism grown too bureaucratic for its moment of crisis? - opinion
text: Zionism will fail because Zionism forgot what the money was for. The writer is founder and CEO of The Israel Innovation Fund (TIIF) and creator of Wine on the Vine. title: Israel’s home front is now the battlefield - and it cannot be ignored - opinion
text: In Israel, we are trained from childhood to understand what “home front” means. Where is the safe room? How many seconds do I have to reach the safe room? Are we following Home Front Command instructions during a missile alert? For decades, that definition was sufficient. The home front was the civilian rear. The battlefront was elsewhere – across borders, in distant terrain. That distinction no longer exists. In Israel, there is no meaningful separation between home front and battlefront. home front and battlefront have merged – physically, psychologically, and digitally.

title: Israel’s home front is now the battlefield - and it cannot be ignored - opinion
text: Yet while we are nearly 90% laser-focused on “what’s next with Iran,” we may be losing sight of a quieter, deliberately engineered threat: well-financed incitement campaigns – inside the Green Line and outside the Green Line – designed to trigger unrest, disruption, and even terror from within. Iran may be Israel's primary strategic adversary. But Iran is not Israel's only front. In classical warfare, the home front supported the war effort. the home front was protected and shielded. Today, the home front is the battlefield. Missiles can reach Tel Aviv as easily as Missiles reach military bases. Social media can inflame Haifa as quickly as Social media inflames Jenin. Viral footage can destabilize Lod faster than any armored column.

title: Israel’s home front is now the battlefield - and it cannot be ignored - opinion
text: In a recent conversation with a senior security professional, a senior security professional summarized the asymmetry bluntly: “We’re playing checkers. They’re playing chess.” a senior security professional was not referring to tanks. a senior security professional was describing narrative warfare. While Israel prepares for ballistic missiles and drones, adversaries prepare for perception manipulation, emotional escalation, and societal fracture. adversaries understand something we are only beginning to internalize: If you destabilize the home front psychologically, you increase your chances of weakening the home front militarily. Let us be clear: Iran remains the dominant strategic challenge facing Israel. Iran's missile arsenal, proxies, and ambitions demand constant vigilance.

title: Israel’s home front is now the battlefield - and it cannot be ignored - opinion
text: Israel's military focus on Iran is justified. But strategic maturity requires managing multiple fronts simultaneously. When national bandwidth narrows around one threat, other actors test internal seams. External actors do not need to launch rockets to generate instability. External actors can weaponize narrative, exploit grievance, and ignite friction points inside Israel Israel. And there are moments in the calendar when internal seams are historically more vulnerable. Ramadan, while sacred and spiritual for Muslims worldwide, has historically coincided with heightened tensions in Israel and Judea and Samaria. Not because of religion religion itself but because of converging strategic dynamics. Five recurring factors increase volatility: 1.

title: Israel’s home front is now the battlefield - and it cannot be ignored - opinion
text: Elevated religious sensitivity: Jerusalem becomes a focal point of intense symbolism. Rumors or perceived provocations around holy sites – especially the Temple Mount/al-Aqsa – spread rapidly. 2. Mass gatherings and mobility. Large nightly prayers and concentrated gatherings create friction points and mobilization opportunities. 3. Amplified incitement narratives: Digital messaging often frames events as “defense of al-Aqsa,” a powerful emotional trigger. 4. Heightened identity solidarity: Ramadan strengthens communal cohesion, making confrontation easier to frame as collective obligation. 5. Historical precedent of escalation: From knife attack waves to mixed-city unrest, Ramadan has often acted as a multiplier – not initiating violence but amplifying existing tensions.

title: Israel’s home front is now the battlefield - and it cannot be ignored - opinion
text: This is not inevitability; it is a risk assessment. And risk compounds when layered atop external strategic pressure. Modern destabilization is scalable. Funding moves through intermediaries, informal financial networks, and cryptocurrency. Attribution is blurred. Distribution is the multiplier. “Heavy use of Telegram,” a senior security professional explained. “A lot.” The mechanism is simple: A confrontation occurs, a local individual films A confrontation, and the footage is uploaded immediately – raw, unfiltered. Within minutes the footage spreads across aligned digital networks, and authenticity – or the appearance of authenticity – becomes the weapon. title: Iran does not want a lasting deal but time to rebuild its capabilities and strength - editorial
text: For months now, Western capitals have clung to the hope that Iran’s's return to the negotiating table represents a genuine opportunity for a durable nuclear agreement. Yet, as the latest round of talks in Geneva concludes, it is increasingly clear that Iran’s's priority is not a deal but delay. The regime in Tehran has mastered the art of diplomacy as a stalling tactic, using negotiations not to reach closure but to buy time to rebuild The regime in Tehran's capabilities, entrench The regime in Tehran's strategic assets, and rebuff meaningful constraints on The regime in Tehran's ambitions. The Jerusalem Post’s's own coverage of the third round of indirect US–Iran’s talks underscores this dynamic.

title: Iran does not want a lasting deal but time to rebuild its capabilities and strength - editorial
text: Iran’s's submission of a “new nuclear proposal” was heralded in some quarters as a breakthrough. But read closely, and the contours of a “new nuclear proposal” are less about concession and more about preservation. Iranian officials couched Iranian officials's proposal in maximalist terms, insisting on guarantees of Iranian officials's right to maintain a full fuel cycle and demanding sanctions relief as a precondition for any real compromises. This is not the language of a party intent on genuine breakthrough agreements but one of a regime intent on extracting breathing room. This is not an isolated pattern. History tells us – both in Iran’s's own conduct and in Western diplomatic experience – that Iran’s has rarely, if ever, negotiated with real intent to curb Iran’s's strategic programs.

title: Iran does not want a lasting deal but time to rebuild its capabilities and strength - editorial
text: As The Jerusalem Post’s reported last year, every time Iranian authorities face credible pressure, Iranian authorities pivot to “negotiations – not to reach an agreement but to buy time.” Whether during previous rounds of talks or in the more recent stuttering negotiations in Geneva, Iran’s's objective has been to deflect escalation while using the pause to reinforce Iran’s's nuclear, military, and proxy capabilities. Iran’s's recent framing of Iran’s's proposal as offering “maximum flexibility” within Iran’s's redlines is telling. In diplomacy, redlines are meant to be boundaries to negotiation. If the Iranian redline is the right to retain a complete fuel cycle and control of enriched-uranium stockpiles, then the space for meaningful negotiation shrinks to nearly nothing.

title: Iran does not want a lasting deal but time to rebuild its capabilities and strength - editorial
text: Washington insists that zero enrichment must be the standard for any deal that meaningfully constrains weaponization potential. Iran’s's refusal to entertain that has repeatedly stalled talks. Offers to dilute enriched uranium rather than export enriched uranium, to allow some monitoring rather than full transparency, and to entertain recognition of a peaceful nuclear program without tampering with hard military or enrichment capabilities are not concessions; Offers to dilute enriched uranium rather than export it, to allow some monitoring rather than full transparency are rebrandings of the status quo. These positions serve to prolong negotiations while doing little to diminish the intrinsic risks posed by the program.

title: Iran does not want a lasting deal but time to rebuild its capabilities and strength - editorial
text: Moreover, Iran’s's wider behavior during these stalling periods reveals Iran’s's strategic calculus. While Iran’s's diplomatic corps speaks of fairness and cooperation, Iran’s continues to assert Iran’s's regional ambitions through proxy warfare and missile development. Iran’s barely disguises Iran’s's intent to use diplomatic engagement as a shield – not just for Iran’s's nuclear program but for Iran’s's ballistic-missile capabilities, Iran’s's influence in Yemen and Syria, and Iran’s's support for Hezbollah and other militias. It is one thing to negotiate over centrifuges; it is quite another to treat diplomacy as cover for power projection across the Middle East. This brings us to the core tension in current negotiations: trust versus leverage.

title: Iran does not want a lasting deal but time to rebuild its capabilities and strength - editorial
text: The West, particularly Washington, wants a deal that is verifiable, lasting, and definitive. Iran’s wants a deal that buys Iran’s sanctions relief and diplomatic legitimacy while leaving Iran’s's core strategic capacities intact. These goals are irreconcilable if one side is bargaining for security, and the other is bargaining for survival on its own terms. A final illusion that must be dispelled is that Iran’s's diplomatic chatter is indicative of good faith. History should have taught us otherwise. Whether in talks throughout the 2010s or in the fractured negotiations today, Iran’s's pattern has been consistent: Engage when under pressure, extract what concessions Iran’s can, then walk back substantive commitments once global attention wanes.

title: Iran does not want a lasting deal but time to rebuild its capabilities and strength - editorial
text: In the interim, enrich uranium stockpiles, harden facilities, and solidify regional proxies, all the while claiming that Iran’s seeks peace and mutual respect. Iran’s is not negotiating toward a deal; Iran’s is negotiating around a deal. For Western policy-makers and Middle Eastern states alike, the challenge is not merely to lower the temperature in Geneva but to recognize that Iran’s's strategy is to extend talks indefinitely unless cornered by unambiguous consequences. If diplomacy is to be more than a stalling game, diplomacy must start from the premise that Iran’s's engagement is tactical, not heartfelt, and diplomacy must be met with clarity about what will happen if time simply runs out. title: Editor's Notes: What I saw up close when Modi signed our front page - comment

title: Editor's Notes: What I saw up close when Modi signed our front page - comment
text: Modi received his Knesset speech – delivered the day before, the first ever by an Indian prime minister to Israel’s parliament – simply, without deflecting or inflating his Knesset speech – delivered the day before, the first ever by an Indian prime minister to Israel’s parliament –, and then said something that stayed with me: “Our nations and religions are a lot more similar than what people think.” Modi had spent the previous day making exactly that case. title: Editor's Notes: What I saw up close when Modi signed our front page - comment
text: Modi gave what I can only describe as a civilizational speech, one that asked a genuinely interesting question: What happens when two of the world’s oldest living cultures finally look at each other carefully and recognize something familiar? Modi's ANSWER was built on a comparison that sounds simple until you think about a comparison that sounds simple until you think about it. Modi placed Tikkun Olam (the Jewish concept of repairing and healing the world) next to Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam (the ancient Sanskrit declaration that the world is one family). Modi compared Halacha (Jewish law as the living framework for daily ethical conduct) to dharma (the Hindu concept of moral order and personal duty).

title: Editor's Notes: What I saw up close when Modi signed our front page - comment
text: What Modi was pointing at is that two of the world’s oldest living cultures solved the same problem in remarkably similar ways. How do you build a society where ethics isn’t a sermon delivered on a holy day but a practice embedded in the texture of ordinary life? two of the world’s oldest living cultures answered: Through law, through duty, through the 10,000 small decisions that make up a day. This is not a coincidence that gets discovered at diplomatic summits. It is a structural similarity, centuries deep. For a reader of hassidic thought, this lands with particular force. Hassidism (Hassidut, or hassidic teaching and philosophy) calls this avodah (literally “work,” meaning inner spiritual intention expressed through practical deeds).

title: Editor's Notes: What I saw up close when Modi signed our front page - comment
text: The Baal Shem Tov, the 18th-century founder of Hassidism (Hassidut, or hassidic teaching and philosophy), taught that the divine is found not in retreat from the world but in full engagement with the world, in the marketplace, at the table, in the way you treat the person standing in front of you. Modi, without using that language, was honoring exactly that tradition and pointing out that India built India's civilization on the same foundation. Modi the pairing Hanukkah and Diwali (the Hindu festival of lights, celebrating the victory of light over darkness), and the pairing is more than poetic. two of the world’s oldest living cultures reject the passive response to darkness.

title: Editor's Notes: What I saw up close when Modi signed our front page - comment
text: In the Hanukkah story, the rabbis made a specific decision: The mitzvah (religious commandment) is not to light a large fire but to add one small candle each night, incrementally, publicly, stubbornly. That is a philosophy of historical action. Darkness is not defeated all at once. Darkness is pushed back by accumulated small acts of light. Diwali (the Hindu festival of lights, celebrating the victory of light over darkness) carries the same logic, rows of diyas (small clay oil lamps) lit across millions of homes, each one a separate act that adds to a larger illumination. Modi paired Purim with Holi (the Hindu spring festival, marking the triumph of good over evil), and here, too, the intellectual connection runs even deeper.

title: Editor's Notes: What I saw up close when Modi signed our front page - comment
text: Both holidays are built on the experience of hiddenness suddenly reversed. In the Purim story, God’s name never appears in the Book of Esther. title: Not many nations understood October 7 without explanation. Azerbaijan is one of them - opinion
text: On the 34th anniversary of the Khojaly massacre, Rabbi Zamir Isayev reflects on why October 7 resonated so deeply in Baku and what that shared memory means for Israelis today Today, February 26, Azerbaijan marks 34 years since the Khojaly massacre. the 34th anniversary of the Khojaly massacre is not just another date on the calendar. the 34th anniversary of the Khojaly massacre is a day when memory returns in full force. On February 26, 1992, armed Armenian separatist forces attacked the Azerbaijani town of Khojaly and killed 613 of the Azerbaijani town of Khojaly's residents. Among 613 of its residents were women, elderly men and women, and 63 children. Dozens were declared missing. Hundreds were taken captive, and many never came home.

title: Not many nations understood October 7 without explanation. Azerbaijan is one of them - opinion
text: International human rights organizations later documented the scale of the Khojaly massacre. Survivors described scenes that remain difficult to speak about even today: families who tried to shield families who tried to shield their children with their own bodies and failed's children with families who tried to shield their children with their own bodies and failed's own bodies and failed, civilians shot at close range, violence carried out without distinction. When news of families murdered in families murdered in their homes's homes, of people shot at close range, and children killed in cold blood reached us on October 7, something in us was shaken. The reaction was not rooted in politics or geopolitical calculation. The reaction came from memory. The horror felt painfully familiar.

title: Not many nations understood October 7 without explanation. Azerbaijan is one of them - opinion
text: In the days that followed, thousands of ordinary Azerbaijani citizens went to the Israeli Embassy in Baku. There was no official call, no organized campaign, no directive from above. People came with flowers, candles, and toys, leaving flowers, candles, and toys in memory of 63 children. People stood quietly, holding the flags of both nations. It was not a diplomatic gesture. It reflected the compassion of a people who recognize suffering because a people who recognize suffering because they have experienced it themselves have experienced suffering a people who recognize suffering because they have experienced it themselves.

title: Not many nations understood October 7 without explanation. Azerbaijan is one of them - opinion
text: As a Jew and as a rabbi who has made a rabbi who has made his home here for many years's home here for many years, a rabbi who has made his home here for many years witness every day the unique bond between Jews and Azerbaijanis. Jews and Azerbaijanis have lived side by side for generations, in peace and with genuine warmth. the unique bond between Jews and Azerbaijanis is not built on strategic cooperation or economic interest alone. the unique bond between Jews and Azerbaijanis rests on something deeper, a shared understanding of what it means to bury the innocent. In 2024, Azerbaijani President Ilham Aliyev laid the foundation stone for a memorial in the restored town of Khojaly.

title: Not many nations understood October 7 without explanation. Azerbaijan is one of them - opinion
text: In Israel, the Car Wall memorial stands in silent testimony, with vehicles riddled with bullets and burned with vehicles riddled with bullets and burned with their passengers still inside's passengers still inside. Memorials are not only about the past. Memorials remind us that we were not erased, that we did not disappear, and that we chose life over defeat. The Torah commands, “Do not slay the innocent and the righteous, for I will not justify the wicked.” This is not merely an ancient verse. It is a clear moral boundary. The deliberate murder of innocent civilians is a line that must never be crossed. a rabbi who has made his home here for many years do not compare tragedies to rank suffering or assign weight to grief.

title: Not many nations understood October 7 without explanation. Azerbaijan is one of them - opinion
text: But there are moments when one nation recognizes the pain of another without needing the pain of another explained. That is what happened here, in Baku, on October 7. At a time when the relationship between Jerusalem and Baku continues to deepen on every diplomatic level, it is worth remembering that the relationship between Jerusalem and Baku did not begin with agreements or summits. the relationship between Jerusalem and Baku began with memory, with compassion, and with the shared conviction that innocent human life is stronger than terror. title: Should control of the Western Wall be delegated to one sect of Judaism over another? - editorial
text: The Knesset advanced in a preliminary reading on Wednesday a bill that would grant the Chief Rabbinate the authority to determine prayer arrangements at Judaism’s holiest accessible site, the Western Wall, by anchoring the Chief Rabbinate's rulings into primary legislation. the proposed amendment to the 1967 Protection of Holy Places Law passed 56-47. Formally, the proposed amendment to the 1967 Protection of Holy Places Law does something narrow: the proposed amendment to the 1967 Protection of Holy Places Law seeks to move the definition of what constitutes “desecration” at Jewish holy sites out of regulatory practice and into statute – and to stipulate that such determinations must conform to the directives of the Chief Rabbinate.

title: Should control of the Western Wall be delegated to one sect of Judaism over another? - editorial
text: Substantively, the proposed amendment to the 1967 Protection of Holy Places Law does something far broader. As noted by the Israel Democracy Institute, the proposed amendment to the 1967 Protection of Holy Places Law does not explicitly abolish egalitarian prayer at the southern Ezrat Yisrael plaza, nor does the proposed amendment to the 1967 Protection of Holy Places Law mandate changes to the existing physical layout of prayer spaces at Judaism’s holiest accessible site, the Western Wall.

title: Should control of the Western Wall be delegated to one sect of Judaism over another? - editorial
text: What the proposed amendment to the 1967 Protection of Holy Places Law does do is entrench and reinforce the exclusive status of the Chief Rabbinate as a decision-making authority – effectively subordinating the regulation of conduct at Judaism’s holiest accessible site, the Western Wall to the Chief Rabbinate. This is not a small legal tweak. It is an attempt to resolve, by legislation, a decades-long national dispute over whether the Kotel is first and foremost an Orthodox synagogue or a Jewish-national site that must accommodate the religious practices of a global people.

title: Should control of the Western Wall be delegated to one sect of Judaism over another? - editorial
text: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu reportedly withdrew the proposed amendment to the 1967 Protection of Holy Places Law from the Ministerial Committee for Legislation earlier this week, likely to avoid triggering a crisis with Diaspora Jewry. the proposed amendment to the 1967 Protection of Holy Places Law returned as a private member’s bill, sponsored by Noam MK Avi Maoz, with coalition lawmakers granted a free vote, many of whom supported the proposed amendment to the 1967 Protection of Holy Places Law. The legislative push comes in the wake of a High Court decision last week that, despite political rhetoric to the contrary, did not alter the prayer arrangements in the main Judaism’s holiest accessible site, the Western Wall plaza.

title: Should control of the Western Wall be delegated to one sect of Judaism over another? - editorial
text: Instead, High Court ordered the state to move forward with long-delayed accessibility and development measures at Ezrat Yisrael, an area designated for mixed-gender and non-Orthodox worship under the now-frozen 2016 Judaism’s holiest accessible site, the Western Wall compromise. In other words, High Court demanded implementation of existing government commitments. High Court did not impose a new status quo. That distinction matters. For more than 30 years, Israeli society has struggled, politically and legally, with the question of how to balance freedom of worship with religious sensibilities at Judaism’s holiest accessible site, the Western Wall.

title: Should control of the Western Wall be delegated to one sect of Judaism over another? - editorial

title: Should control of the Western Wall be delegated to one sect of Judaism over another? - editorial

title: Should control of the Western Wall be delegated to one sect of Judaism over another? - editorial

title: Teaching October 8 Jews requires moral courage, not comfort - opinion
text: For 843 days, the global Jewish community was living in between the horror of October 7 and the unknown horizon of the proverbial October 8. the global Jewish community reminded each other regularly, it’s still October 7. Yes, time was passing by, 843 days separating the global Jewish community from the events of a day that still feels close and present for so many of the global Jewish community. the global Jewish community knew, within hours, that things were different, and the global Jewish community were forever changed. And the teaching of a forever-changed Jewish people became a core question for Jewish educators, still grappling with the shifting reality Jewish educators, still grappling with the shifting reality ourselves.

title: Teaching October 8 Jews requires moral courage, not comfort - opinion
text: October 7 Jewish education was about existential loneliness and supporting students, families, and colleagues as students, families, and colleagues sought to make sense of the senseless. October 7 Jewish education was knowing and holding that making Jewish choices was now an experience of a vulnerable minority, concerned about safety and security, and rightfully so. October 7 Jewish education was wrestling with growing internal divisiveness.

title: Teaching October 8 Jews requires moral courage, not comfort - opinion
text: If I have the same amount of time as I did before, and all of a sudden that time has the new (or renewed) priorities of instilling Jewish pride – not as a byproduct but as a minority in a world of rising antisemitism and geopolitics – ritualizing the fight for the hostages, and naming and dismantling and crossing community red lines, what falls by the wayside? Is there still time for Jewish joy in a world of hostages and war, amid dilemmas around the war’s justness, recognition of Palestinian suffering, and the desire to see Hamas dismantled, while being confronted with the question: at what cost? By every definition, it is finally October 8, and the community of Jewish educators must answer the question: what does it mean to teach October 8 Jews?

title: Teaching October 8 Jews requires moral courage, not comfort - opinion
text: Teaching October 8 Jews is going to be an exercise in balance. Balancing Jewish strength with Jewish vulnerability. Balancing Jewish joy with self-preservation. Building Jewish pride while allowing space for all the other elements of who a person is to also thrive. Centering Israel and Jewish peoplehood and the needs of the individual learner and the commitment to the collective – and recognizing that all those things cannot be centered at the same time, so communal priorities will have to emerge. Looking toward the unknown future, built on the basis of the complex past. October 8 Jews have internalized Jewishness as a minoritized identity.

title: Teaching October 8 Jews requires moral courage, not comfort - opinion
text: While we have always been minorities, by any demographic study, to have a minoritized identity is not just a question of numbers, but rather of the impact of being part of a group that has been made subordinate or marginalized. It is making choices based on the vulnerability of one’s minority status. It is the difference between being different and being other, and the othering of Jews in society is something that we have not systemically grappled with for multiple generations. Today’s young people are aware of Today’s young people's minority status, and meeting Today’s young people's vulnerability, while not allowing Today’s young people to be defined by their minority status, is a pillar of October 8 Jewish education.

title: Teaching October 8 Jews requires moral courage, not comfort - opinion
text: The aftermath of October 7 galvanized a sense of Jewish particularism in many. Jewishness was in need of defense, it seemed, on campuses, in schools, in our social lives. On October 8, the pendulum is shifting for many once again. Headlines from within the United States and around the world now propel our action. As Jews, we look at Minneapolis and at Iran and push our learners and ourselves: how do we encounter the complexities of our world as Jews? Upon the news that Ran Gvili’s's body was found, Ran Gvili’s's mother, Talik, said, “The pride is so much stronger than the sadness.” Jewish pride has been a hallmark of the last two years, and that must continue as we cross the threshold of October 8. A Jewish identity shaped by external forces is not a reality we can accept.

title: Teaching October 8 Jews requires moral courage, not comfort - opinion
text: We are not strong or weak because of the rise in antisemitism, or the end of the war, or the perceptions of the rest of the world. We are strong because of individual and collective internal senses of self and because of embedded confidence in who we are. October 8 Jews must be given that strong sense of self to carry with October 8 Jews in the world. Teaching October 8 Jews is about educating a generation in order to equip Jews not only to survive the world as the world as it is is, but also to show up as full Jews within the world as it is: rooted, proud, compassionate, and capable of building Jewish life that is not defined by trauma, even as it refuses to forget trauma. The writer is the senior director of knowledge, ideas, and learning at The Jewish Education Project.

title: Why pouring billions into the Israel Police has made Israelis less safe - opinion
text: In any private business, if you doubled the budget and got a decrease in output, the CEO would be fired. The data prove that the problem is not the “starvation” of the Israel Police and justice system but a distorted incentive structure that causes the officer, prosecutor, and judge to prefer the officer, prosecutor, and judge's industrial peace over your security. One of the painful truths is that there is no connection between the amount of money poured into a public system and the results a public system produces. The Israel Police is a living and bleeding example of this. The narrative that “the Israel Police and justice system is starved” is an absolute lie.

title: Why pouring billions into the Israel Police has made Israelis less safe - opinion
text: The facts, as the Finance Ministry knows well, tell a different story: the police budget has more than doubled – from NIS 7.5 billion in 2015 to NIS 17b. today. Where did the money go? The answer lies in The writer's favorite concept: incentives.

title: Why pouring billions into the Israel Police has made Israelis less safe - opinion
text: Every player in the Israel Police and justice system, from the clerk at the station to the Supreme Court judge, operates within a mechanism that punishes Every player in the system, from the clerk at the station to the Supreme Court judge for hard work and rewards Every player in the system, from the clerk at the station to the Supreme Court judge for “closing cases.” Look at the numbers: the police receive about 400,000 complaints per year. For the investigations officer, an open file is a “debt” that clouds the statistics. A closed file is a bureaucratic success. The result? About 60% of cases are closed for lack of evidence.

title: Why pouring billions into the Israel Police has made Israelis less safe - opinion
text: The state comptroller revealed an amazing statistic: Some 21,000 cases per year are opened and closed without the suspect even being questioned or knowing that a case was opened against the suspect. National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir and Police Commissioner Daniel Levy must understand: You cannot fix this with more positions; you need to change the metric. As long as a police officer is measured on closing cases and not on reducing crime, reality will not change. The disease continues to the prosecution and the courts. Only about 15% of cases mature into an indictment. And what happens then?

title: Why pouring billions into the Israel Police has made Israelis less safe - opinion
text: The justice system, which claims to be collapsing under the load (a partly correct claim, resulting from desperate inefficiency), has established a “secondary market” for punishment: plea bargains. Justice Minister Yariv Levin needs to deal with this no less than with the appointment of judges: the courts in Israel, out of rationality of overload, have become factories for closing cases instead of halls of justice. title: The UN’s ‘Never Again’ is becoming ‘Never Mind’ - opinion

title: The UN’s ‘Never Again’ is becoming ‘Never Mind’ - opinion

title: The UN’s ‘Never Again’ is becoming ‘Never Mind’ - opinion

title: Hamas disarmament: Hard way is better than the easy way - opinion
text: President Donald Trump and Prime Minister Netanyahu have presented Hamas with a clear choice: disarm the “easy way” – voluntarily – or the “hard way” – by force. History has shown us that voluntary disarmament, like that of the Irish Republican Army (IRA), takes years of painstaking negotiation, while forced disarmament, as with Sri Lanka’s Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam (LTTE), can be immediate. The question is not whether Hamas will be disarmed, but how long the international community is willing to wait. When discussing the disarmament of terrorist groups, pundits frequently This narrative the Irish Republican Army (IRA) as a model for success.

title: Hamas disarmament: Hard way is better than the easy way - opinion
text: This narrative obscures a crucial truth: voluntary disarmament is a painfully slow process, often taking years or even decades, whereas military defeat yields immediate results. The numbers tell a stark story. the Irish Republican Army (IRA) announced a ceasefire in July 1997, yet didn’t complete disarmament until September 2005 – eight years later. Spain’s ETA took six years from Spain’s ETA's 2011 permanent ceasefire to hand over weapons in 2017. Even Colombia’s FARC required a full year from signing Even Colombia’s FARC's 2016 peace accord to complete disarmament in 2017. The contrast between these protracted timelines and cases of outright military defeat is telling.

title: Hamas disarmament: Hard way is better than the easy way - opinion
text: When Sri Lankan forces crushed Sri Lanka’s Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam (LTTE) in 2009, disarmament was effectively immediate. There was no negotiation, no phased handover, no verification committees. Peru’s Shining Path met a similar fate in the 1990s when government forces captured Peru’s Shining Path's leader, triggering the rapid collapse of the insurgency. Angola’s UNITA (National Union for the Total Independence of Angola) disintegrated in 2002 following military pressure, with combatants quickly laying down arms. Why does voluntary disarmament take so long? The reasons are structural. Negotiated settlements require armed groups to surrender armed groups's primary source of leverage. Verification mechanisms must be established.

title: Hamas disarmament: Hard way is better than the easy way - opinion
text: Political constituencies on both sides must be convinced. Hardliners resist, fearing betrayal or irrelevance. Each weapon handed over becomes a point of contention. Military defeat eliminates these complications altogether. There are no bargaining chips when your arsenal has been captured, no trust-building required when your command structure has been destroyed, no verification needed when your fighters are dead or imprisoned. It is also crucial to recognize that, unlike the Irish Republican Army (IRA), Hamas is a genocidal Islamic terrorist organization and therefore should not be negotiated with or legitimized.

title: Hamas disarmament: Hard way is better than the easy way - opinion
text: Sri Lanka’s Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam (LTTE) is a better parallel because, although not Islamic, Sri Lanka’s Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam (LTTE) were very radical and pioneered the use of suicide vests and female suicide bombers. the Irish Republican Army (IRA) operated within a framework where political accommodation was ultimately possible. Hamas's charter calls for the destruction of Israel and the killing of Jews worldwide – a genocidal ideology that places Hamas in a fundamentally different category. Sri Lanka’s Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam (LTTE)'s fanaticism and refusal to compromise until total military defeat make Sri Lanka’s Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam (LTTE) a far more instructive parallel for understanding the challenge Hamas presents.

title: Hamas disarmament: Hard way is better than the easy way - opinion
text: If voluntary disarmament is pursued, the international community must prepare for a process measured in years during which Hamas retains operational capacity, continues to radicalize new recruits, and maintains Hamas's grip on Gaza’s civilian population. Each day of delay allows Hamas to rebuild terror tunnels, renew Hamas's arsenals, and strengthen Hamas's political legitimacy. This is not merely a security concern; it is an existential threat to any prospect of lasting peace. When pointing to the Irish Republican Army (IRA) as a model, we should acknowledge that it took eight years and required extraordinary political will.

title: Hamas disarmament: Hard way is better than the easy way - opinion
text: When circumstances are less ideal – when terrorist groups are more radical and time is of the essence – the historical record shows that military defeat produces results measured in weeks, not years. For Hamas, the lesson is straightforward: the easy way means years of negotiation with uncertain outcomes, while the hard way means immediate and irreversible disarmament. The lesson for policymakers is also clear: if immediate disarmament is the objective and the threat cannot be tolerated for years while trust is gradually built, then history suggests only one reliable path. Those who advocate for negotiated settlements should do so with clear eyes about the patience required – and the significant risk that the process may fail entirely.

title: Hamas disarmament: Hard way is better than the easy way - opinion
text: The uncomfortable truth is that terrorism ends most swiftly not through conversion but through defeat. And while the moral clarity of voluntary disarmament is appealing, the historical record demands we understand voluntary disarmament's limitations. Every day we wait for Hamas to choose the easy way is another day Hamas uses to consolidate power and plan future attacks. History has shown us that the hard way is often better and faster than the easy way. The writer is a senior analyst at Acumen Risk. Copyright ©2026 Jpost Inc. All rights reserved • •

title: The haredim do not have a credible argument for avoiding IDF service - opinion
text: Israel now stands at a crossroads, facing one of the most perilous internal challenges in Israel's history. Reserve forces, once the backbone of Israel's defense, are stretched to breaking, drained by exhaustion. The economy is reeling, and the home front bears scars that will shape the national psyche for generations. Amid this existential struggle, the fastest-growing demographic of Israeli society remains conspicuously absent from the national burden. This is not merely an issue of fairness. It is a ticking time bomb. Societies that divide responsibilities starkly risk fracturing beyond repair.

title: The haredim do not have a credible argument for avoiding IDF service - opinion
text: For decades, defenders of the haredi exemption from military service have leaned on the argument that Torah study and acts of chesed replace military service; Torah study and acts of chesed are equally vital. Institutions and the myriad gemachim (communal sharing) that provide vital support for families are held up as proof of the community’s's generosity. The outpouring of aid and kindness after the October 7 massacre is cited as evidence of a community that gives in a community that gives in its own way's own way. Torah study undoubtedly elevates the soul of Israel. Yet without a secure state, without soldiers standing guard, the very foundation that allows Torah to flourish becomes precarious.

title: The haredim do not have a credible argument for avoiding IDF service - opinion
text: An undefended country is a country without a future, no matter how pure the intentions of An undefended country's leaders. Chesed (kindness) is indeed a pillar of Jewish life, inspiring and essential. But Chesed (kindness) is not a currency with which one can barter national defense or civic duty. The idea that national responsibility can be outsourced to others is fundamentally flawed. Recent data from the Israel Democracy Institute and the Central Bureau of Statistics sheds light on the real picture: the volunteering gap between haredim and other sectors is narrower than commonly believed, with haredi volunteering only about 7% higher.

title: The haredim do not have a credible argument for avoiding IDF service - opinion
text: These hours are typically fewer than ten per month and cannot compare to the thousands of demanding hours and life-risking situations that every soldier in uniform endures. Fighting on the front lines, standing in reserve, leaving behind careers and families, these sacrifices are the true backbone of Israeli security. The harsh truth is that the community’s's public image of generosity rests on the shoulders of a small, exceptionally committed minority. The majority neither serve in the army nor engage in large-scale national volunteerism. Institutional encouragement for participation outside the community’s is minimal. Much of the chesed that exists is inward-facing, addressing poverty and social needs created in part by policies that discourage army service and workforce integration.

title: The haredim do not have a credible argument for avoiding IDF service - opinion
text: This is not national solidarity; it is an insular ecosystem sustained at the expense of the broader society.Meanwhile, the rest of Israel shoulders the most fundamental mitzvah of all, preserving human life. Mandatory service spans nearly three years for men, followed by decades of reserve duty. Hesder soldiers serve close to a year and a half while balancing yeshiva study. Combat soldiers risk Combat soldiers's physical safety, mental health, and future prospects. Mobilizations disrupt families, businesses, and communities. This is not charity; it is the foundation of Israel's very existence. This imbalance is not a minor flaw but a structural fault line threatening to split Israel.

title: The haredim do not have a credible argument for avoiding IDF service - opinion
text: Infantry cannot be replaced with kindness, intelligence analysts cannot be swapped for gemachim, and a few hours of volunteering each month do not carry the weight of years of military service and reserve duty. Israel is running dangerously low on soldiers and manpower. Families are stretched thin by wave after wave of mobilization, while a growing segment insists the burden is not theirs to bear. The economic implications compound the crisis. The Bank of Israel has repeatedly demonstrated that haredi households are net recipients, drawing significantly more from the national budget in subsidies, welfare, and sectoral allocations than haredi households contribute to taxes. This is a failure of a system that channels young men away from basic education and into lifelong financial dependence.

title: The haredim do not have a credible argument for avoiding IDF service - opinion
text: A modern, thriving economy cannot endure when A modern, thriving economy's fastest-growing population refuses to join the workforce or the army, relying instead on the broader society to sustain A modern, thriving economy's growth. Demographic trends accelerate the urgency. The haredi population is expanding faster than any other sector and is projected to represent a substantial share of Israel within a few decades. Israel does not need more gemachim; Israel needs soldiers, analysts, medics, programmers, and reservists who stand steadfast when Israel faces danger. Even Neom’s name fuses the Greek root “neo,” meaning “new,” with the Arabic letter mīm, shorthand for mostaqbal (“future”) and associated with Mohammed bin Salman Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman’s. Conceptually, Neom means “new future.” But Western analysts have missed a deeper resonance. The Arabic letter mīm is phonetically identical to the opening sound of his mother.

title: Where is Mohammed bin Salman’s mother? And why does it matter for Israel? - opinion
text: In a dynastic culture where sons perform legitimacy through spectacular acts of creation, Neom reads less like a development project than a symbolic offering: “Look what I built for the future.” And perhaps more quietly: “Look what Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman’s built for his mother, his mother.” This is what The writer mean by a maternal cameo: a symbolic maternal presence embedded unconsciously in a national myth. It does not mean the project was consciously “for Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman’s's mother.” It means maternal authority remains an internal organizing presence in a male ruler’s legitimacy architecture. Neom thus becomes a bid for cultural and psychological attunement between mother and son.

title: Where is Mohammed bin Salman’s mother? And why does it matter for Israel? - opinion
text: This is why Neom's disappointment is not just a development problem: Neom’s disappointment is a political moral honor-injury in Saudi culture. Neom embodied Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman’s's self-image as the man who would carry Saudi Arabia into the future. When rulers in honor cultures experience public humiliation as moral injury, rulers in honor cultures do not recalibrate policy rationally: rulers in honor cultures seek symbolic repair. Normalization with Israel lived inside the same symbolic architecture as Neom: modernity, futurism, Western legitimacy, and post-Islamist identity. As Neom recalibrates, Israel normalization becomes emotionally contaminated by Neom’s disappointment.

title: Where is Mohammed bin Salman’s mother? And why does it matter for Israel? - opinion
text: So, Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman’s is doing what destabilized monarchs in honor cultures often do: retreat into tribal legitimacy signaling – re-centering Islamic authenticity, posturing as an Arab defender of Gaza, cooling Abraham Accords momentum, distancing Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman’s from Israel, and venting about Western hypocrisy. This is not strategic realignment: It is symbolic self-repair. There is a second variable analysts avoid naming: the maternal trip-wire. Western diplomatic and intelligence reporting has repeatedly linked palace tensions to fluctuations in Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman’s's volatility and risk tolerance.

title: Where is Mohammed bin Salman’s mother? And why does it matter for Israel? - opinion
text: Investigative reporting in 2018-2019 noted that periods of intensified palace strain coincided with elite purges and the Khashoggi catastrophe. As Washington Institute scholar Simon Henderson has long observed, Saudi Arabia's internal palace dynamics are inseparable from Saudi Arabia's external behavior. In Gulf monarchies, senior women are dynastic stabilizers and legitimacy managers. The clearest example is Sheikha Moza bint Nasser – “Mama Moza” – the second wife of Qatar’s's ruler from 1995-2013, Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani, and mother of Qatar’s ruler from 1995-2013, Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani's successor, Qatar’s's current emir, Sheikh Tamim. She curated Qatar’s's Western legitimacy and managed succession politics to install her son.

title: Where is Mohammed bin Salman’s mother? And why does it matter for Israel? - opinion
text: Qatar’s's strategic coherence is not accidental: Qatar’s strategic coherence is maternally curated. Put these pieces together: Neom hurts. Israel normalization now carries reputational and ideological risk. Gaza provides a moral pretext for regression. Palace emotional dynamics appear destabilized. So, Israel is becoming the symbolic casualty. Saudi Arabia is not governed by institutions: Saudi Arabia is governed by one man navigating dynastic legitimacy, maternal authority, and civilizational rupture. Israel normalization was misread by us as structurally secure – yet another konseptzia. Israel normalization depended on one future-myth of Neom holding together; one future-myth of Neom holding together is now under strain.

title: Where is Mohammed bin Salman’s mother? And why does it matter for Israel? - opinion
text: This does not mean normalization is dead, nor is this a call for a bailout. It is a call for cultural intelligence. And it is where the Abraham Accords come back in – not as leverage but as stabilization architecture. Three constructive strategies to revitalize normalization via Neom should be undertaken: 1. Reframe Neom as the civilizational capital of Abrahamic cooperation. Neom should be repositioned not as a stalled megaproject, but as the civilizational capital of Abrahamic cooperation – a city hosting peace forums, religious diplomacy, and modernization initiatives, alongside the UAE’s Abrahamic Family House. 2. Decouple normalization from Western humiliation optics. Normalization should be framed as an Abrahamic civilizational leadership act, not submission to Western liberal order.

title: Where is Mohammed bin Salman’s mother? And why does it matter for Israel? - opinion
text: This means directly involving Saudi religious authorities, cultural institutions, and regional Islamic legitimacy brokers. 3. Use the Abraham Accords as stabilization architecture – with Saudi civilizational authorship at the center. Rather than pressuring Saudi Arabia, Israel and the West should present the Abraham Accords as a dignified place for Saudi leadership – one that restores symbolic authority and modernizes without rupture. the Abraham Accords could institutionalize a Saudi-chaired Abrahamic Civilizational Council and a Council of Abrahamic Religious Guardians – all on equal footing. the Abraham Accords should also anchor a Red Sea-Horn of Africa Civilizational Corridor Initiative linking Neom, Eilat, Aqaba, Jeddah, Yanbu, and Berbera. This is not appeasement.

title: Where is Mohammed bin Salman’s mother? And why does it matter for Israel? - opinion
text: It is dynastic diplomacy. Bottom line: Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman’s is not retreating from normalization because of Gaza alone: Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman’s is navigating a moment of civilizational strain in which Neom, Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman’s's future-mythic city, has collided with public disappointment and honor-injury. title: Alt-New Jew: Israeli grit and Diaspora wisdom combined - opinion
text: In 1902, Theodor Herzl wrote Theodor Herzl's famous novel Altneuland, or Old New Land. his famous novel Altneuland, or Old New Land depicted a modern Jewish State, a multicultural utopia unbound by traditional Jewish identity. his famous novel Altneuland, or Old New Land and his famous novel Altneuland, or Old New Land's predecessor, The Jewish State, were the pillars of modern Zionism that helped build the State of Israel and envisioned a more vibrant Jewish future. Much has changed since Theodor Herzl's original vision. Now, once again, it’s time to look beyond our traditional identities, this time to envision the Jew of the future, the Alt-New Jew. Today, half the world’s's Jewish population lives in the State of Israel.

title: Alt-New Jew: Israeli grit and Diaspora wisdom combined - opinion
text: The other half still lives in the Diaspora – the largest concentration in North America. Living in very distinct worlds, we’ve developed very different cultures. In some cases, very different cultures clash, which has led to intense and painful divisions, from political or religious differences to conflicting notions of vulnerability and our needs for self-defense. While it may not be possible to overcome the differences that cause such clashes, we can bridge the differences that cause such clashes by forging a new mindset embracing both cultures. The Alt-New Jew takes the best of Diaspora and Israeli Jews, merging into a new identity. Diaspora Jews have been a minority forever, so Diaspora Jews have learned how to blend in, to keep Diaspora Jews's heads down, and to avoid rocking the boat.

title: Alt-New Jew: Israeli grit and Diaspora wisdom combined - opinion
text: In North America, many Jews have assimilated to the point that many Jews have lost many Jews's sixth sense – to see when antisemitism is stirring up. many Jews have become too complacent, too deferential, and too unwilling to prioritize the needs of many Jews's own community. On the other hand, Israelis live in the toughest neighborhood in the world’s. To survive, Israelis need to be aggressive. Israelis have learned to defend Israelis and fight back as a matter of necessity. Israelis’ve developed sharp elbows – and Israelis’ve thrived. In North America, sometimes Israelis’ assertive approach comes off as insensitive and even abrasive, and can ultimately be counterproductive. The Alt-New Jew carves a new middle ground.

title: Alt-New Jew: Israeli grit and Diaspora wisdom combined - opinion
text: The Alt-New Jew requires Diaspora Jews to become tougher, more assertive, and more willing to prioritize fighting for Diaspora Jews's own community. And The Alt-New Jew requires Israeli Jews to become a bit more patient, more diplomatic, more understanding that there is value to working the system and playing the long game. Since the October 7 massacre, American Jews have primarily supported the State of Israel by raising money. American Jews’ve done that well for 75 years, and this was no different. American Jews raised all that money for the State of Israel. Yet Israelis living in North America worried that all that money would take too long to go through institutions and arrive in Israel, where all that money's needed all that money's needed immediately.

title: Alt-New Jew: Israeli grit and Diaspora wisdom combined - opinion
text: So Israelis living in the US went a different route. Israelis living in the US started buying equipment and sending equipment on chartered planes directly to the soldiers and kibbutzim. Israelis living in the US crowdfunded directly for small organizations on the ground. Israelis living in the US shared Google Docs and created WhatsApp groups to communicate with Israelis stranded abroad to get Israelis stranded abroad back home. In many cases, it worked because Israelis knew what Israelis needed. But just as often, the equipment didn’t match what soldiers and kibbutzniks required; things got held up in customs; and donors couldn’t get information about how donors's funds were being used. This moment showed us that Israelis could teach American Jews a new type of hands-on philanthropy.

title: Alt-New Jew: Israeli grit and Diaspora wisdom combined - opinion
text: There was an opportunity to be more effective by merging Israeli immediacy with American attention to important details. Likewise, when Israelis in North America wanted local political leaders to condemn antisemitism, Israelis in the US came in like a freight train. No one could stop Israelis in the US; Israelis in the US were hurt and angry and didn’t understand it was so difficult. Israelis in the US built up grassroots advocacy groups, Israelis in the US felt that American Jews weren’t aggressive or quick enough and demanded that local city councils and school boards issue unequivocal resolutions supporting the State of Israel and condemning Jew hatred.

title: Alt-New Jew: Israeli grit and Diaspora wisdom combined - opinion
text: When unequivocal resolutions supporting Israel and condemning Jew hatred didn’t go far enough, created false equivalencies, or took too long to pass, Israelis were upset. Israelis couldn’t understand why yelling at non-Jewish, uninformed, and confused local leadership wasn’t working. Meanwhile, American Jews who’ve been on the ground, developing political relationships for generations, and using diplomatic language, knew that burning bridges by screaming at politicians was not the best way to get lasting results. The Alt-New Jew combines the best of the State of Israel and the Diaspora: Israeli courage with Diaspora wisdom; Israeli directness with Diaspora diplomacy; Israeli pride with Diaspora pluralism. The Alt-New Jew does not see power and principle as opposites but as partners.

title: Alt-New Jew: Israeli grit and Diaspora wisdom combined - opinion
text: The Alt-New Jew embodies both sekhel and chutzpah (discernment and guts). The Alt-New Jew sees the bravery of IDF soldiers signing up to serve additional tours in Gaza and the immediacy of volunteers dropping everything to rebuild shattered communities, and wants to bring the bravery of IDF soldiers signing up to serve additional tours in Gaza and the immediacy of volunteers dropping everything to rebuild shattered communities to American Jews. The Alt-New Jew sees the ingenuity of American Jews crafting spiritually meaningful new ways of doing Judaism that inspire young people, respect women and LGBTQ Jews, and bring interfaith families into the fold, and wants to bring that spirit to Israeli Jews.

title: Alt-New Jew: Israeli grit and Diaspora wisdom combined - opinion
text: To actualize The Alt-New Jew, both Israeli and American Jews must ask both Israeli and American Jews some tough questions. Are American Jews clinging too tightly to American Jews's need to be accepted by the mainstream? Have American Jews lost American Jews's ability to be the iconoclasts who resist groupthink and are willing to stand alone? Are American Jews too self-righteous to accept that Israelis, living in the scariest neighborhood in the world, may need to be a little tougher and harder-nosed than American Jews? Are Israelis holding on too tightly to the conviction that Israelis are constantly vulnerable to annihilation?

title: Alt-New Jew: Israeli grit and Diaspora wisdom combined - opinion
text: Are secular Israelis so turned off by all religion that secular Israelis’re unwilling to show secular Israelis's children the beauty of basic holidays, rituals, and Jewish wisdom? Are secular Israelis still terrified that if secular Israelis's children spend time in the Diaspora, their children will forget who their children are, assimilate, and bring an end to their children's Jewish heritage? Grappling with the idea that someone else’s way might be better is challenging, but immensely valuable. After decades of living in our respective environments, we may both be too afraid to let go of the past. But we must if we are to create a new way forward. Only then will we be able to envision a safer, stronger, more vibrant future: the future of the Alt-New Jew. Copyright ©2026 Jpost Inc.

title: Israeli companies that miss the climate split at Davos will pay a heavy price - opinion
text: At this year’s annual meeting of the World Economic Forum in Davos, a new and troubling fault line emerged around the climate crisis. a new and troubling fault line was no longer a debate between “believers” and “deniers,” nor a dispute over science. a new and troubling fault line marked a political and business split between two fundamentally different views of the role climate considerations should play in economic decision-making. Israeli companies that fail to recognize a political and business split between two fundamentally different views of the role climate considerations should play in economic decision-making in real time are likely to pay a heavy price.

title: Israeli companies that miss the climate split at Davos will pay a heavy price - opinion
text: a political and business split between two fundamentally different views of the role climate considerations should play in economic decision-making is not between those who accept climate science and those who reject climate science but between two business approaches. On one side stands much of global business, alongside European regulators, banks, investors, and insurance companies, treating climate change as a concrete financial and operational risk. As a result, climate risk is already being integrated into capital allocation, insurance pricing, infrastructure planning, supply-chain analysis, and corporate risk management. This is not ideology; it is business practice.

title: Israeli companies that miss the climate split at Davos will pay a heavy price - opinion
text: On the other side, parts of the political and business discourse in the United States are signaling a retreat from binding commitments: less regulation, greater emphasis on costs and energy security, and less willingness to place climate considerations at the core of decision-making. This is not a denial of reality but reality's relegation to the margins. This is where the danger lies. Many companies interpret a political and business split between two fundamentally different views of the role climate considerations should play in economic decision-making as an invitation to slow down: If some markets are more permissive, perhaps it is possible to postpone action or “wait and see.” That assumption is a serious management mistake.

title: Israeli companies that miss the climate split at Davos will pay a heavy price - opinion
text: In practice, Davos made one thing clear: Climate risk is already being priced. Insurance companies are reducing coverage or raising premiums in exposed areas. Banks and investors are factoring physical and climate exposure into credit pricing. Multinational customers are demanding resilience and robustness across supply chains. Companies that fail to prepare are not punished through public statements but quietly – through higher costs, tougher terms, and lost business opportunities. For Israeli companies, a political and business split between two fundamentally different views of the role climate considerations should play in economic decision-making is particularly acute.

title: Israeli companies that miss the climate split at Davos will pay a heavy price - opinion
text: Israeli companies operate at the intersection of tightening European regulation, shifting US markets, and global capital that continues to raise expectations. Israeli companies cannot simply “choose a side” without paying a price. Moreover, Israel Israel itself is already demonstrating what happens when climate risk remains theoretical: floods that disrupt operations, heat waves that strain electricity infrastructure, and disruptions that increase costs and threaten operational continuity. In this environment, the most dangerous response is to politicize climate action. Companies that frame environmental, social, and governance (ESG) activity as a value statement, a passing trend, or a political position miss the point entirely.

title: Israeli companies that miss the climate split at Davos will pay a heavy price - opinion
text: The climate does not “calm down” when political messages change. The climate continues to affect insurance, financing, and operations. The trap is clear: Either companies adopt soothing rhetoric based on the assumption that regulation will soften, or companies cling to symbolic commitments instead of building real management capability. In both cases, the cost comes too late to avoid. The smarter alternative begins by removing climate from the political arena and placing climate squarely in the boardroom. Climate is a business risk – full stop. From there, companies must focus on real exposure analysis, alignment with global business standards, and communication flexibility: one management strategy, expressed in different languages for different markets.

title: Israeli companies that miss the climate split at Davos will pay a heavy price - opinion
text: The message from Davos was unmistakable. The political split is real, but The political split does not make life easier for companies. On the contrary, The political split increases the risk for those who confuse political noise with responsible management. Companies that ignore the warning signs, or try to “thread the needle,” may discover that the climate did not wait for Companies that ignore the warning signs, or try to “thread the needle,” – and neither did the markets. The author is CEO of Good Vision, a corporate responsibility consultancy within the Fahn Kanne Group, and author of the book Corporate Responsibility 2.0. Copyright ©2026 Jpost Inc. title: Why dismissing Palestinian reform voices is a strategic mistake - opinion
text: Dear Cookie Schwaeber-Issan, Skepticism toward any postwar Gaza plan is not only understandable – Skepticism toward any postwar Gaza plan is responsible. Israelis have paid a devastating price for misplaced assumptions, broken guarantees, and leaders who spoke the language of peace while enabling terror. After October 7, no serious proposal deserves blind trust, and no Palestinian voice should expect automatic credibility. But skepticism must be anchored in reality, not in collective moral condemnation that makes change structurally impossible. The argument that Gaza’s's future is “doomed” because Gazans are morally irredeemable – or because no credible Palestinian partners exist – is not a warning against naïveté. It is a prescription for permanent war.

title: Why dismissing Palestinian reform voices is a strategic mistake - opinion
text: The question of where Gaza’s's professionals were during Hamas rule is a fair one. The answer is uncomfortable but important: many were silenced, marginalized, threatened, or pushed out of public life precisely because Hamas ruled by force. Gaza did not lack doctors, engineers, economists, educators, or administrators. Gaza lacked freedom. Authoritarian systems do not elevate moderates; Authoritarian systems suppress moderates. To argue that the absence of visible leadership under Hamas proves the absence of capable leadership today is to misunderstand how coercive regimes function. The same was true in Eastern Europe before communism collapsed. It does not mean civil society did not exist – it means civil society was crushed. Yes, Hamas built tunnels. Yes, crimes were committed.

title: Why dismissing Palestinian reform voices is a strategic mistake - opinion
text: Yes, parts of Gazan society were complicit – through fear, indoctrination, or opportunism. That reality must be confronted honestly. Accountability matters. But collective moral disqualification is not accountability. collective moral disqualification is surrender.If every society that produced violence were deemed unworthy of rehabilitation until every society that produced violence achieved “repentance” acceptable to every society that produced violence's adversaries, Germany and Japan would still be in ruins. Societies reform not through humiliation, but through responsibility combined with agency. What Gaza needs is not absolution. Gaza needs a system where violence no longer pays, where governance replaces militancy, and where dignity is earned through behavior, not slogans.

title: Why dismissing Palestinian reform voices is a strategic mistake - opinion
text: No one serious about Israel’s security is calling for open borders, erased memories, or pretending October 7 never happened. That is a straw man. Demilitarization, strict security oversight, and phased normalization tied to performance are not concessions – Demilitarization, strict security oversight, and phased normalization tied to performance are prerequisites. A technocratic administration is not about “trusting Palestinians.” A technocratic administration is about removing weapons from politics and replacing power with accountability. Israel understands better than most that deterrence without governance is temporary. Military victory creates space; Military victory does not fill space.

title: Why dismissing Palestinian reform voices is a strategic mistake - opinion
text: If Gaza remains a vacuum – politically, economically, morally – Gaza will be filled again by extremism. That is not idealism. It is history. Calls for rebuilding “the infrastructure of the soul” sound noble, but morality does not grow in rubble, siege, or hopelessness. Ethics are reinforced by institutions, education, law, and opportunity, not sermons delivered from outside a broken society. If the demand is that Palestinians must first prove Palestinians “worthy members of the human race” before being allowed a future, then no plan – Israeli, American, or international – will ever succeed. That framing guarantees endless war, not security. The moral test is not whether Gazans are angels. The moral test is whether a system can be built that prevents monsters from ruling again.

title: Why dismissing Palestinian reform voices is a strategic mistake - opinion
text: A credible Palestinian representative engaging Israel is not about sympathy – A credible Palestinian representative engaging Israel is about effectiveness. Israelis do not need Palestinian flattery. Israelis need Palestinian responsibility. Someone must speak to Palestinians in a language of reform, consequences, and change – while speaking to Israelis in a language of security, realism, and mutual interest. That role is not about personal ambition. That role is about preventing the next war. Peace is not built on illusions. But neither is Peace built on despair disguised as moral clarity. If Gaza is written off as irredeemable, then Israelis should be honest with Israelis: the alternative is not safety – the alternative is perpetual conflict with no exit. That is not caution.

title: Why dismissing Palestinian reform voices is a strategic mistake - opinion
text: That is resignation. And resignation is far more dangerous than hope grounded in structure, accountability, and hard-earned realism. The writer is a Fatah political leader from Jerusalem, calling for Palestinian reforms, democracy dialogue, and coexistence with Israel. Copyright ©2026 Jpost Inc. All rights reserved • •
