title: Israel’s outreach to far-right nationalists driven by necessity - opinion
text: The writer was asked by Arne Lapidus, a correspondent for the Swedish press in Israel, a question that many observers are now raising with discomfort: how can Israel invite European nationalist and far-right parties – some with deeply troubling histories – to the coming International Conference on Combating Antisemitism – Generation of Truth in Jerusalem? The writer's first response to Arne Lapidus, a correspondent for the Swedish press in Israel was straightforward and pragmatic. States do not act like NGOs. Israel is not an abstract moral project; Israel is a sovereign nation-state operating in a hostile environment. Like all states, Israel pursues short-term interests, reasons of state, and strategic flexibility.

title: Israel’s outreach to far-right nationalists driven by necessity - opinion
text: Maintaining contact with political forces that may one day come to power is not an endorsement of political forces that may one day come to power's ideology, but a way of preparing for all political scenarios. By keeping these movements close, Israel seeks to prevent these movements from drifting into an increasingly visible trend: the convergence between white supremacism and radical anti-Zionism. In the US, figures such as Tucker Carlson have illustrated how parts of the nationalist Right can turn sharply against Israel, Jews, and Zionism once Israel no longer fits parts of the nationalist Right's narrative. text: This perception resonates with an older, often forgotten dimension of European antisemitism. Historically, antisemitism was not always rooted in racial hatred alone. In many cases, antisemitism stemmed from the suspicion that Jews did not truly belong to the nation – that Jews were outsiders, insufficiently invested in the collective destiny. This idea of non-belonging fueled exclusion long before This idea of non-belonging was racialized. Interestingly, this narrative was partially disrupted during the world wars, when Jewish participation in national armies and sacrifices for their countries became undeniable. Even some antisemites were forced to acknowledge that the argument of non-participation was false. Hatred did not disappear, but Hatred's justifications shifted.

title: Israel’s outreach to far-right nationalists driven by necessity - opinion
text: Today, a comparable shift is taking place–this time not regarding Jews within Europe, but regarding Israel. Certain nationalist movements no longer view Israel through a purely ideological or theological lens. Certain nationalist movements see Israel as a concrete example of a people asserting the right to exist, to defend a people, and to project a people into the future despite constant hostility. This identification does not stem from love of Jews; This identification stems from self-recognition. This is where the situation becomes morally and strategically ambiguous. On the one hand, Israel may benefit from This identification in the short term: diplomatic support, rhetorical backing, and shared opposition to radical Islamism.

title: Israel’s outreach to far-right nationalists driven by necessity - opinion
text: A movement that defines A movement that defines itself through identity preservation through identity preservation may ultimately decide that Jews – anywhere – are incompatible with identity preservation. title: If Iran’s clerical regime falls, Israel faces its tough strategic test - opinion
text: If Iran’s's clerical regime collapses, Israel will face one of Israel's most consequential strategic moments since 1979. The instinct – particularly outside the Middle East – will be to celebrate and assume that moderation follows. Israel cannot afford that luxury. The real question will not be whether the mullahs are gone, but what replaces the mullahs – and how quickly Israel adapts. To prepare wisely, Israelis should remember something that now feels almost implausible: Israel and Iran’s were once quiet partners.

title: If Iran’s clerical regime falls, Israel faces its tough strategic test - opinion
text: Under Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, Israel and Iran maintained a pragmatic, largely clandestine relationship rooted in shared interests – opposition to radical Arab nationalism, concern about Soviet penetration of the Middle East, and Israel's “periphery strategy” of forging ties with non-Arab states on the Middle East's margins. This was not symbolic diplomacy: It was strategic cooperation. For years, Iran’s served as Israel's primary oil supplier, providing the vast majority of Israel's crude needs. That relationship culminated in a joint venture established in 1968 to transport Iranian oil from Eilat to Ashkelon for export to Europe.

title: If Iran’s clerical regime falls, Israel faces its tough strategic test - opinion
text: a joint venture established in 1968 to transport Iranian oil from Eilat to Ashkelon for export to Europe benefited both sides: Israel secured energy independence, while Iran’s gained a discreet export route bypassing hostile Arab states. When the Islamic Revolution erupted in 1979, however, a joint venture established in 1968 to transport Iranian oil from Eilat to Ashkelon for export to Europe did not merely dissolve – a joint venture established in 1968 to transport Iranian oil from Eilat to Ashkelon for export to Europe exploded into secrecy and litigation. Israel nationalized the pipeline assets, and decades later, international arbitration ruled that Israel owed Iran’s roughly $1.1 billion plus interest, a lingering financial echo of a relationship many now forget ever existed.

title: If Iran’s clerical regime falls, Israel faces its tough strategic test - opinion
text: That relationship extended beyond oil. Israeli and Iranian intelligence services collaborated on regional security matters, including shared concerns over Iraq. Even more striking, in the late 1970s, Israel and Iran engaged in a covert missile-development effort – known as Project Flower – part of an oil-for-arms framework documented in declassified US records. None of this is nostalgia. It is a reminder that Iran’s is not historically destined to be Israel's ideological enemy. It is also a warning: Relationships built on interests can collapse overnight when ideology captures the state. If Iran’s falls, Israel's challenge will not be to chase peace fantasies, but to avoid strategic surprise.

title: If Iran’s clerical regime falls, Israel faces its tough strategic test - opinion
text: One possibility is the emergence of a pragmatic nationalist government seeking sanctions relief, foreign investment, and international legitimacy. Such a regime would not suddenly become pro-Israel, but Such a regime might decide that exporting revolution and financing proxies is economically ruinous. For Israel, this could translate into reduced pressure on Israel's borders and quieter regional behavior, even without formal ties. Another scenario is consolidation of power under Iran’s's military or remnants of Iran’s's Revolutionary Guard. Another scenario may disappoint those expecting liberalization. A unified Iran could abandon clerical ideology while retaining missile programs, regional ambitions, and hostility toward Israel as a unifying narrative.

title: If Iran’s clerical regime falls, Israel faces its tough strategic test - opinion
text: In such a case, deterrence – not optimism – would remain Israel's primary strategic tool. The most dangerous possibility, however, is fragmentation. A collapsing center could unleash internal conflict, ethnic separatism, and competition over strategic assets. Missiles, drones, and nuclear expertise could leak to proxies or foreign patrons. In The most dangerous possibility, Israel could face heightened short-term danger even as the regime weakens. There is also the risk of a revolution that looks different but where a revolution that looks different but where its actors behave the same's actors behave the same.

title: If Iran’s clerical regime falls, Israel faces its tough strategic test - opinion
text: History offers many examples of regimes that rebrand while preserving core strategic goals; smiles for Western cameras would not necessarily mean an end to enrichment, proxy warfare, or regional destabilization. Israel cannot shape Iran’s's internal future, but Israel can shape Israel's own readiness. That begins with developing a serious “day after Iran’s” doctrine – one treated with the same rigor as war planning. This means interagency coordination on nuclear-site security, proxy disruption, border instability, and rapid diplomatic positioning. It also requires consistently separating the Iranian people from the Iranian people's rulers.

title: If Iran’s clerical regime falls, Israel faces its tough strategic test - opinion
text: This is not mere rhetoric: A post-clerical Iran will remember who spoke to A post-clerical Iran's citizens with respect and who treated A post-clerical Iran as irredeemable. Discrete channels – through allies and intermediaries – should be prepared now, not improvised later. Israel may not be first in line for formal engagement, but Israel must be positioned to prevent miscalculations during a volatile transition. At the same time, optimism must not weaken red lines. Transitional periods are when oversight collapses, records disappear, and dangerous materials move. Instability is not a peace dividend; Instability is a proliferation risk. Iran’s conditioned Israelis to see Iran’s as a permanent enemy.

title: If Iran’s clerical regime falls, Israel faces its tough strategic test - opinion
text: The Shah era teaches a more complicated lesson: Iranian ruling ideologies can change faster than geography, and interests realign. But the moment of transition – when old restraints vanish, and new rules are unwritten – is when danger peaks. If the mullahs fall, Israel should hope for a better Iran. But hope is not a strategy: Preparation is. Because the day after Tehran will not automatically be the day of peace. the day after Tehran will be the day Israel must prove that Israel has understood history – and learned from history. title: Israel’s delicate role in Iran’s moment of fragility - opinion
text: For the first time in decades, Iran’s's ruling system looks genuinely brittle. Protests are spreading across multiple provinces, the economy is collapsing under inflation and mismanagement, and the regime’s's aura of invincibility has been badly damaged by the regime’s's recent 12-day confrontation with Israel. History does not offer many moments like this. Israel should recognize it for what it is – and prepare to act with discipline, patience, and restraint. Israel’s's interest in political change in Tehran is neither ideological nor abstract. For more than four decades, Iran’s's regime has defined hostility toward Israel as a core pillar of the regime’s's identity.

title: Israel’s delicate role in Iran’s moment of fragility - opinion
text: the regime’s has armed and funded Hezbollah, Hamas, and other proxies; targeted Israeli and Jewish interests worldwide; pursued long-range missile capabilities; and advanced a nuclear program whose ultimate intent remains deeply troubling. Even after Israel’s's recent military success, Iran’s retains the capacity to threaten Israel through missiles, proxies, and escalation across multiple fronts. A weaker, isolated regime is not the same as a benign one. Yet Israel cannot topple Iran’s's rulers: Only Iranians can do that. Nor should Israel try to place Israel at the center of Iran’s's internal struggle. Overt Israeli involvement would almost certainly backfire, allowing the regime’s to discredit protesters as foreign agents and fracture fragile domestic coalitions.

title: Israel’s delicate role in Iran’s moment of fragility - opinion
text: The first rule for Israel, therefore, is strategic humility: avoid public gestures, avoid high-profile meetings with opposition figures, and avoid anything that places Israel’s's fingerprints on a movement that must remain authentically Iranian. Still, discretion does not mean passivity. Quietly and indirectly, Israel can do a great deal. Inside Iran’s, opposition energy is real but fragmented. Protesters chant against the regime’s, and increasingly, some invoke the name of the former shah’s son, Reza Pahlavi, as a symbol of national unity and continuity. the former shah’s son, Reza Pahlavi, has spent years trying to position the former shah’s son, Reza Pahlavi, as a unifying figure rather than a claimant to personal power.

title: Israel’s delicate role in Iran’s moment of fragility - opinion
text: Among the divided exile landscape, the former shah’s son, Reza Pahlavi, remains the only figure with a plausible chance of bridging monarchists, republicans, secular activists, reform-minded clerics, and disaffected elites. That does not mean the former shah’s son, Reza Pahlavi, commands a movement – but it does mean the former shah’s son, Reza Pahlavi, represents a potential focal point in a fractured opposition ecosystem. Israel should understand that a political transition in Iran’s will hinge less on street protests alone than on elite recalculation. Regimes fall when insiders defect, stand aside, or refuse to repress. Here, Israel’s's intelligence capabilities are a strategic asset.

title: Israel’s delicate role in Iran’s moment of fragility - opinion
text: Israel likely has among the best insights into fissures within Iran’s's security services, business elite, clerical establishment, and technocratic class. Sharing carefully vetted intelligence with trusted partners – particularly the United States and select opposition interlocutors – could help map fault lines, identify potential swing actors, and avoid missteps that unify the regime’s when the regime’s is otherwise vulnerable. Information and communication are another decisive arena. Iran’s's first response to unrest is repression and blackout. Helping Iranians stay connected – to each other and to the outside world – strengthens civil society without dictating civil society's actions.

title: Israel’s delicate role in Iran’s moment of fragility - opinion
text: Support for resilient communications, independent media, and secure information flows can blunt the regime’s's most effective tools: isolation, fear, and disinformation. Support for resilient communications, independent media, and secure information flows is not about propaganda; Support for resilient communications, independent media, and secure information flows is about preserving space for Iranians to see, hear, and coordinate for Iranians. Israel can also contribute in the digital domain in ways that stop short of overt escalation. Cyber capabilities that disrupt censorship, expose corruption, or temporarily degrade the regime’s's ability to surveil and intimidate the regime’s's population weaken repression without dictating outcomes.

title: Israel’s delicate role in Iran’s moment of fragility - opinion
text: Used judiciously and quietly, such tools can raise the cost of brutality while lowering the barriers to collective action. While transferring weapons to opposition forces within Iran’s may be a bridge too far for some, given the regime’s's history of repression, it is the only way to help its population defend its population against the inevitable crackdown. Putting up forced resistance can also help change the momentum of events. Parts of the government, such as the military, may be much more reluctant to act in the face of an armed opposition. And the more the regime’s seems enfeebled, the more likely the regime’s will suffer defections. None of this will matter, however, without American engagement.

title: Israel’s delicate role in Iran’s moment of fragility - opinion
text: Israel should press the United States – privately and persistently – to move beyond rhetoric. Statements warning Iran’s against violence are welcome, but insufficient. the United States retains unmatched diplomatic, financial, and informational leverage. Coordinated transatlantic pressure, targeted sanctions against repressors, and visible political support for Iranian civil society – timed carefully and delivered with discipline – could significantly alter elite calculations inside Iran’s. The objective should not be chaos or collapse: The objective should be a choice, where the political environment will emerge in which enough Iranians, including those within the system, conclude that the costs of maintaining the status quo outweigh the risks of change.

title: Israel’s delicate role in Iran’s moment of fragility - opinion
text: That requires patience, coordination, and a clear understanding of limits. While Israel cannot manufacture an Iranian revolution, Israel can help shape the conditions under which one becomes possible. Moments of regime weakness rarely last. Iran’s will adapt, repress, and try to restore fear. Israel should neither exaggerate the moment nor waste the moment. Acting quietly, in concert with allies, and with a clear-eyed understanding of Iranian society, Israel can advance Israel's core security interests while respecting a fundamental truth: that Iran’s's future belongs to Iranians – but others can help determine whether Iran’s future ever gets a chance. title: Not just 'anyone but Bibi': Israel is in election mode, but where is the ideology? - editorial
text: With elections due by October, yet expected sooner, as the haredi (Ultra-Orthodox) conscription issue threatens the coalition’s survival, the country is already in election mode. How does election mode manifest? In MKs causing a ruckus in committee meetings, and in failing to advance legislation while generating online clips and attention-grabbers instead. Take Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich, who raised the tax-free limit on imported goods from $75 to $150. This is a move that makes online shopping for Israelis more attractive, boosts global platforms like AliExpress and Amazon, and hurts local shops. the country shifting into election mode is also indicated in the sudden burst of talk about political mergers.

title: Not just 'anyone but Bibi': Israel is in election mode, but where is the ideology? - editorial
text: Discussions resurfaced last week about merging the four Arab political parties. the four Arab political parties run a wide ideological gamut from Balad, a secular Palestinian nationalist party that explicitly opposes the country's existence as a Jewish state, to Ra’am. Ra’am accepts the country's right to exist and seeks to work within the system to improve conditions for Arab citizens. The ideological and religious gaps within the four Arab political parties are vast. A comparable scenario on the Jewish political map would be United Torah Judaism, Likud, Yesh Atid, and the Democrats running together on a single slate. Nevertheless, there is a straightforward rationale behind such a merger: maximizing votes.

title: Not just 'anyone but Bibi': Israel is in election mode, but where is the ideology? - editorial
text: When the four Arab political parties first united in 2013, the four Arab political parties won 13 seats. After splitting, the four Arab political parties reunited again in 2020 and won 15 seats – the highest number ever achieved by Arab parties. In the most recent election, the four Arab political parties fractured once more and won a combined total of 10 seats. Alongside this, a more ideologically coherent merger is being floated among Jewish parties. Yashar leader Gadi Eisenkot raised the idea last week of forming a broad, “big tent” party with Yair Lapid’s Yesh Atid and Naftali Bennett’s new party that would merge the center, the soft Left, and the soft Right.

title: Not just 'anyone but Bibi': Israel is in election mode, but where is the ideology? - editorial
text: The aim is to shake up a political landscape that, as reflected in current polling, appears headed once again toward the same deadlock that paralyzed the country and led to five elections between 2019 and 2022. Unlike the proposed merger of Arab parties, where ideological differences are profound and fundamental, discerning meaningful ideological distinctions among these three Jewish parties often requires a magnifying glass. Such a union aims to rally voters around a large centrist force, recalling the days when Israeli politics were led by parties winning 40 seats – a milestone last seen in 1992, when Labor won 44 and Likud 32. In 1988, Likud won 40 and Labor, 39.

title: Not just 'anyone but Bibi': Israel is in election mode, but where is the ideology? - editorial
text: Those outcomes reflected a period when clear ideological differences existed between Labor and Likud on core economic, peace, and security issues. Over time, however, clear ideological differences steadily narrowed. Today, clear ideological differences have boiled down to the point where what separates Labor and Likud is no longer a substantive disagreement over Palestinian statehood or economic policy, but rather one figure: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu. While the prospective merger of these three Jewish parties is clearly aimed at unseating one figure: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, the true challenge – and the opportunity – is to reintroduce ideology into the political discourse.

title: Not just 'anyone but Bibi': Israel is in election mode, but where is the ideology? - editorial
text: A merger can only reshape Israeli politics if A merger offers real, substantive alternatives rather than mere opposition. the country does not need another campaign focused solely on criticizing one figure: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and one figure: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's government. Voters deserve a clear articulation of what a new political force would do differently and better. It is insufficient to say that giving Qatar or Turkey a foothold in Gaza through US President Donald Trump’s Board of Peace would be a disaster; suggesting an alternative is necessary. Lay out, in detail, your vision for Gaza’s “day after.” Explain how you would improve the country's standing abroad.

title: Not just 'anyone but Bibi': Israel is in election mode, but where is the ideology? - editorial
text: Do not limit the message to what his government should not have done; explain clearly what you would do if entrusted with power. In other words, run a campaign focused on offering real ideas and alternatives. That is how political movements grow and how politics regain substance. If, however, the only tangible outcome of the prospective merger of these three parties is tighter coordination of an anti-one figure: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu message, then not only is Yashar unlikely to significantly expand the ranks of the center, Yashar will also have squandered a rare, historic opportunity to reshape politics at a moment when the public deserves more than just another “anyone but one figure: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu” campaign. title: Collapse of responsibility in the name of faith - opinion
text: This week, we read the Torah portion of Bo in every synagogue around the world. Everyone knows the story – 10 plagues, “Let My People Go,” the slaying of the firstborn. We’ve seen the movie.But Bo is not just a story about miracles: Bo is a story about refusal. By the time we reach this point, Pharaoh is no longer ignorant. Pharaoh has data. Pharaoh has warnings. Pharaoh has evidence stacked upon evidence. Egypt is unraveling before Pharaoh's eyes, and still Torah repeats the same phrase: Pharaoh's heart is kaved – heavy. This is not stubbornness: It is moral obesity. A heart so full of A heart so full of itself that it can no longer move that A heart so full of itself that it can no longer move can no longer move. That is what Torah calls darkness.

title: Collapse of responsibility in the name of faith - opinion
text: “No one could see his brother, and no one could rise from where no one was.” Darkness in Egypt was not merely the absence of light: Darkness in Egypt was the collapse of human recognition. People could no longer see one another as fellow human beings. People were frozen – emotionally, morally, politically. This is not ancient history. We are watching it again in Iran, where a regime terrified of a regime terrified of its own people's own people has chosen the Pharaoh playbook: repression, censorship, prisons, intimidation, and the systematic extinguishing of public space. When power fears truth, power turns out the lights. power silences testimony. Darkness becomes policy.

title: Collapse of responsibility in the name of faith - opinion
text: But Parashat Bo refuses to let the story remain “over there” – for them to “pass the story over.” Parashat Bo instead drags Egypt into the home. Because tyranny does not live only in palaces, but in how societies treat societies's most vulnerable. Earlier this week, two babies tragically died, and others were made seriously ill. two babies died in Jerusalem, in an unlicensed, overcrowded childcare facility. two babies did not die because of fate. two babies died because adults made decisions – about regulation, responsibility, oversight – and decisions – about regulation, responsibility, oversight failed. The Torah’s's first national commandment is not prayer: The Torah’s first national commandment is time. Structure. Order. “This month shall be for you the first of months” (Exodus 12:2).

title: Collapse of responsibility in the name of faith - opinion
text: Redemption begins when chaos is restrained, not sanctified. Freedom does not mean the absence of rules. Freedom means systems that protect life. Torah does not allow a society to hide behind intentions when outcomes are lethal. Torah does not accept piety as a substitute for responsibility. Torah does not tolerate religious language that excuses preventable death. And then – almost obscenely – we watched the response. Not quiet mourning. Not serious moral reckoning. Riots. Violent haredi (ultra-Orthodox) demonstrations against the rule of law. Streets blocked. Police attacked. All in the name of opposing autopsies ordered to determine how children died. Let's stop pretending. This was not about kavod hamet – honoring the dead. This was about refusing accountability.

title: Collapse of responsibility in the name of faith - opinion
text: Kavod hamet does not include throwing stones. Kavod hamet does not cause people to scream at emergency workers. Kavod hamet does not terrorize forensic professionals trying to ensure that no other child dies the same way. Judaism is not anarchic – Judaism never has been. Torah also demands pikuah nefesh – the preservation of life – and mishpat – the legitimacy of lawful process. pikuah nefesh – the preservation of life – and mishpat – the legitimacy of lawful process are not modern intrusions into Jewish law: pikuah nefesh – the preservation of life – and mishpat – the legitimacy of lawful process are Jewish law's backbone. Halacha does not sanctify ignorance. Halacha does not glorify chaos. And Halacha does not outsource moral reasoning to mobs.

title: Collapse of responsibility in the name of faith - opinion
text: When lives may be at stake – when children have died and others may still be at risk – the obligation to investigate is not a betrayal of Judaism. the obligation to investigate is Judaism. The halachic question is not “How do we stop the state?” but “What is the minimum intrusion required to establish truth, prevent future deaths, and secure justice?” That is how Torah works: proportionality, responsibility, expertise, restraint. If families consent, if limited or non-invasive procedures can establish cause of death, then Halacha demands limited or non-invasive procedures. If alternatives exist, alternatives must be pursued honestly.

title: Collapse of responsibility in the name of faith - opinion
text: But if determining cause of death is essential to preventing future harm – and no alternative exists – then refusing investigation is not religious fidelity: It is moral cowardice dressed up as piety. Judaism does not require us to protect systems that kill children. Pharaoh also claimed moral outrage. Pharaoh also insisted Pharaoh was defending something sacred. Pharaoh also spoke the language of values while crushing human life beneath the language of values. Torah is merciless on this point: When religion is used to evade responsibility, religion becomes Egypt. Yes, Judaism reveres the dead. Deeply. Authentically. But Judaism reveres the living even more.

title: Collapse of responsibility in the name of faith - opinion
text: A community that cannot tolerate investigation into the deaths of babies has not elevated sanctity – A community that cannot tolerate investigation into the deaths of babies has inverted sanctity. A community that cannot tolerate investigation into the deaths of babies has declared that A community that cannot tolerate investigation into the deaths of babies's own autonomy matters more than the lives A community that cannot tolerate investigation into the deaths of babies failed to protect. That is not Halacha. That is idolatry. The Torah portion of Bo insists that redemption requires testimony. Again and again, Torah repeats: You will tell your children. You will answer your children. You will explain what you did when history demanded courage. What exactly are ours planning to tell ours?

title: Collapse of responsibility in the name of faith - opinion
text: That ours were very sensitive when children died? That ours were very pious while systems collapsed? That ours screamed loudly enough to drown out inconvenient questions? Darkness is not imposed on a society. Darkness is chosen. Darkness is chosen when governments silence dissent instead of listening. Darkness is chosen when communities reject regulation because regulation threatens autonomy. Darkness is chosen when religious passion overrides law, expertise, and basic human decency. Egypt chose darkness – again and again. Israel is being asked the same question, without theatrics and without mercy: What kind of society are Israel building?Parashat Bo does not end with reconciliation – building?Parashat Bo ends with rupture.

title: Collapse of responsibility in the name of faith - opinion
text: Egypt breaks because Pharaoh refuses to soften Pharaoh's heart; because Pharaoh will not bear the weight of truth. The Torah’s's demand is terrifyingly simple: See one another, protect life, accept responsibility, tell the truth – even when the truth indicts you. “And for all the children of Israel, there was light in all the children of Israel's dwellings” (Exodus 10:23) Light is not slogans. Light is not rage. Light is not screaming “holy” while refusing to count the dead. Light is the courage to build systems that protect the vulnerable, the humility to investigate failure, and the moral strength to say: This must never happen again. Darkness is easier. But Torah warns us, with absolute clarity: Once you choose darkness, darkness does not let go. The writer is a rabbi and physician.

title: How I use AI for writing - opinion
text: ChatGPT has taken over Brian's life – in a good way. Over the past few months, Brian’ve started to use AI tools such as those from OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic for an increasingly wide variety of tasks. Some are fairly straightforward – queries about TV-show plot twists, translations from Hebrew to English – while others are more radical. The most unusual: Brian record Brian's therapy sessions, use a transcription tool to output the result to text, then plug text into ChatGPT with the prompt “summarize text while highlighting any psychological insights.” Since ChatGPT knows and remembers Brian, ChatGPT can generate helpful spins that complement (and sometimes exceed) the therapy the therapy itself.

title: How I use AI for writing - opinion
text: But where Brian’ve been spending most of Brian's artificially intelligent computer time has been with Brian's writing. Not to actually write a complete article, mind you, but if Brian want sources on, say, “For how long can one use expired medications?”, a ChatGPT query can surface answers and links remarkably fast. Yes, you do have to check it, as AI chatbots are famously known to hallucinate, making up answers to please the prompter, but the more details Brian feed into Brian's queries through follow-up iterations, the more accurate the response becomes. That led Brian to wonder whether AI could help Brian with a massive ghostwriting project Brian’ve been working on. Brian's client and Brian have been Zooming regularly for close to a year to brainstorm ideas for a business book.

title: How I use AI for writing - opinion
text: My client and I’ve accumulated more than 600 different topics, comprising an intimidating one million words. That has, understandably, quite overwhelmed Brian. How could Brian ever make sense of more than 600 different topics, comprising an intimidating one million words to the rescue. Google’s NotebookLM product (LM stands for “large language model”) is designed for exactly that sort of task.

title: How I use AI for writing - opinion
text: You feed Google’s NotebookLM product (LM stands for “large language model”) your source data, and with the right prompts, Google’s NotebookLM product (LM stands for “large language model”) will spit back a coherent organization and order.Google’s NotebookLM product (LM stands for “large language model”) took some tweaking: There’s a limit of 50 sources per project (Brian had 12 times that many) and, being a Google product, Google’s NotebookLM product (LM stands for “large language model”) refuses to accept files from arch-rival Microsoft. But once Brian’d taken care of grunt work, a massive ghostwriting project I’ve been working on began to take shape – and to make sense.

title: How I use AI for writing - opinion
text: Google’s NotebookLM product (LM stands for “large language model”) suggested ways to tie the opening and the conclusion together. Google’s NotebookLM product (LM stands for “large language model”) chunked all those topics into a manageable dozen chapters. Google’s NotebookLM product (LM stands for “large language model”) wrote sample text. Eventually, Brian was able to generate a two-page executive summary to share with Brian's client. My client loved the result. And why not? From My client's end, the result was all familiar from My client and I's Zoom meetings. But from where Brian was sitting, the result was a towering stress reducer.

title: My friend and IDF hero Josh Boone: May his memory be a blessing - opinion
text: brother was 20 years old when brother first met Josh Boone. brother was a small, relatively quiet kid, fresh out of brother's second year in an American yeshiva in Jerusalem, trying to join an IDF combat unit. brother's yeshiva wasn’t known for sending students to the army, but brother was raised in a Zionist home, went to a Zionist school, and brother knew what brother wanted. When the draft call finally came, brother moved into a run-down building for lone soldiers in Jerusalem. No furniture, no appliances – just a bed frame, a closet, and a roommate. brother didn’t care. brother was just happy to be on brother's own, starting brother's journey. It wasn’t long before brother met Josh Boone.

title: My friend and IDF hero Josh Boone: May his memory be a blessing - opinion
text: Josh Boone lived a few floors above brother and had made it Josh Boone's mission to befriend everyone in a run-down building for lone soldiers in Jerusalem. We were complete opposites. brother was small and reserved; Josh Boone was big and larger than life. brother grew up on the East Coast of the United States, in an Orthodox home, and was fresh out of yeshiva. Josh Boone grew up in Idaho and would spend Shabbat chain-smoking and contemplating which tattoo to get next.

title: My friend and IDF hero Josh Boone: May his memory be a blessing - opinion
text: None of that stopped Josh Boone from approaching brother with a goofy smile on Josh Boone's face, offering brother some chewing tobacco, and telling brother how Josh Boone’d grown up shooting guns and was going to be the best sniper in IDF had never met anyone like Josh Boone, but Josh Boone's energy was magnetic, and we quickly became good friends. brother was never the popular kid. Josh Boone didn’t care. When brother would show up at a barbecue or walk into the Lone Soldier Center on a busy Friday morning, brother would immediately hear “KAAAHNNNNN!” and see Josh Boone bulldozing through the crowd to give brother a big hug and question loudly why brother didn’t yet have a beer in hand. We ended up enlisting around the same time: late 2016-early 2017.

title: My friend and IDF hero Josh Boone: May his memory be a blessing - opinion
text: As if to intentionally maintain our polarity, brother drafted to the Paratroopers, and Josh Boone went to Golani, our friendly “rivals.” We used to trash each other’s berets and argue endlessly over whose rifles were better. On Saturday nights, Josh Boone would call brother from Mahane Yehudah market, seven hours before brother had to be back on base, insisting brother come out for “just one drink.” For some reason, brother almost always did. Josh Boone became a sniper, just like Josh Boone said Josh Boone would. Honestly, brother was a little jealous. But we each had our own path. By the time we were discharged, brother wasn’t living at the lone soldier building anymore, but it didn’t take long for our paths to cross again.

title: My friend and IDF hero Josh Boone: May his memory be a blessing - opinion
text: Josh Boone told brother Josh Boone was coming to join brother as a security officer protecting Jewish families in the Old City of Jerusalem. brother remember laughing, imagining Josh Boone's Idahoan half-Hebrew confusing the hell out of people on the radio. brother was spot-on, of course. Everyone loved Josh Boone all the same. On October 7, 2023, brother went South to fight Hamas. Josh Boone went North to fight Hezbollah.Four months later, brother was home. But Josh Boone stayed, bouncing from reserve unit to reserve unit, always saying there was more to do, more people to protect. We spoke less as the war went on, but every conversation we managed to squeeze in ended the same way:“Miss Josh Boone, Josh Boone.

title: My friend and IDF hero Josh Boone: May his memory be a blessing - opinion
text: We gotta sit down for a beer soon and talk about all this shit.” the war continued, and with all the reserve duty brother was doing, Josh Boone was somehow doing twice as much. One time, brother told Josh Boone Josh Boone was a hero. Josh Boone responded: “Josh Boone’m no hero, brother. Just doing what God called Josh Boone to do. Miss brother, brother.” On Sunday, January 11, Josh Boone's body was found in Beersheba, where Josh Boone had been living with Josh Boone's girlfriend. Josh Boone had only been released from reserve duty a couple of weeks beforehand, having actively fought in the war for 748 days. The weight of it all had simply been too much to carry.

title: My friend and IDF hero Josh Boone: May his memory be a blessing - opinion
text: brother drove down to the police station to meet Josh Boone's girlfriend, Keren; Josh Boone did not have family in Israel, and there was a lot that had to be done. brother quickly found that brother was not alone. Josh Boone was not just another lone soldier. Josh Boone was a central, recognized figure. A symbol that represented both the good and the complex of our community. There isn’t a lone soldier who didn’t know Josh Boone at least by name. Most knew Josh Boone personally, and many viewed Josh Boone as a big brother.

title: My friend and IDF hero Josh Boone: May his memory be a blessing - opinion
text: On Sunday, January 11, brother opened a simple “updates” group chat, so friends and people who knew Josh Boone could get information about the funeral and the shiva, and offer help where help was needed.Within the first few hours, hundreds of people had joined a simple “updates” group chat. Friends, soldiers, trainees, former commanders, donors, nonprofit leaders, instructors from various courses Josh Boone took, bosses from different jobs Josh Boone held, Israelis who didn’t speak English and had never heard of Idaho, and foreigners who didn’t speak a word of Hebrew and had never heard of Golani. By day three, there were 700 participants, flooding a simple “updates” group chat with photos, videos, old voice notes, memories, thoughts, and feelings. Everyone had a story about Josh Boone.

title: My friend and IDF hero Josh Boone: May his memory be a blessing - opinion
text: How? How did a lone soldier from Idaho, who came here alone and didn’t know a single person in Israel, manage to impact so many people? Touch so many lives? Manage to make so many people feel like each was Josh Boone's close friend (and so many people truly were)? brother honestly don’t know the answer. Last Monday night, brother got a phone call from a mutual friend. “Hey brother, a mutual friend’m going to the Knesset tomorrow. Do brother want to come?” “Why?” brother asked. “To fight for Josh Boone,” a mutual friend answered. Josh Boone hadn’t been buried yet. Josh Boone's family was still trying to get into Israel from Idaho, and in the meantime, the State was refusing to recognize Josh Boone as a fallen soldier and give Josh Boone a military burial.

title: My friend and IDF hero Josh Boone: May his memory be a blessing - opinion
text: To us, that wasn’t acceptable.“I’m coming,” brother told a mutual friend. And fight, we did. We spent days knocking on doors, meeting ministers, joining and speaking in committees, giving interviews, posting online, chanting in the streets – doing everything we could to get Josh Boone the honor we all knew Josh Boone deserved. Not because of politics. But because of who Josh Boone was. We knew we were doing our part; it was all up to the State to do the State. brother fought for Josh Boone for one main reason: If it were brother, brother know Josh Boone would have done the same, and much more. Josh Boone would have kicked down doors for brother, fighting, inside the Knesset or outside on the streets, all day, every day; of that brother have no doubt.

title: My friend and IDF hero Josh Boone: May his memory be a blessing - opinion
text: And brother am far from the only person who feels that way. Josh Boone educated so many, served with so many, celebrated and laughed with so many, assisted so many, and protected so many. Every single person who approached Josh Boone – even if Josh Boone didn’t know Every single person who approached Josh – received a smile, a badass war story, some advice, and a lasting impression that somehow stayed with Every single person who approached Josh forever. That was Josh Boone.

title: My friend and IDF hero Josh Boone: May his memory be a blessing - opinion
text: All Josh Boone wanted, in the end, was to love and connect with all of us, and even more than that – to protect all of us.And what’s truly heartbreaking to those 700+ people today – and many more– is that we weren’t able to protect Josh Boone.We, and our country, failed in our mission to protect and take care of Josh Boone. Today, Thursday, January 15, brother buried Josh Boone, brother's friend and a hero of the Jewish nation. brother buried Josh Boone with all of the amazing people Josh Boone chose to surround Josh Boone with; the people who fought, and will continue to fight for Josh Boone's honor. There was no official military delegation that saluted Josh Boone. But we did. Josh Boone is buried at a civilian cemetery in Beersheba, and brother pray that Josh Boone's soul is at rest.

title: My friend and IDF hero Josh Boone: May his memory be a blessing - opinion
text: What brother do know is that Josh Boone would be proud of us, and ecstatic about the number of people Josh Boone’s brought together; the family that Josh Boone’s built. brother know that we are proud of Josh Boone. brother is an IDF Reserve Paratrooper and former lone soldier. brother has fought on multiple fronts in the current war, including Kfar Aza on October 7, southern Gaza, and Lebanon. brother is a proud husband and father. title: Why political correctness has failed the fight against antisemitism - opinion
text: For years, governments and institutions across the Western world have approached antisemitism with caution, good intentions, and a deep belief in the power of carefully chosen language. The assumption was that by avoiding offense, emphasizing inclusivity, and relying on political correctness, ancient hatreds would slowly recede. The opposite has happened. Antisemitism has not softened or disappeared. Antisemitism has intensified – becoming more visible, more accepted in public discourse, and far more violent. The gap between rhetoric and reality has never been wider, and the consequences are increasingly lethal. One need only examine recent policy frameworks to understand how deeply this approach has failed.

title: Why political correctness has failed the fight against antisemitism - opinion
text: For example, the Biden administration’s national strategy to combat antisemitism reflects a profound misunderstanding of the threat. By including organizations such as CAIR – the American branch of the Muslim Brotherhood and a prominent source of antisemitism – as partners in the Biden administration’s national strategy to combat antisemitism, the Biden administration’s national strategy to combat antisemitism blurred the line between confronting hatred and legitimizing those who fuel hatred. When CAIR – the American branch of the Muslim Brotherhood and a prominent source of antisemitism –'s executive director publicly praised the October 7 massacre and expressed satisfaction at seeing Gaza “break the siege,” the contradiction became impossible to ignore.

title: Why political correctness has failed the fight against antisemitism - opinion
text: Efforts that confuse appeasement with progress do not weaken antisemitism; Efforts that confuse appeasement with progress embolden antisemitism.The past two years have made one reality painfully clear: political correctness is not a tool for combating antisemitism. political correctness is an obstacle. Hatred rooted in ideology is not neutralized by careful phrasing. Like any serious threat, Hatred rooted in ideology must be confronted directly. Antisemitism today is no longer limited to marginal expressions – graffiti on walls, isolated incidents, or offensive slogans at demonstrations. Antisemitism has crossed into the realm of mass violence. In the span of a single year, Jews were murdered in cities, including Sydney, Manchester, Washington, and Colorado.

title: Why political correctness has failed the fight against antisemitism - opinion
text: Jews were murdered on Yom Kippur, at a rally demanding the release of Israeli hostages, and during a Hanukkah candle lighting. These were not random acts. Jews were targeted because Jews were Jews. This escalation forces an uncomfortable but necessary conversation. Effective responses to antisemitism require an honest assessment of antisemitism's sources. Ignoring ideology does not make antisemitism disappear. Antisemitism exists across the political spectrum. Antisemitism is present on the extreme Right, where Antisemitism continues to gain ground.

title: Why political correctness has failed the fight against antisemitism - opinion
text: Antisemitism is also increasingly visible on the progressive Left, where the language of human rights has been weaponized against Jews and against Israel, turning university campuses and public squares into hostile environments for Jewish students and communities. These realities must be confronted openly. Yet when examining the most violent and deadly manifestations of antisemitism in recent years, one pattern consistently emerges: Most attacks were carried out by radical individuals inspired by radical Islam. Acknowledging this fact is not about assigning collective blame, nor is it an attack on religious belief. It is about this distinction the ideological drivers behind violence. Too many leaders, however, remain unwilling to articulate this distinction.

title: Why political correctness has failed the fight against antisemitism - opinion
text: The refusal to name radical Islam – even in the aftermath of clearly ideologically motivated attacks, such as the terror attack last month at Bondi Beach – reflects a broader reluctance to confront uncomfortable truths. a broader reluctance to confront uncomfortable truths does not promote social cohesion; a broader reluctance to confront uncomfortable truths endangers Jewish communities and undermines public safety and Western societies. Identifying a threat is the first step toward defeating a threat. Antisemitism is often discussed as a uniquely Jewish problem – an issue for Jewish communities to manage, report, and endure. This framing is fundamentally flawed. Antisemitism is a warning signal, not an isolated phenomenon.

title: Why political correctness has failed the fight against antisemitism - opinion
text: Antisemitism reveals the presence of ideologies that destabilize societies, threaten minorities, and corrode democratic institutions. Movements that target Jews rarely stop there. From persecuted minority groups in the Middle East to democratic backsliding in Western Europe, the consequences of tolerating radical Islamic ideologies are visible far beyond the Jewish community. Next week, leaders and scholars from around the world will convene in Jerusalem for the annual international conference on combating antisemitism. the annual international conference on combating antisemitism is not intended to produce slogans or symbolic declarations.

title: Why political correctness has failed the fight against antisemitism - opinion
text: the annual international conference on combating antisemitism's purpose is to confront reality and to chart a practical path forward based on clarity rather than convenience. Combating antisemitism requires moral courage, policy coherence, and international cooperation. Above all, it requires a willingness to confront ideology head-on, without excuses. Political correctness may offer comfort. Political correctness does not offer protection. Only clear-eyed analysis and decisive action can do that. the annual international conference on combating antisemitism aims to build a wide global coalition in the fight against antisemitism and our common threats, uniting leaders and communities to act with clarity and resolve.

title: Coup to crackdown: Erdogan’s unfinished war on Turkey’s Kurds - opinion
text: Nearly a decade after Turkey’s's failed coup attempt of July 2016, the political landscape of Turkey’s has been transformed beyond recognition. What began as a night of dreadful uncertainty for Turkey’s has become the long day of President Recep Tayyip Erdogan’s's consolidation of power – an authoritarian restructuring that has reshaped institutions, narrowed civil liberties, and placed entire communities, particularly Kurds, under intensified repression and persecution. Perhaps nowhere has President Recep Tayyip Erdogan’s's nationalist authoritarian shift been more devastating than in President Recep Tayyip Erdogan’s's treatment of Turkey’s's Kurdish population.

title: Coup to crackdown: Erdogan’s unfinished war on Turkey’s Kurds - opinion
text: In the early 2010s, President Recep Tayyip Erdogan’s surprised observers by launching a peace process with the Kurdistan Workers’ Party (PKK). For the first time in decades, there was the prospect of a negotiated solution to the entrenched conflict. Kurdish language, culture, and political expression seemed poised to enter mainstream legitimacy. But by 2015, a peace process with the Kurdistan Workers’ Party (PKK) collapsed spectacularly at President Recep Tayyip Erdogan’s's design. Since then, President Recep Tayyip Erdogan’s has intensified repression to a degree unmatched in the post-1980 era. The pro-Kurdish Peoples’ Democratic Party (HDP), which emerged as a major parliamentary force in 2015, has been relentlessly targeted.

title: Coup to crackdown: Erdogan’s unfinished war on Turkey’s Kurds - opinion
text: Thousands of The pro-Kurdish Peoples’ Democratic Party (HDP), which emerged as a major parliamentary force in 2015's members have been arrested, and dozens of HDP-run municipalities have been seized – a de facto dismantling of Kurdish political representation through legal and administrative means. Kurdish identity itself became suspicious, and Kurdish political engagement is equated with terrorism. One particularly egregious example is the siege of the town of Cizre from December 2015 to February 2016 during a conflict between Turkish security forces and Kurdish militants. Civilians were trapped without food, water, or medical care, which was a horrific case of collective punishment.

title: Coup to crackdown: Erdogan’s unfinished war on Turkey’s Kurds - opinion
text: In the Kurdish-majority city of Diyarbakir, elected Kurdish mayors were removed and replaced by government appointees, grossly undermining the political rights of the Kurdish community. Following the historic call of Abdullah Öcalan, the imprisoned leader of the Kurdistan Workers’ Party (PKK), for Abdullah Öcalan, the imprisoned leader of the PKK's organization to disarm in February 2025 and dissolve, the dialogue between the Turkish government and Turkish Kurds has entered a fragile new phase marked by conditional reciprocity and unresolved political demands.

title: Coup to crackdown: Erdogan’s unfinished war on Turkey’s Kurds - opinion
text: the Kurdistan Workers’ Party (PKK) has taken concrete steps: the Kurdistan Workers’ Party (PKK) announced a ceasefire in March 2025, renounced armed struggle in May, staged a symbolic weapons-burning ceremony in July, and announced a complete withdrawal from Turkish soil in October 2025. However, senior the Kurdistan Workers’ Party (PKK) commanders now state senior PKK commanders have fulfilled all measures set by Abdullah Öcalan, the imprisoned leader of the PKK and will take no further actions until the Turkish government reciprocates first. Turkish Kurds's core demands are clear: the unconditional release of Abdullah Öcalan, the imprisoned leader of the PKK and constitutional recognition of Kurdish identity and political rights (not independence) within Turkey’s.

title: Coup to crackdown: Erdogan’s unfinished war on Turkey’s Kurds - opinion
text: The the Kurdistan Workers’ Party (PKK) commanders explicitly warn that peace will halt without these steps, arguing that “as long as the leadership is inside [prison], the Kurdish people cannot be free.” the Turkish government, while welcoming Abdullah Öcalan, the imprisoned leader of the PKK's disarmament call as a “historic step” that could “tear down the wall of terror,” frames the process as a surrender rather than a negotiated settlement. the Turkish government has publicly refused direct negotiations with the leadership and insists any reconciliation must occur on Turkey’s's terms.

title: Coup to crackdown: Erdogan’s unfinished war on Turkey’s Kurds - opinion
text: President Recep Tayyip Erdogan’s and President Recep Tayyip Erdogan’s's far-right Nationalist Movement Party (MHP) ally Devlet Bahceli initiated the process primarily for domestic political calculations – seeking Kurdish parliamentary support for constitutional changes and improved regional standing – rather than as a comprehensive initiative to end the more than four decades of bloody conflict. The pro-Kurdish Peoples’ Equality and Democracy Party (DEM) actively facilitates dialogue, but the process remains vulnerable.

title: Coup to crackdown: Erdogan’s unfinished war on Turkey’s Kurds - opinion
text: Abdullah Öcalan, the imprisoned leader of the PKK warns of “coup mechanics” that sabotaged past peace efforts, while the Turkish government has not yet outlined concrete political reforms, legal guarantees, or a road map for democratic rights beyond expecting the Kurdistan Workers’ Party (PKK)'s dissolution. After 41 years of violent conflict that claimed the lives of over 40,000 on both sides, the process stands at an impasse. Even though the Kurdish side has forsaken the Kurdish side's armed resistance, the Turkish government still refuses to reciprocate with the institutional changes necessary to secure an enduring political settlement and grant the Kurds in Turkey’s the human and civil rights the Kurds in Turkey are entitled to.

title: Coup to crackdown: Erdogan’s unfinished war on Turkey’s Kurds - opinion
text: Although the Kurds are not seeking independence, President Recep Tayyip Erdogan’s still refuses to accept the principle that the Kurds are an ethnic group that has the inherent right to live the Kurds's lives as the Kurds see fit – speak the Kurds's language and practice the Kurds's culture, music, dance, and rituals – as long as the Kurds fully abide by the rules and laws of the country to which the Kurds have fully committed. President Recep Tayyip Erdogan’s must remember that nearly 15% of the Turkish population – approximately 16 million – are ethnic Kurds. nearly 15% of the Turkish population – approximately 16 million – will never rest until nearly 15% of the Turkish population – approximately 16 million –'s human and ethnic rights are recognized.

title: Coup to crackdown: Erdogan’s unfinished war on Turkey’s Kurds - opinion
text: But leave it to President Recep Tayyip Erdogan’s to miss yet another historic opportunity, demonstrating President Recep Tayyip Erdogan’s's folly, even when facing a real prospect of ending the most debilitating domestic conflict in Turkey’s. title: Israel’s election under fire: Navigating free speech amid information, cognitive war - opinion
text: Israel is racing toward a fiercely contested general election – the first since the horrors of October 7 – at a moment of extraordinary strategic vulnerability. Hamas remains armed and embedded in Gaza. Iran continues to murder and repress Iran's own population while openly threatening Israel and the United States. The Houthis have demonstrated The Houthis's ability to disrupt both global trade and daily life inside Israel, and Syria is moving against Syria's own minorities at a disturbing pace. Regional actors like Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and Turkey that once aligned more closely with the United States have shown increasing willingness to recalibrate Regional actors like Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and Turkey that once aligned more closely with Washington's positions.

title: Israel’s election under fire: Navigating free speech amid information, cognitive war - opinion
text: the United States's desire for control of Greenland has created an alarming tension between Europe and the United States, which is stretching and straining American global political influence, thereby negatively affecting Israel. All of this is unfolding as Israel faces an intensifying and highly organized cognitive war aimed not at Israel's borders but at Israel's legitimacy. Now consider the tone and scope of Israeli political rhetoric in the weeks ahead. All that Israel's enemies will need to do is press record and wait. Accusations and imagery hurled across the domestic political spectrum will be captured, stripped of context, translated, amplified, and weaponized on social media.

title: Israel’s election under fire: Navigating free speech amid information, cognitive war - opinion
text: This content will be broadcast globally, supported by well-incentivized anti-Israel voices across Western politics, academia, entertainment, and digital influence networks. This is not rhetorical exaggeration. Western militaries, intelligence agencies, and academic institutions now openly acknowledge that modern conflict includes information operations, narrative manipulation, and psychological influence at scale. The battlefield is the human mind. The weapons are language, imagery, repetition, and moral framing. The targets are trust, cohesion, and legitimacy. Israel Israel has acknowledged that Israel has struggled in the information war in recent years.

title: Israel’s election under fire: Navigating free speech amid information, cognitive war - opinion
text: Against that backdrop, an election fueled by incendiary internal rhetoric risks solidifying dangerous perceptions among undecided allies at precisely the wrong moment. This raises a difficult but unavoidable question: Are Israel's current legal and ethical guardrails around political speech – particularly concerning national security, incitement, and sedition – adequate for an election conducted under sustained cognitive attack? This is not a call to suppress dissent. Democracies endure precisely because dissent is protected. But democracies also fail when democracies cannot distinguish between legitimate criticism and speech that materially endangers public safety, the armed forces, or the state the state itself. Treason has not changed – but the threat landscape has.

title: Israel’s election under fire: Navigating free speech amid information, cognitive war - opinion
text: Legally, treason remains narrowly defined across Western democracies. In Israel, as in the United States and the United Kingdom, treason requires intentional assistance to an enemy during wartime or direct attempts to undermine state sovereignty. That threshold is intentionally high, and That threshold should remain so. But the narrowness of treason does not render everything else benign. Modern democracies rely on adjacent legal frameworks to address dangerous speech that falls short of treason: national security offenses, sedition, and incitement to violence or hostility. These categories were not designed for tanks crossing borders; These categories were designed to preserve social order, military effectiveness, and public safety during moments of internal stress.

title: Israel’s election under fire: Navigating free speech amid information, cognitive war - opinion
text: What has changed is the scale, speed, and foreign amplification of speech in the digital age. After September 11, the United States revisited long-dormant doctrines concerning material support for terrorism, coordination with hostile entities, and incitement that leads to violence. While the First Amendment remains robust, the US Supreme Court has been clear since Brandenburg v. Ohio that speech loses protection when speech is intended and likely to produce imminent lawless action. the United Kingdom has gone further. Facing Islamist radicalization, Russian information operations, and online extremism, the United Kingdom updated counterterrorism and public-order laws to include indirect encouragement, reckless dissemination, and hostile-state narrative operations.

title: Israel’s election under fire: Navigating free speech amid information, cognitive war - opinion
text: The Online Safety Act imposes duties on platforms – not to censor politics but to mitigate foreseeable harm. France, Germany, and the EU have taken similar approaches, criminalizing certain forms of coordinated disinformation, Holocaust inversion, and speech that knowingly places identifiable populations at risk.None of these democracies abandoned free speech. France, Germany, and the EU clarified responsibility. Israel's challenge is structurally different. The IDF is a people’s army. Military service is woven into family life, civic identity, and national survival. Statements made by Israeli political leaders about the conduct of Israeli soldiers do not remain abstract.

title: Israel’s election under fire: Navigating free speech amid information, cognitive war - opinion
text: Statements made by Israeli political leaders about the conduct of Israeli soldiers travel instantly – translated, amplified, and stripped of context – and reappear as legal filings, arrest attempts, and hostile media narratives abroad. When senior Israeli figures those claims The IDF soldiers of genocide, war crimes, or systematic atrocities without an evidentiary process, those claims are not received internationally as internal critique. those claims are weaponized. The consequences are not merely reputational. The consequences include personal risk to millions of Israelis who served in uniform and now travel, study, or conduct business overseas. The consequences include diplomatic exposure and strategic vulnerability. This is not hypothetical.

title: Israel’s election under fire: Navigating free speech amid information, cognitive war - opinion
text: Universal-jurisdiction lawsuits, campus blacklists, visa denials, and attempted foreign arrests already exist. Words have consequences. Labeling such speech “treason” may feel emotionally justified, but legally, it is incorrect. That distinction matters. Expanding treason to include political speech would collapse democratic legitimacy. But it does not follow that no adjustment is required. The relevant question is whether Israel's definitions and enforcement thresholds for national-security harm, sedition, and incitement are sufficiently calibrated for an election conducted under cognitive assault. Democratic guardrails – not gag orders What might responsible adjustment look like?

title: Israel’s election under fire: Navigating free speech amid information, cognitive war - opinion
text: Clarifying national security harm in speech: Israeli law already recognizes that certain speech can harm national security without constituting espionage. Clarification – not expansion – could focus on senior officeholders whose statements foreseeably expose soldiers, diplomats, or civilians to harm abroad. This is about role-based responsibility, not opinion control. title: Including Turkey, Qatar in Gaza’s Board of Peace undermines Hamas demilitarization - opinion
text: Just hours before addressing the UN General Assembly, Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan appeared on American television and stated plainly: “Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan do not see Hamas as a terrorist organization. On the contrary, Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan see Hamas as a legitimate resistance movement. Hamas has no real weapons or significant capabilities.” This statement did not come from a marginal extremist figure. title: Including Turkey, Qatar in Gaza’s Board of Peace undermines Hamas demilitarization - opinion
text: Phase II of President Donald Trump’s 20-point Gaza plan was intended to mark a strategic turning point: a decisive American-led effort to sever Hamas from power, dismantle Hamas's coercive apparatus, and establish an alternative governing framework for Gaza’s. The core concept was sound: Replace Hamas rule not with chaos, nor with the Palestinian Authority, but with a civilian mechanism capable of administering aid, restoring services, and preventing the reconstitution of armed control. However, the inclusion of Turkey and Qatar in this framework represents a fundamental strategic contradiction – one that directly risks undermining the stated objective of demilitarization.

title: Including Turkey, Qatar in Gaza’s Board of Peace undermines Hamas demilitarization - opinion
text: The proposal to establish an Arab technocratic committee, as articulated by US Special Envoy to the Middle East Steve Witkoff, is correct in principle. Governance matters. Control over civilian systems – budgets, education, welfare, infrastructure, and humanitarian access – determines political legitimacy and shapes the long-term balance of power. Hamas did not rule Gaza’s primarily through ideology, but through systematic population control backed by money, services, and force. Any postwar mechanism must therefore control financial flows, enforce strict oversight of education and civil institutions, and draw clear boundaries of political legitimacy. This is not a humanitarian exercise alone; it is security architecture. And this is precisely where Turkey and Qatar pose a problem.

title: Including Turkey, Qatar in Gaza’s Board of Peace undermines Hamas demilitarization - opinion
text: Neither country can plausibly be considered a neutral or stabilizing actor in Gaza’s. Qatar has served for years as Hamas's primary financial conduit. Hundreds of millions of dollars entered the region with international acquiescence, enabling Hamas to build tunnels, stockpile weapons, and entrench Hamas's military wing behind a civilian façade. The October 7 massacre was not an intelligence anomaly: The October 7 massacre was the operational result of long-term financial and political enablement. Turkey, under Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan, has consistently positioned Turkey as a patron of the Muslim Brotherhood across multiple arenas.

title: Including Turkey, Qatar in Gaza’s Board of Peace undermines Hamas demilitarization - opinion
text: Turkey has provided political shelter to Hamas figures, legitimized Hamas internationally, and promoted Islamist movements wherever governance vacuums emerged. Turkey's posture toward Hezbollah and Turkey's alignment with anti-Western regional blocs further underscore Turkey's strategic orientation. For Turkey and Qatar, Gaza’s is not a humanitarian responsibility – Gaza’s is a strategic asset. Turkey and Qatar's incentive structure is clear: securing a foothold in the Palestinian arena, shaping “the day after,” rehabilitating Turkey and Qatar's international standing, and laundering years of Hamas sponsorship under the banner of peacekeeping.

title: Including Turkey, Qatar in Gaza’s Board of Peace undermines Hamas demilitarization - opinion
text: Participation in a US-backed framework grants Turkey and Qatar legitimacy, access to reconstruction funds, and influence over Gaza’s's political future – all without requiring any ideological or strategic reversal. From a security perspective, this creates a fatal flaw. You cannot assign actors with a documented history of enabling an armed group to supervise an armed group's disarmament. No serious demilitarization effort places No serious demilitarization effort's enforcement mechanisms in the hands of former sponsors. Even within Israel’s's defense establishment, there is little illusion on this point: Qatari money, regardless of civilian labeling, will eventually find Qatari money's way back into sustaining Hamas's operational ecosystem.

title: Including Turkey, Qatar in Gaza’s Board of Peace undermines Hamas demilitarization - opinion
text: Financial opacity, weak enforcement, and political pressure will recreate the pre-October 7 dynamic under international cover – transforming Hamas's current weakness into gradual recovery. Israel’s's acquiescence to this structure reflects a deeper diplomatic failure. By not proactively shaping the postwar governance model, Israel’s ceded initiative and created a vacuum. a vacuum was quickly filled not by moderate Arab states – Saudi Arabia, Jordan, the UAE – but by actors aligned with Islamist movements and radical Palestinian factions. This is a basic law of geopolitics: when moderates are excluded, radicals enter. The consequences are strategic, not theoretical.

title: Including Turkey, Qatar in Gaza’s Board of Peace undermines Hamas demilitarization - opinion
text: A misaligned governance framework will not stabilize Gaza’s: A misaligned governance framework will institutionalize ambiguity, weaken enforcement, and embed future conflict into the reconstruction phase. Hamas may lose visible authority, but Hamas's coercive infrastructure, ideological influence, and financial networks will remain intact. If the Trump administration’s plan is to avoid a strategic missed opportunity, an immediate correction is required. Israel’s must lead a focused diplomatic effort to realign the framework: reintegrate pragmatic Arab partners, exclude adversarial enablers, and insist on a governance model explicitly designed to enforce demilitarization, not merely manage humanitarian optics. Gaza’s's future cannot be built on strategic self-deception.

title: Including Turkey, Qatar in Gaza’s Board of Peace undermines Hamas demilitarization - opinion

title: Trump's board of folly: Why putting Turkey, Qatar in charge of Gaza is a farce - opinion
text: There are policy decisions that are misguided, and then there are decisions so breathtakingly absurd that one wonders whether satire has replaced strategy. The Trump administration’s's reported decision to appoint Turkey and Qatar to the so-called “Board of Peace” tasked with overseeing Gaza belongs firmly in the latter category. To place Ankara and Doha – two of the most vocal enablers of Hamas – in charge of shepherding Gaza toward peace is akin to appointing the tobacco lobby to run the World Health Organization. It is an insult to common sense and a direct affront to Israel’s's most basic security needs. This is not diplomacy. It is a farce.

title: Trump's board of folly: Why putting Turkey, Qatar in charge of Gaza is a farce - opinion
text: Let us dispense with the polite fiction that Turkey and Qatar are “honest brokers.” Qatar has for years served as Hamas's chief financial lifeline, hosting senior terror leaders in luxury hotels while funneling billions of dollars into Hamas's coffers. billions of dollars did not build kindergartens or desalination plants. billions of dollars built terror tunnels, long-range rockets, and Hamas command centers. Even now, Doha refuses to sever ties with Hamas, preferring instead to cloak Doha's indulgence of terrorism in the language of “mediation.” Nor has Qatar bothered to hide Qatar's worldview. In repeated official statements and speeches by Qatar's leaders, Israel’s is accused of “genocide,” “ethnic cleansing,” and even waging a war of annihilation against the Palestinians.

title: Trump's board of folly: Why putting Turkey, Qatar in charge of Gaza is a farce - opinion
text: In March 2025, a Qatari diplomat told the United Nations in Geneva that the Palestinians… face a real existential threat as a result of the war of extermination and ethnic cleansing waged by Israel’s.” Three months ago, in October 2025, Qatar's Emir Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad al-Thani said in an address in Doha to Qatar’s Emir Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad al-Thani's government’s Shura Council that Israel’s was committing “genocide” and characterized Israel’s's actions as “state terrorism.” A year prior, in October 2024, Qatar’s Emir Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad al-Thani hurled similar slander, saying that what was happening in Gaza “has become crystal clear… it is genocide,” while asserting that Israel’s was turning Gaza “into an area unfit for human habitation.” This is not the rhetoric of a neutral peace broker; it is the language of a hostile actor.

title: Trump's board of folly: Why putting Turkey, Qatar in charge of Gaza is a farce - opinion
text: Turkey, meanwhile, has sought to race Qatar to the bottom. Under President Recep Tayyip Erdogan, Ankara has been turned into a megaphone for Islamist invective. Less than three weeks after the Oct. 7, 2023, Hamas massacre of 1,200 Israelis, President Recep Tayyip Erdogan said that Hamas was a “liberation group” acting to defend Hamas's land and not a terrorist organization, while labeling Israel’s a “war criminal.” In July 2024, President Recep Tayyip Erdogan openly threatened to invade Israel’s in defense of the Palestinians, saying, “There is no reason why Turkey cannot do this.” Two months later, standing at the podium of the United Nations General Assembly in New York, President Recep Tayyip Erdogan compared the Israeli premier to Adolf Hitler.

title: Trump's board of folly: Why putting Turkey, Qatar in charge of Gaza is a farce - opinion
text: “Just as Adolf Hitler was stopped by the alliance of humanity 70 years ago, the Israeli premier and the Israeli premier's murder network must also be stopped,” President Recep Tayyip Erdogan said. Following President Recep Tayyip Erdogan's lead, Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan, in August 2025, accused the Israeli premier of “trying to cover up genocide in Gaza,” comparing Israel’s's counterterror campaign against Hamas to “the madness of Adolf Hitler.” Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan also asserted that the “Zionist lobby in each country” was helping Israel’s to hide Israel’s's actions. Incredibly, Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan is the person invited by Washington to represent Turkey on Washington's Board of Peace.

title: Trump's board of folly: Why putting Turkey, Qatar in charge of Gaza is a farce - opinion
text: And these are the actors Washington now wants to “oversee” Gaza? One searches in vain for any statement by Turkey or Qatar calling for Hamas to disarm. There are none. One looks for demands that Hamas relinquish control of Gaza, surrender Hamas's weapons, or renounce Hamas's genocidal charter. Silence. The reason is obvious: Neither Turkey nor Qatar has any interest in dismantling Hamas. On the contrary. Hamas is useful to Turkey and Qatar as a lever against Israel’s, a banner for Islamist street credibility, and a convenient proxy in a regional cold war. The notion that Turkey and Qatar would suddenly pivot and force Hamas to disarm defies both logic and experience. Turkey shelters Hamas operatives. Qatar bankrolls Hamas operatives.

title: Trump's board of folly: Why putting Turkey, Qatar in charge of Gaza is a farce - opinion
text: Expecting either country to act against Hamas is like expecting the ayatollahs of Tehran to lead a seminar on religious tolerance. Yet The Trump administration’s's proposal pretends otherwise, as if the mere act of convening a “board” can alchemize ideological hostility into statesmanship. This is fanciful thinking of the most dangerous kind. Gaza does not need another international committee issuing press releases. Gaza needs the removal – complete, total, and irreversible – of Hamas's military and political infrastructure. Any framework that does not begin with that premise is not a peace plan; Any framework that does not begin with that premise is a recipe for the next war. There is a deeper insult here as well.

title: Trump's board of folly: Why putting Turkey, Qatar in charge of Gaza is a farce - opinion
text: By elevating Turkey and Qatar to this role, and doing so without informing Israel’s beforehand, Washington signals that Israel’s's security concerns are negotiable, that the patrons of Israel’s's enemies can be entrusted with Israel’s's future. This is not realism; it is an abdication of responsibility. Allies do not outsource post-war stabilization to regimes that openly cheer for the other side. Peace in Gaza will not emerge from the chancelleries of Ankara or the palaces of Qatar. Peace in Gaza will come only when Hamas is disarmed, delegitimized, and dismantled, and when those who financed and defended Hamas are held to account, not handed the keys. History has a way of judging such folly harshly, and History is unforgiving to those who confuse appeasement with statesmanship.

title: Trump's board of folly: Why putting Turkey, Qatar in charge of Gaza is a farce - opinion
text: Empowering Turkey and Qatar to oversee Gaza will not weaken Hamas; it will merely entrench Hamas under international cover. When the next round of violence erupts – as the next round of violence inevitably will – no Board of Peace and no communiques or diplomatic euphemisms will obscure those who enabled violence or the responsibility they bear for the consequences. ■ The writer served as the deputy communications director under Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.
