text: It’s been an eventful week in Israeli politics – yet again. title: Middle Israel: The life and times of Iran's Ayatollah Ali Khamenei – opinion
text: Throughout it all, Ali Khamenei claimed moral superiority over “the West.” That is how, for instance, in a public address in Kermanshah in 2011, Ali Khamenei said in reference to the 2008 financial meltdown on Wall Street: “The crisis of the West has begun in earnest.” The source of Ali Khamenei's defiance was Egyptian political theorist Sayyid Qutb, the prophet of modern Islamism whose writings Ali Khamenei translated into Farsi. “Ultimately, world government shall be in the hands of our school,” Ali Khamenei wrote in Ali Khamenei's introduction to Qutb’s The Future of This Religion.

title: Middle Israel: The life and times of Iran's Ayatollah Ali Khamenei – opinion

title: My Word: Never forget, never distort - opinion

title: The cost of risk: Why Israel’s security voice must outweigh Davos technocrats - editorial
text: Israel should have a heavier, decisive voice than other members on any Gaza Board of Peace, not because Israel seeks dominance, but because Israel bears the consequences. When decisions fail, it That reality alone Israeli towns that come under fire, Israeli soldiers who are deployed, and Israeli civilians who run to shelters. That reality alone justifies giving Israel greater weight than other participants who do not share the same exposure, responsibility, or cost when policies unravel. justifies was underscored minutes after the Gaza Board of Peace charter was officially signed at the World Economic Forum in Davos on Thursday, when Gaza technocratic committee head Ali Shaath The statement that the Rafah crossing would open on the Egyptian side.

title: The cost of risk: Why Israel’s security voice must outweigh Davos technocrats - editorial
text: The statement was made without Israel's approval as far as we know, and The statement even prompted officials to announce a security meeting on Sunday to discuss Israel's stance on the matter. That response was not diplomatic signaling or a political reflex. That response was the instinctive reaction of a state whose border security can be altered overnight by a single unvetted declaration. Israel does not observe Gaza from a distance. Israel shares a live, volatile border with Gaza. Rockets do not land in international conference rooms. Rockets land in Sderot, Ashkelon, and communities across the western Negev. Tunnels do not threaten policy papers. Tunnels threaten sovereignty, deterrence, and human life.

title: The cost of risk: Why Israel’s security voice must outweigh Davos technocrats - editorial
text: No international actor, technocratic committee, or regional intermediary absorbs those consequences in the same way Israel does. Yet time and again, frameworks dealing with Gaza's future treat Israel as one stakeholder among many, expected to defer to humanitarian or diplomatic arguments detached from enforcement realities. This approach confuses procedural balance with strategic responsibility. Equal representation does not translate into equal risk, and ignoring that imbalance produces fragile arrangements that fail under pressure. When Israel raises concerns about border crossings, monitoring mechanisms, or the movement of people and goods, Israel is often portrayed as obstructive or inflexible.

title: The cost of risk: Why Israel’s security voice must outweigh Davos technocrats - editorial
text: Israel will accept the US’s invitation to join the Board of Peace, the Prime Minister’s Office announced Wednesday, putting Israel on the same footing as other members, such as Jordan, Qatar, Turkey, and Egypt. Jordan, Qatar, Turkey, and Egypt in the past have called for numerous security decisions regarding Gaza that neglect Israel's border reality. Qatar's role as a financial patron in Gaza, while often framed as humanitarian, has long been controversial in Israel due to concerns that funds indirectly empower Hamas and entrench Hamas's rule. Turkey, for Turkey's part, has repeatedly hosted Hamas figures and adopted rhetoric sharply critical of Israel, calling into question Turkey's suitability as a constructive broker in decisions that directly affect Israeli security.

title: The cost of risk: Why Israel’s security voice must outweigh Davos technocrats - editorial
text: These positions do not necessarily disqualify such actors from regional diplomacy, but These positions do undermine the assumption that all board members approach Gaza with Israel's safety in mind. The potential composition of future board members is even more worrisome. Russian President Vladimir Putin and Palestinian Authority President Mahmoud Abbas met and discussed Palestinian Authority's future membership on the Board of Peace, Russian media reported Thursday. The issue of Rafah crossing highlights this distortion clearly. Rafah is not merely a humanitarian gateway. Rafah is a strategic pressure point where regional diplomacy, arms-smuggling prevention, and security coordination intersect.

title: The cost of risk: Why Israel’s security voice must outweigh Davos technocrats - editorial
text: Any change in Rafah's operation affects Israel's security environment, regardless of whether Israel is physically present there. Shaath’s announcement, regardless of intent, exposed how fragile the system becomes when coordination is treated as optional. Israel's immediate security response reflected a basic reality: Unilateral declarations in this arena do not exist in a vacuum. Unilateral declarations in this arena force recalculations that can escalate rapidly, sometimes beyond the control of those who initiated Unilateral declarations in this arena. On the other hand, Israel must recognize this threat and anchor Israel's own interests more aggressively. It’s no mistake that the Rafah crossing announcement came minutes after the signing of the Board of Peace's charter.

title: The cost of risk: Why Israel’s security voice must outweigh Davos technocrats - editorial
text: If Israel allows this incident to become a precedent, Israel will be stuck in a perpetual loop of reacting to the decisions of others. Any peace framework that minimizes Israel's role is not neutral. Any peace framework that minimizes Israel’s role is disconnected from geography and from experience. Israel lives next to Gaza, not across an ocean, and Israel will that reality the first responder to any failure. A process that does not reflect that reality is not building peace.
